curriculum.subtopics_raw: Xylem and phloem structure and function | Water uptake, route through plant | Transpiration: definition, factors, significance
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Transport Systems in Plants
Large plants need transport systems because diffusion alone is too slow to supply all cells with water and minerals or to move photosynthesis products to where they are needed.
Plants have TWO transport tissues:
1. XYLEM: transports water and dissolved mineral ions UPWARD from roots to leaves (one-directional)
2. PHLOEM: transports dissolved sugars (mainly sucrose) and other organic compounds in BOTH DIRECTIONS — up and down (from leaves to all other parts)
Xylem Structure and Function
XYLEM VESSELS:
Made of dead, hollow cells that have lost their cytoplasm
Cell walls are thickened with LIGNIN — a rigid, waterproof material that:
1. Strengthens the vessel walls (prevents collapse under the tension of water columns)
2. Makes the walls waterproof (water must move through pits/pores)
Form continuous hollow tubes from roots to leaves — like water pipes
One-way transport: only upward (from root → stem → leaf)
How water moves through xylem:
A continuous column of water molecules is pulled upward
Water molecules are cohesive (stick together by hydrogen bonds)
Evaporation of water from leaves creates a TENSION (pulling force) that draws the water column upward — this is the TRANSPIRATION PULL
This creates a continuous flow: soil water → root hair cells → cortex → xylem → leaves
PHLOEM:
Made of LIVING cells
SIEVE TUBE ELEMENTS: elongated cells with perforated end walls (sieve plates) allowing flow of sap
COMPANION CELLS: packed with mitochondria; provide energy to load sugars into phloem and maintain sieve tubes
Transports SUCROSE (the main transported sugar) in a concentrated solution called PHLOEM SAP
Transport is called TRANSLOCATION
Water Uptake and Route Through the Plant
PATH OF WATER FROM SOIL TO LEAF:
1. SOIL WATER → ROOT HAIR CELLS:
Water in soil surrounds root hair cells
Root hair cells have a lower water potential than soil water (because of dissolved solutes in cell sap)
Water enters root hair cells by OSMOSIS
2. ROOT HAIR CELLS → CORTEX → ENDODERMIS → XYLEM:
Water passes from cell to cell across the root cortex by osmosis (each cell has lower water potential than the previous one)
Water enters xylem vessels in the centre of the root
3. XYLEM (ROOT → STEM → LEAF):
Transpiration pull (created by evaporation from leaves) draws water up through xylem
The cohesion of water molecules (sticking together) means the entire water column is pulled up as one unit
This is the COHESION-TENSION MECHANISM
4. LEAF CELLS → STOMATA → ATMOSPHERE:
Water evaporates from cell walls in spongy mesophyll into air spaces
Water vapour diffuses out through stomata to the outside air
Transpiration
DEFINITION: Transpiration is the loss of water vapour from the surfaces of a plant, mainly through the stomata of leaves.
Why does transpiration occur?
Cells inside leaves are surrounded by moist air spaces
Water evaporates from cell walls into these air spaces
Water vapour concentration inside the leaf > outside the leaf
Water vapour diffuses OUT through stomata down this concentration gradient
Significance of transpiration:
BENEFICIAL: Creates the transpiration pull that draws water and minerals UP from roots (the main mechanism for water transport in tall plants)
COOLING: Evaporation of water removes heat from the leaf
DISADVANTAGE: Plants lose water — if too much is lost and not replaced, the plant wilts
FACTORS AFFECTING THE RATE OF TRANSPIRATION:
1. TEMPERATURE: Higher temperature → more evaporation from cell walls → steeper concentration gradient for water vapour → faster transpiration rate
2. HUMIDITY: High humidity (lots of water vapour in air) → smaller concentration gradient between inside and outside leaf → SLOWER transpiration. Low humidity → steeper gradient → FASTER transpiration.
3. WIND SPEED: Moving air removes water vapour from around the leaf → maintains steep concentration gradient → FASTER transpiration. Still air accumulates water vapour near leaf surface → slows transpiration.
4. LIGHT INTENSITY: More light → stomata open wider → more water vapour escapes → FASTER transpiration. Darkness → stomata close → transpiration nearly stops.
5. SURFACE AREA OF LEAVES: More leaf area → more stomata → more transpiration.
MEASURING TRANSPIRATION — using a POTOMETER:
A potometer measures the RATE OF WATER UPTAKE by a plant cutting (not directly transpiration, but closely related)
As the plant transpires, water is drawn along a capillary tube; a bubble of air moves along the tube
Measure the distance the bubble moves per minute → calculate rate
Translocation
TRANSLOCATION: the movement of dissolved sugars (and other organic solutes) through phloem from SOURCES (where they are made or stored) to SINKS (where they are needed).
Source: leaves (where photosynthesis produces sucrose)
Sinks: growing tips, roots, fruits, seeds, storage organs
Translocation is an ACTIVE process (requires energy from companion cells):
1. Sucrose is actively loaded into sieve tubes at the source
2. This increases solute concentration → water enters sieve tubes by osmosis → pressure builds up at the source end
3. Sucrose is removed (unloaded) at the sink → pressure falls at the sink end
4. Pressure difference drives the flow from source to sink
This is called MASS FLOW or PRESSURE-FLOW HYPOTHESIS
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw the cross-section of a plant stem showing: xylem vessels (dead, lignified), phloem sieve tubes and companion cells, relative positions
2. Draw a diagram showing the route of water from soil to atmosphere: soil → root hair cell → cortex → xylem → leaf cell → air space → stoma → atmosphere
curriculum.activity: Transpiration Investigation using a Potometer: Set up a plant shoot in a potometer. Measure the rate of water uptake in: (1) still air, (2) with a fan blowing, (3) with a light shining. Record results and explain differences in terms of concentration gradients for water vapour.
curriculum.homework:
1. State TWO structural differences between xylem and phloem.
2. Describe the route taken by a water molecule from the soil to the atmosphere through a plant.
3. Explain why transpiration rate is higher on a hot, dry, windy day than on a cool, humid, still day.
4. A student places a potted plant in full sun with a plastic bag around its leaves. After an hour, water droplets form inside the bag. Explain this observation.
5. Distinguish between transpiration and translocation.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.topic: Transport in Animals — The Circulatory System
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Double circulatory system | Heart structure and function | Blood vessels: arteries, veins, capillaries
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Why Animals Need a Circulatory System
Small organisms (single cells) can rely on diffusion alone for transport. But in large, complex animals, diffusion is far too slow to supply all cells at distance from absorbing surfaces.
The CIRCULATORY SYSTEM in mammals consists of:
The HEART: a muscular pump
BLOOD VESSELS: tubes carrying blood
BLOOD: the transport medium
Functions of the circulatory system:
Transport OXYGEN from lungs to all cells
Transport CARBON DIOXIDE from cells to lungs
Transport GLUCOSE and other nutrients from gut to all cells
Transport UREA (waste) from liver to kidneys
Transport HORMONES from glands to target organs
Distribute HEAT around the body
Carry ANTIBODIES and WHITE BLOOD CELLS to fight infection
Double Circulation
Mammals (including humans) have a DOUBLE CIRCULATORY SYSTEM — the blood passes through the HEART TWICE per complete circuit of the body:
Circuit 1 — PULMONARY CIRCULATION:
Right side of heart → LUNGS → Left side of heart
Blood picks up oxygen and loses CO₂ in the lungs
Blood returns to the heart rich in oxygen (oxygenated)
Circuit 2 — SYSTEMIC CIRCULATION:
Left side of heart → BODY → Right side of heart
Oxygenated blood is pumped to all organs
Blood delivers O₂ and nutrients, picks up CO₂ and waste
Returns to heart as deoxygenated blood
Advantages of double circulation:
Blood pressure is maintained high enough to supply all organs efficiently
Oxygenated and deoxygenated blood are COMPLETELY SEPARATED — no mixing
Left ventricle pumps at higher pressure than right ventricle (body circuit is longer)
Single circulation (e.g. fish): blood passes through heart once per circuit (heart → gills → body → heart). Pressure is lower after passing through gill capillaries.
The Heart — Structure
The heart is a muscular pump about the size of a clenched fist, located slightly left of centre in the chest between the lungs.
STRUCTURE:
Four chambers: two ATRIA (upper, thin-walled, receive blood) and two VENTRICLES (lower, thick-walled, pump blood)
LEFT SIDE: handles oxygenated blood from lungs → pumps to body
RIGHT SIDE: handles deoxygenated blood from body → pumps to lungs
VALVES (prevent backflow of blood):
ATRIOVENTRICULAR (AV) VALVES between atria and ventricles:
BICUSPID (MITRAL) VALVE: between left atrium and left ventricle
TRICUSPID VALVE: between right atrium and right ventricle
These open when ventricles fill; close when ventricles contract
SEMILUNAR VALVES: at the exits of ventricles (pulmonary and aortic valves)
Open when ventricles contract (blood pushed out)
Close when ventricles relax (prevent blood flowing back into heart)
MAJOR BLOOD VESSELS:
AORTA: leaves left ventricle; carries oxygenated blood to body
PULMONARY ARTERY: leaves right ventricle; carries deoxygenated blood to lungs
VENA CAVA (superior and inferior): carries deoxygenated blood from body → right atrium
PULMONARY VEINS (4): carry oxygenated blood from lungs → left atrium
CORONARY ARTERIES: supply the heart muscle itself with blood
Note: The pulmonary artery carries DEOXYGENATED blood (unusual for an artery). The pulmonary veins carry OXYGENATED blood (unusual for veins).
The Cardiac Cycle
The heart beats rhythmically and automatically — about 70 times per minute at rest.
CARDIAC CYCLE (one heartbeat):
STEP 1 — DIASTOLE (Relaxation):
Both atria and ventricles RELAX
Blood flows from vena cava/pulmonary veins into relaxed atria
AV valves open; blood flows into ventricles
STEP 2 — ATRIAL SYSTOLE (Atria contract):
Atria CONTRACT — completing filling of ventricles
AV valves remain open
STEP 3 — VENTRICULAR SYSTOLE (Ventricles contract):
Ventricles CONTRACT — pump blood out under pressure
Left ventricle → aorta → body
Right ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs
AV valves CLOSE (preventing backflow into atria) — produces the "LUBB" heart sound
Semilunar valves OPEN (blood pushed out)
STEP 4 — Ventricles relax:
Semilunar valves CLOSE (prevent backflow into ventricles) — produces the "DUP" heart sound
Cycle repeats
PACEMAKER: The sinoatrial (SA) node in the right atrium generates electrical impulses that coordinate the heartbeat. It sets the rate (hence "natural pacemaker"). The atrioventricular (AV) node then relays impulses to the ventricles. Artificial pacemakers replace the SA node when it malfunctions.
HEART RATE and FACTORS:
Exercise: heart rate increases to deliver more O₂ to muscles
Adrenaline: increases heart rate
Temperature: higher body temperature increases heart rate
Fitness: trained athletes have lower resting heart rate (heart is stronger)
Blood Vessels
Three types of blood vessels:
1. ARTERIES (carry blood AWAY from heart):
Thick, muscular, elastic walls
Narrow central channel (lumen)
No valves (blood flows under HIGH PRESSURE)
Elastic walls stretch with each heartbeat then recoil — maintaining continuous blood pressure between beats
Carry oxygenated blood (except pulmonary artery)
2. VEINS (carry blood TOWARD heart):
Thinner walls with less muscle/elastic tissue
Wide lumen
Have VALVES (pocket valves) to prevent backflow — essential because blood is at LOW PRESSURE
Blood moves due to: skeletal muscle squeezing veins during body movement; breathing movements
Carry deoxygenated blood (except pulmonary veins)
3. CAPILLARIES (link arteries to veins within tissues):
SMALLEST blood vessels — diameter just wide enough for one red blood cell
Wall only ONE CELL THICK (no muscle or elastic tissue)
Very large total surface area throughout body tissues
Allow exchange of substances:
O₂ and glucose diffuse from blood → tissue cells
CO₂ diffuses from tissue cells → blood
Plasma leaks out of capillary walls → forms TISSUE FLUID which bathes cells
Capillary bed: the network of capillaries in every organ and tissue
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a fully labelled diagram of the heart showing: right atrium, left atrium, right ventricle, left ventricle, aorta, pulmonary artery, pulmonary veins, vena cava, bicuspid valve, tricuspid valve, semilunar valves
2. Draw cross-sections of an artery, vein, and capillary side by side, labelling walls and features. Show relative sizes.
curriculum.activity: Heart Rate Investigation: Measure resting heart rate for 1 minute (feel pulse at wrist or neck). Then exercise (step up and down on a step) for 3 minutes. Measure heart rate immediately after, then at 1-minute intervals until it returns to resting rate. Plot heart rate vs time. Calculate recovery rate.
curriculum.homework:
1. Explain the difference between single and double circulation.
2. Trace the path of a red blood cell from the right atrium of the heart to the left arm and back to the heart.
3. Explain the function of valves in the heart and in veins.
4. Compare arteries and veins under THREE headings: wall thickness, lumen size, presence of valves.
5. A patient's left ventricle wall is thicker than the right ventricle wall. Explain why this is normal and expected.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Composition of blood | Functions of red blood cells, white blood cells, platelets, plasma | Tissue fluid and lymph
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Composition of Blood
Blood is a connective tissue consisting of:
PLASMA (about 55% of blood volume): pale yellow liquid
RED BLOOD CELLS (about 44% of blood volume): erythrocytes
WHITE BLOOD CELLS (less than 1%): leucocytes
PLATELETS (less than 1%): cell fragments for clotting
Blood is pumped through the body by the heart; total volume in an adult = about 5 litres.
Plasma
PLASMA is a pale yellow aqueous solution that transports:
DISSOLVED NUTRIENTS: glucose, amino acids, fatty acids, vitamins
HORMONES: insulin, glucagon, adrenaline (transported from glands to target organs)
CARBON DIOXIDE: dissolved as carbonic acid (about 5%) and bicarbonate ions (about 85%) — transported from tissues to lungs
UREA: waste product from protein breakdown; transported from liver to kidneys for excretion
ANTIBODIES: proteins produced by lymphocytes to fight pathogens
FIBRINOGEN: soluble protein involved in blood clotting
MINERAL SALTS, WATER
HEAT: distributes heat from metabolically active organs (liver, muscles) to cool parts of body
Red Blood Cells (Erythrocytes)
STRUCTURE:
Biconcave disc shape (flattened disc with dents on both surfaces)
Very small: diameter about 7–8 micrometres
NO NUCLEUS when mature (lost during development)
Packed with HAEMOGLOBIN (a red pigment protein)
FUNCTION: Transport OXYGEN from lungs to all body cells
How oxygen is transported:
In the lungs (high O₂): Haemoglobin + Oxygen → OXYHAEMOGLOBIN (bright red)
In tissues (low O₂): Oxyhaemoglobin → Haemoglobin + Oxygen (released to cells)
Adaptations of red blood cells:
Biconcave shape: maximises surface area (600 µm² compared to 380 µm² for a sphere of same volume) for rapid diffusion of O₂
No nucleus: more space for haemoglobin
Small and flexible: can squeeze through narrowest capillaries
Many present: ~5 million per mm³ of blood
Short life (~120 days); constantly replaced in bone marrow
CO₂ transport:
Most CO₂ (~85%) is transported as BICARBONATE IONS (HCO₃⁻) in plasma
Some (~10%) binds to haemoglobin as carbaminohaemoglobin
Small amount dissolved in plasma (~5%)
White Blood Cells (Leucocytes)
White blood cells are part of the IMMUNE SYSTEM. They are larger than red blood cells, have nuclei, and are far fewer in number (about 7,000 per mm³). Two main types:
1. PHAGOCYTES (Neutrophils and Monocytes):
Function: PHAGOCYTOSIS — engulf and destroy pathogens (bacteria, dead cells, debris)
Process: phagocyte detects bacteria → extends cytoplasm around bacterium → engulfs it into a vacuole → lysosomes release digestive enzymes → bacterium is digested
Can squeeze through capillary walls to reach infection sites
First line of cellular defence
2. LYMPHOCYTES:
Function: PRODUCE ANTIBODIES in response to specific pathogens
Each lymphocyte produces ONE type of antibody specific to ONE antigen
ANTIBODIES: Y-shaped protein molecules that:
Bind to ANTIGENS on pathogen surfaces
Agglutinate bacteria (clump them together)
Neutralise toxins
Flag pathogens for destruction by phagocytes
Lymphocytes also produce MEMORY CELLS for long-term immunity
B lymphocytes: produce antibodies
T lymphocytes: destroy infected cells and coordinate immune response
Platelets and Blood Clotting
PLATELETS are tiny cell fragments (not complete cells) produced in bone marrow. They play a vital role in blood clotting (haemostasis).
BLOOD CLOTTING PROCESS:
1. Skin is cut; blood vessels damaged → platelets aggregate (clump) at wound site
2. Platelets + damaged tissues release THROMBOPLASTIN (clotting factor)
3. Thromboplastin + calcium ions + clotting factors → convert PROTHROMBIN (inactive) to THROMBIN (enzyme)
4. Thrombin catalyses: FIBRINOGEN (soluble protein) → FIBRIN (insoluble protein threads)
5. FIBRIN forms a meshwork of fibres that traps red blood cells → clot forms
6. Clot dries → SCAB forms, sealing wound
Importance of clotting:
Prevents further blood loss
Prevents entry of pathogens into wound
Creates scaffold for healing
Antipathogen agents: Some pathogens produce chemicals that prevent clotting. Some medical treatments (anticoagulants like warfarin, heparin) prevent clotting in blood vessels.
Tissue Fluid and Lymph
As blood flows through capillaries:
Hydrostatic pressure FORCES plasma out through capillary walls into spaces between cells
This fluid (without blood cells or large proteins) is called TISSUE FLUID
Tissue fluid BATHES all cells: delivers O₂, glucose, amino acids; receives CO₂ and waste products
Most tissue fluid re-enters capillaries at the venous end (osmotic pressure draws it back).
Some tissue fluid enters LYMPH CAPILLARIES → becomes LYMPH.
LYMPH:
Similar composition to tissue fluid (watery, with dissolved nutrients)
Flows through LYMPHATIC VESSELS (one-way, with valves) → eventually drains into blood
Passes through LYMPH NODES: contain white blood cells (lymphocytes) that filter bacteria and debris
Carries absorbed fats from gut (as chylomicrons) into blood via the thoracic duct
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a red blood cell in side view (biconcave disc) and from above, labelling the biconcave shape, haemoglobin content, no nucleus
2. Draw the blood clotting cascade as a flow diagram: injury → platelets → thromboplastin → prothrombin → thrombin → fibrinogen → fibrin → clot
curriculum.activity: Blood Cell Observation: Using prepared slides of blood, observe under microscope. Draw what you see. Identify: the numerous small biconcave red blood cells (no nucleus), the larger white blood cells (visible nucleus), and platelets. Count in a field of view the ratio of red to white blood cells.
curriculum.homework:
1. List FIVE substances transported in blood plasma.
2. Explain THREE ways in which the structure of a red blood cell is adapted for oxygen transport.
3. Distinguish between phagocytes and lymphocytes in terms of structure and function.
4. Describe the sequence of events in blood clotting after a skin wound.
5. What is the difference between plasma and tissue fluid? How does tissue fluid form?

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Pathogens and transmission | Defences against disease: physical, chemical, cellular | Vaccines and immunity | Antibiotics
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Pathogens and Disease
A PATHOGEN is a microorganism that causes disease. Types of pathogens:
1. BACTERIA: e.g. Mycobacterium tuberculosis (TB), Vibrio cholerae (cholera), Salmonella (food poisoning). Bacteria can directly damage cells or produce TOXINS (poisons) that cause disease symptoms.
2. VIRUSES: e.g. Influenza virus (flu), HIV (AIDS), SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19). Viruses invade cells, hijack cell machinery to replicate, then burst out, destroying the cell.
3. FUNGI: e.g. Tinea (ringworm, athlete's foot), Candida (thrush). Parasitic fungi on skin/mucous membranes.
4. PROTOCTISTS: e.g. Plasmodium (malaria), Entamoeba (amoebic dysentery).
HOW DISEASES SPREAD (Transmission):
DROPLET INFECTION: tiny droplets in coughs/sneezes containing pathogens → breathed in. Examples: influenza, TB, COVID-19
CONTAMINATED WATER/FOOD: cholera, typhoid, food poisoning (Salmonella)
DIRECT CONTACT: STIs (HIV, gonorrhoea), ringworm, athlete's foot
VECTORS (animals that carry pathogens to humans): mosquitoes carry Plasmodium (malaria); flies carry bacteria on food
CONTAMINATED BLOOD: HIV, hepatitis B (sharing needles, blood transfusions)
VERTICAL TRANSMISSION (mother to child): HIV during birth/breastfeeding; rubella virus through placenta
Body Defences Against Disease
The body has THREE lines of defence:
FIRST LINE — PHYSICAL BARRIERS (prevent pathogen entry):
SKIN: tough, intact skin is an effective physical barrier; produces slightly acidic secretions that inhibit bacterial growth
MUCUS and CILIA in airways: mucus traps pathogens; ciliated cells beat mucus (and trapped pathogens) upward to the throat where it is swallowed
TEARS: contain lysozyme — an enzyme that breaks down bacterial cell walls
SALIVA: also contains lysozyme
STOMACH ACID (HCl): pH 2 kills most pathogens that enter with food/drink
CERUMEN (earwax): traps bacteria and debris in the ear canal
URINE FLOW: flushes bacteria from the urethra
SECOND LINE — INFLAMMATORY RESPONSE (general non-specific):
Damaged tissue releases chemicals → increase blood flow → affected area becomes red, hot, swollen (inflammation)
Phagocytes arrive and engulf (phagocytose) pathogens
Fever: raised body temperature slows pathogen reproduction; speeds up immune responses
THIRD LINE — SPECIFIC IMMUNE RESPONSE:
Lymphocytes (B and T cells) recognise specific pathogens
B lymphocytes produce ANTIBODIES against specific ANTIGENS on pathogen surfaces
T lymphocytes destroy infected cells
MEMORY CELLS formed for rapid response to future infection
Antigens and Antibodies
ANTIGEN: Any molecule (usually on the surface of a pathogen, or on an abnormal cell) that triggers an immune response. Each pathogen has unique antigens.
ANTIBODY: A Y-shaped protein produced by B lymphocytes, specific to one antigen. The shape of the antibody's binding site is COMPLEMENTARY to the antigen (like enzyme-substrate).
How antibodies destroy pathogens:
1. AGGLUTINATION: antibodies clump bacteria together → easier for phagocytes to engulf
2. NEUTRALISATION: antibodies bind to toxins → render them harmless
3. OPSONISATION: antibodies coat bacteria → mark them for destruction by phagocytes
4. LYSIS: some antibody-antigen complexes activate proteins that puncture bacterial membranes
IMMUNOLOGICAL MEMORY:
After first exposure to a pathogen, MEMORY CELLS are produced
These persist in the body for years or a lifetime
Second exposure: memory cells rapidly produce LARGE QUANTITIES of antibodies much faster → the pathogen is destroyed before symptoms appear
This is the basis of IMMUNITY
Vaccination
VACCINATION (IMMUNISATION): Introducing a VACCINE to stimulate the immune system to produce antibodies and memory cells against a pathogen WITHOUT causing the actual disease.
Vaccine types:
Dead pathogens (e.g. killed influenza bacteria)
Weakened (attenuated) live pathogen (e.g. MMR vaccine — measles, mumps, rubella)
Toxoids (inactivated toxin) (e.g. tetanus)
Subunit vaccines (just the antigen, not the whole pathogen) (e.g. Hepatitis B)
mRNA vaccines (instruct cells to produce the antigen) (e.g. COVID-19 mRNA vaccines)
HOW VACCINATION WORKS:
1. Vaccine administered (injection, oral, nasal)
2. Immune system recognises antigens in vaccine
3. B lymphocytes produce antibodies
4. Memory cells formed
5. If actual pathogen later encountered → rapid, massive antibody production → disease prevented
HERD IMMUNITY: If enough people in a community are vaccinated, even unvaccinated individuals are protected because the pathogen cannot spread. Typically need 80–95% vaccination coverage, depending on the pathogen.
Examples of vaccine-preventable diseases: Polio, measles, tetanus, cholera, TB (BCG vaccine), meningitis, COVID-19.
Antibiotics
ANTIBIOTICS are chemicals (originally from bacteria/fungi or now synthetic) that kill or inhibit the growth of BACTERIA.
Mode of action examples:
Penicillin: prevents bacteria from building their cell walls → bacteria burst
Streptomycin: inhibits bacterial ribosomes → stops protein synthesis
IMPORTANT LIMITATIONS:
Antibiotics are EFFECTIVE AGAINST BACTERIA only
They do NOT work against VIRUSES (viruses use host cell machinery, which would also be damaged)
This is why antibiotics cannot treat influenza, COVID-19, or the common cold
ANTIBIOTIC RESISTANCE — a serious global health crisis:
Some bacteria have evolved (natural selection) to resist antibiotic effects
MRSA (Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus): resistant to most antibiotics
Causes:
Overuse/misuse of antibiotics (e.g. not completing courses)
Antibiotics given for viral infections (inappropriate use)
Antibiotics used in livestock farming
Prevention: only use antibiotics when necessary; complete full course; develop new antibiotics
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a flow diagram showing what happens when a bacterium enters the body: physical barriers → phagocytosis → lymphocyte response → antibody production → memory cells
2. Draw a graph showing antibody levels over time: first exposure (slow rise, lower peak), then second exposure/vaccination (rapid, higher peak)
curriculum.activity: Antiseptics and Antibiotics Testing (if available): Place discs soaked in different antibiotics/antiseptics on agar plates inoculated with bacteria (Micrococcus luteus — safe). After incubation, measure the clear zones (zones of inhibition) around each disc. Compare effectiveness. Note safety precautions.
curriculum.homework:
1. Distinguish between a pathogen and a vector. Give an example of each.
2. Describe THREE non-specific defences that prevent pathogens entering the body.
3. Explain why a person who has had chickenpox (caused by varicella-zoster virus) is unlikely to get it again.
4. Explain the difference between how antibiotics fight bacteria and why they are ineffective against viruses.
5. Explain what is meant by antibiotic resistance and describe how it develops through natural selection.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Structure of the human gas exchange system | Mechanism of breathing | Gas exchange in the alveoli | Effects of smoking
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
The Need for Gas Exchange
All living cells need OXYGEN for aerobic respiration and produce CARBON DIOXIDE as waste. Gas exchange is the process by which O₂ is absorbed into the blood and CO₂ is removed.
In humans, gas exchange occurs in the LUNGS, specifically in tiny air sacs called ALVEOLI.
Structure of the Human Gas Exchange System
NASAL CAVITY / NOSE:
Warms and moistens inhaled air
Hairs and mucus filter dust and particles
TRACHEA (Windpipe):
Tube from throat to bronchi
Walls reinforced by C-shaped cartilage rings — prevent collapse when inhaling
Lined with mucus and ciliated epithelium — trap and remove particles
BRONCHI (singular: bronchus):
Trachea divides into TWO bronchi (one to each lung)
Also reinforced with cartilage
Lined with mucus and cilia
BRONCHIOLES:
Bronchi divide into smaller and smaller bronchioles
NO cartilage (walls are smooth muscle and elastic tissue)
Eventually lead to alveoli
ALVEOLI (singular: alveolus):
Tiny air sacs at the end of bronchioles
About 300–700 million alveoli in each lung
Total surface area approximately 70–100 m²
Each alveolus is surrounded by a dense network of pulmonary CAPILLARIES
Adaptations of alveoli for gas exchange:
LARGE SURFACE AREA: millions of alveoli → massive total area for diffusion
THIN WALLS: alveolar wall + capillary wall = only TWO cells thick (~0.2 µm) → very short diffusion distance
MOIST LINING: gases dissolve in the moist lining before diffusing
RICH BLOOD SUPPLY: capillaries constantly deliver deoxygenated blood and remove oxygenated blood → maintains steep concentration gradient
VENTILATION: breathing constantly renews air in alveoli → maintains high O₂ and low CO₂ concentration (steep gradients)
Mechanism of Breathing (Ventilation)
Breathing (ventilation) moves air IN and OUT of the lungs using pressure changes created by muscular action.
INSPIRATION (breathing IN):
1. DIAPHRAGM CONTRACTS → domes downward and flattens
2. INTERCOSTAL MUSCLES CONTRACT → ribs move UP and OUT
3. Volume of thorax INCREASES
4. Pressure inside lungs DECREASES (below atmospheric pressure)
5. Air flows INTO lungs down pressure gradient
EXPIRATION (breathing OUT):
1. DIAPHRAGM RELAXES → domes upward again
2. INTERCOSTAL MUSCLES RELAX → ribs move DOWN and IN
3. Volume of thorax DECREASES
4. Pressure inside lungs INCREASES (above atmospheric pressure)
5. Air flows OUT of lungs down pressure gradient
During FORCED expiration (exercise, coughing):
Internal intercostal muscles and abdominal muscles also contract to push ribs down further
Note: Lungs have NO muscle — they are passive. All movement is due to the diaphragm and intercostal muscles.
EFFECT OF EXERCISE:
Respiring muscles produce more CO₂
Raised CO₂ in blood stimulates medulla (in brain) to increase breathing rate and depth
More O₂ delivered; more CO₂ removed
Gas Exchange at the Alveoli
Gas exchange occurs by DIFFUSION, driven by concentration gradients:
OXYGEN:
Alveolar air (high O₂): ~21% O₂
Deoxygenated blood arriving in capillaries (low O₂): ~40 mmHg partial pressure
O₂ diffuses from alveolus → blood → combines with haemoglobin → oxyhaemoglobin
CARBON DIOXIDE:
Deoxygenated blood (high CO₂): ~46 mmHg partial pressure
Alveolar air (low CO₂): ~0.04% CO₂
CO₂ diffuses from blood → alveolus → breathed out
The composition of inhaled and exhaled air:
Inhaled: O₂ ~21%, CO₂ ~0.04%, N₂ ~79%, water vapour variable
Exhaled: O₂ ~16%, CO₂ ~4%, N₂ ~79%, water vapour ~saturated
Effects of Smoking on the Gas Exchange System
Tobacco smoke contains THOUSANDS of chemicals, including:
NICOTINE: addictive substance; increases heart rate and blood pressure
CARBON MONOXIDE (CO): binds irreversibly to haemoglobin (forms carboxyhaemoglobin) — reducing oxygen-carrying capacity of blood; causes tissue hypoxia
TAR: a mixture of carcinogenic (cancer-causing) chemicals; deposits on lung tissues
PARTICULATES: tiny particles that clog the lungs
DAMAGE TO THE RESPIRATORY SYSTEM:
1. BRONCHITIS (inflammation of bronchi):
Smoke irritates bronchi → mucus secretion increases
Cilia are DESTROYED by smoke
Mucus accumulates → blocks airways → "smoker's cough"
Bacteria breed in trapped mucus → chronic infections
2. EMPHYSEMA:
Tar and smoke destroy the WALLS of alveoli
Adjacent alveoli merge → fewer, LARGER air sacs
Total surface area for gas exchange is DRASTICALLY REDUCED
Breathlessness; insufficient O₂ even at rest
Irreversible damage — no cure
3. LUNG CANCER:
Carcinogens in tar cause MUTATIONS in lung cell DNA
Uncontrolled cell division → tumour
Tumours block airways, invade tissues, metastasize
Major cause of cancer death worldwide; strongly linked to smoking
4. CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE:
Nicotine: increases heart rate and blood pressure
Carbon monoxide: reduces O₂ supply → heart works harder
Chemicals damage artery walls → fatty deposits (atherosclerosis) → heart attack, stroke
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a fully labelled diagram of the human respiratory system: nasal cavity, trachea, bronchus, bronchioles, alveoli, lungs, ribs, diaphragm, intercostal muscles
2. Draw a diagram of a single alveolus surrounded by capillaries, showing diffusion of O₂ into blood and CO₂ out of blood with arrows and concentration values
curriculum.activity: Breathing Rate Investigation: Count the number of breaths per minute at rest. Then exercise for 2 minutes. Count breathing rate immediately after, and every minute until it returns to resting rate. Record and plot on a graph. Also measure depth of breathing. Explain the changes in terms of CO₂ production and the need for O₂.
curriculum.homework:
1. List the pathway taken by air from outside the body to the alveoli.
2. Describe the mechanism of inspiration (breathing in), naming the muscles involved and the pressure changes that occur.
3. State FOUR adaptations of alveoli for efficient gas exchange. For each, explain how it helps.
4. Explain why carbon monoxide in cigarette smoke is particularly harmful to the oxygen-carrying capacity of blood.
5. Describe how smoking causes emphysema and explain why it leads to breathlessness.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Aerobic respiration | Anaerobic respiration | ATP and energy release | Comparison of aerobic and anaerobic respiration
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Respiration?
RESPIRATION is the chemical process in cells that RELEASES ENERGY from organic molecules (mainly glucose). This energy is used to drive all the energy-requiring processes of life.
IMPORTANT: Respiration is NOT breathing (ventilation). Breathing is the physical process of moving air in and out of lungs. Respiration is the CHEMICAL REACTION that releases energy inside CELLS.
The energy released is stored in a molecule called ATP (adenosine triphosphate). ATP acts as the immediate energy currency of all cells — when ATP is broken down, energy is released for:
Muscle contraction
Active transport (pumping ions across membranes)
Synthesis of large molecules (proteins, DNA, etc.)
Maintaining body temperature
Nerve impulse transmission
There are TWO types of respiration:
1. AEROBIC RESPIRATION: requires oxygen; fully oxidises glucose; releases maximum energy
2. ANAEROBIC RESPIRATION: no oxygen required; partially breaks down glucose; releases less energy
Aerobic Respiration
AEROBIC RESPIRATION uses OXYGEN to completely break down glucose.
Overall equation:
C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ENERGY
Glucose + Oxygen → Carbon dioxide + Water + Energy
Energy yield: approximately 38 molecules of ATP per molecule of glucose (very efficient)
Where it occurs:
GLYCOLYSIS: in the CYTOPLASM — glucose is broken into 2 molecules of pyruvate (produces 2 ATP; no oxygen needed)
KREBS CYCLE: in the MATRIX of MITOCHONDRIA — pyruvate is fully oxidised to CO₂; produces NADH (hydrogen carriers)
ELECTRON TRANSPORT CHAIN: on the INNER MEMBRANE of mitochondria — NADH releases its hydrogen; electrons pass down a chain of proteins; oxygen is the final electron acceptor → water forms; most ATP is produced here
This is why MITOCHONDRIA are the "powerhouses" of the cell and why aerobically active cells have MANY mitochondria.
Fuels other than glucose:
Fatty acids can also be used in aerobic respiration (enter Krebs cycle)
Amino acids (after deamination) can also be used
Fats yield more ATP per gram than carbohydrates — this is why fats are efficient energy stores
Anaerobic Respiration
ANAEROBIC RESPIRATION occurs WITHOUT OXYGEN. It produces less ATP than aerobic respiration.
IN MAMMALS (animals) during intense exercise:
When O₂ supply to muscles is insufficient for aerobic respiration
Glucose is partially broken down to LACTIC ACID (lactate)
EQUATION: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₃H₆O₃ + energy
Glucose → Lactic acid + energy (only 2 ATP per glucose)
Effect of lactic acid:
Accumulates in muscles → causes MUSCLE FATIGUE and pain
Lactic acid is eventually transported to liver
When oxygen becomes available again, lactic acid is oxidised to CO₂ and water in the liver
The extra O₂ required = "OXYGEN DEBT" (now called excess post-exercise oxygen consumption, EPOC)
This is why we continue to breathe heavily for a while AFTER exercise stops
IN YEAST (fermentation) and plants (anaerobic conditions):
Glucose → ETHANOL (alcohol) + CARBON DIOXIDE + energy
EQUATION: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ + energy
Produces only 2 ATP per glucose
IMPORTANCE OF YEAST FERMENTATION:
BREAD MAKING: CO₂ bubbles cause dough to rise; alcohol evaporates during baking
BREWING (beer, wine): ethanol produced is the alcohol in alcoholic drinks
BIOGAS: bacteria ferment organic waste → methane + CO₂; used as fuel
BIOFUEL: ethanol from fermentation of sugarcane → used as vehicle fuel (e.g. in Brazil)
Comparison: Aerobic vs Anaerobic
Feature comparison:
FEATURE | AEROBIC | ANAEROBIC
Oxygen needed | YES | NO
Glucose breakdown | COMPLETE | PARTIAL
Products in animals | CO₂ + H₂O | Lactic acid
Products in yeast | CO₂ + H₂O | Ethanol + CO₂
Energy yield (ATP) | ~38 ATP per glucose | ~2 ATP per glucose
Duration | Can continue long-term | Short-term only (fatigue)
Occurs during | Rest and moderate exercise | Intense exercise
Location | Cytoplasm (glycolysis) + Mitochondria | Cytoplasm only
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a flow diagram of aerobic respiration: Glucose → (cytoplasm: glycolysis) → Pyruvate → (mitochondria: Krebs cycle) → CO₂ + NADH → (electron transport chain) → ATP + H₂O
2. Draw a graph showing: (a) rate of aerobic respiration vs temperature (bell curve), (b) rate of anaerobic respiration vs O₂ availability (drops as O₂ increases — Pasteur effect)
curriculum.activity: Anaerobic Respiration in Yeast: Set up yeast + sugar solution in a flask with a tube leading to limewater. Observe CO₂ bubbles and limewater turning milky. Compare with: (a) yeast + water only (no sugar — no respiration), (b) boiled yeast (dead — no respiration). Plot CO₂ production rate against time.
curriculum.homework:
1. Write the word equation for aerobic respiration. State where in the cell each stage occurs.
2. Explain why an athlete continues to breathe heavily for several minutes after finishing a race.
3. Write the equation for anaerobic respiration in yeast. State TWO commercial applications of this process.
4. Compare aerobic and anaerobic respiration under FOUR headings.
5. Explain why cells performing lots of active transport (like kidney tubule cells) need many mitochondria.
EXPECTED LEARNING OUTCOMES — YEAR 1, TERM 2
Key Knowledge Students Should Gain
Explain the light-dependent and light-independent reactions of photosynthesis
Describe how light, CO₂ concentration and temperature limit photosynthesis
Explain the role of mineral ions (nitrate, phosphate, magnesium) in plants
Describe the full structure of the leaf and its adaptations
Identify dietary components and explain deficiency diseases
Trace the path of food through the human digestive system
Explain the role of bile, pancreatic juice, and intestinal enzymes
Describe how villi are adapted for absorption
Explain water uptake, transpiration, and translocation in plants
Describe the double circulatory system and heart structure
Compare blood vessels and explain their functions
Skills Students Should Develop
Design investigations to measure the rate of photosynthesis
Interpret graphs of limiting factors in photosynthesis
Analyse nutritional content of foods using given data
Investigate transpiration rates using a potometer
Measure and record heart rate responses to exercise
Practical Competencies Students Should Demonstrate
Test leaves for starch after different light conditions
Investigate factors affecting photosynthesis rate in Elodea
Dissect a flower and identify reproductive structures
Measure heart rate response to different levels of exercise
TEACHER NOTES — YEAR 1, TERM 2
Teaching Tips
The photosynthesis and nutrition units link well — use food as a bridge from plants making food to humans obtaining food
Digestion is best taught by tracing a single food item (e.g. a piece of bread) through the entire system
For the circulatory system: use a pumping demonstration (syringe/tubing) to illustrate blood pressure and valve function
Connect transpiration to osmosis from Term 1 — students should recognise the link
The heart rate investigation is highly motivating — use it as a key practical assessment opportunity
Common Student Misconceptions
Photosynthesis occurs only in chlorophyll — all green parts of a plant (not just leaves) can photosynthesize
Plants do NOT "breathe in CO₂" — CO₂ enters by diffusion down a concentration gradient
Arteries do not always carry oxygenated blood — the pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood
Bile is NOT an enzyme — it physically emulsifies fats but does not chemically digest them
Transpiration is not a waste process — it is essential for pulling water up the plant
The heart is not one pump but effectively two pumps side by side
Materials Required for Activities
Aquatic plants (Elodea or Cabomba) for photosynthesis investigation
Variegated leaves for testing role of chlorophyll
Potometer (or a simple syringe + capillary tube setup)
Model of the heart or heart dissection (if available)
Stethoscope for heart sound activity
Stop watches or phone timers for heart rate recording
YEAR 1 — TERM 3

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Definition and importance of excretion | The kidneys: structure and function | Nephron: filtration and selective reabsorption | Osmoregulation | Dialysis
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Excretion?
EXCRETION: The removal of waste products of METABOLISM (chemical reactions in cells) and substances in excess of requirements from the body.
NOT to be confused with:
EGESTION: removal of UNDIGESTED food (faeces) from the anus — this material was never absorbed into the body
SECRETION: production and release of useful substances (e.g. hormones, enzymes)
Key excretory products in humans:
CARBON DIOXIDE (CO₂): from aerobic respiration; removed by the lungs
UREA: from breakdown of excess amino acids in the liver (deamination); removed by kidneys
WATER: produced in aerobic respiration; removed by kidneys (urine), lungs (expired air), skin (sweat)
BILE PIGMENTS: from breakdown of haemoglobin; excreted into bile → removed in faeces
Why excretion is essential:
Waste products are TOXIC if they accumulate
Urea (from protein catabolism) is toxic to cells
CO₂ in blood would lower pH, disrupting enzyme function
The Liver — Deamination
Proteins cannot be stored in the body. When proteins are broken down or when amino acids are in excess, they cannot simply be excreted as amino acids.
The liver converts excess amino acids by DEAMINATION:
The AMINO GROUP (–NH₂) is removed from the amino acid
The amino group is converted to AMMONIA (NH₃) — toxic
Ammonia is immediately converted to UREA (less toxic) in the liver via the UREA CYCLE
UREA is carried in blood plasma to the KIDNEYS for excretion
The remaining carbon compound is used in respiration or stored as fat
Reaction: Amino acid → Organic acid (for respiration) + UREA (excreted in urine)
The Kidneys — Structure
Humans have TWO KIDNEYS — bean-shaped organs in the abdomen, one on each side of the spine.
KIDNEY STRUCTURE:
Outer CORTEX (dark red): contains the filtering units (nephrons)
Inner MEDULLA: contains collecting ducts and loops
PELVIS: hollow space where urine collects before draining to ureter
Blood supply:
RENAL ARTERY: brings blood with urea (and all other blood components) from aorta into kidney
RENAL VEIN: takes cleaned blood (urea removed) back to vena cava
URETER: tube carrying urine from kidney to BLADDER
URETHRA: tube from bladder to outside; urine is expelled during urination
Each kidney contains approximately ONE MILLION NEPHRONS — the functional units of the kidney.
The Nephron — Structure and Function
A NEPHRON is a microscopic tube that filters blood and produces urine. Each nephron consists of:
1. BOWMAN'S CAPSULE (renal capsule):
Cup-shaped structure enclosing a knot of capillaries called the GLOMERULUS
High blood pressure in the glomerulus forces small molecules out of blood into the capsule
2. PROXIMAL (FIRST) CONVOLUTED TUBULE:
Coiled tube in the cortex
Selective reabsorption of useful molecules
3. LOOP OF HENLÉ:
Long U-shaped section in the medulla
Important in water reabsorption; concentrates urine
4. DISTAL (SECOND) CONVOLUTED TUBULE:
Further regulation of mineral ions and pH
5. COLLECTING DUCT:
Many nephrons empty into one collecting duct
Water reabsorption controlled by ADH
TWO PROCESSES IN THE NEPHRON:
PROCESS 1 — ULTRAFILTRATION (in glomerulus/Bowman's capsule):
Blood enters glomerulus under HIGH PRESSURE
Small molecules are FORCED out of capillaries into Bowman's capsule
Molecules filtered: water, glucose, urea, amino acids, mineral salts, uric acid
Large molecules NOT filtered: red blood cells, white blood cells, platelets, plasma proteins (too big to pass through)
The filtered fluid entering Bowman's capsule = GLOMERULAR FILTRATE
PROCESS 2 — SELECTIVE REABSORPTION (in tubules):
Useful molecules are reabsorbed back into blood as the filtrate passes through the tubule
ALL GLUCOSE: completely reabsorbed by ACTIVE TRANSPORT (requires energy)
ALL AMINO ACIDS: completely reabsorbed by active transport
WATER: most reabsorbed by osmosis; amount controlled by ADH
MINERAL SALTS (Na⁺, K⁺, etc.): selectively reabsorbed
UREA: only partially reabsorbed (stays in tubule → concentrated in urine)
Result: Urine contains: water, urea, mineral salts, uric acid — NO glucose, NO amino acids, NO proteins, NO blood cells
Osmoregulation — Control of Water Balance
The kidneys regulate the WATER CONTENT of blood (osmoregulation).
KEY HORMONE: ADH (ANTI-DIURETIC HORMONE)
Produced by the HYPOTHALAMUS (in brain)
Released by PITUITARY GLAND (below brain)
CONTROL MECHANISM (negative feedback):
When BLOOD IS TOO CONCENTRATED (dehydration — e.g. not enough water drunk, or hot day):
1. Osmoreceptor cells in hypothalamus detect high solute concentration
2. Hypothalamus signals pituitary to RELEASE MORE ADH
3. ADH travels in blood to kidneys
4. ADH makes collecting duct walls MORE PERMEABLE to water
5. MORE water is reabsorbed from collecting duct back into blood
6. SMALL VOLUME of CONCENTRATED urine produced
7. Blood solute concentration returns to normal
When BLOOD IS TOO DILUTE (excess water drunk):
1. Osmoreceptors detect low solute concentration
2. LESS ADH released
3. Collecting duct walls LESS PERMEABLE to water
4. LESS water reabsorbed → MORE water stays in filtrate
5. LARGE VOLUME of DILUTE urine produced
6. Blood solute concentration returns to normal
Alcohol inhibits ADH → large volumes of dilute urine → dehydration (a cause of hangover)
Kidney Dialysis
When both kidneys fail (kidney failure), urea and other wastes build up to toxic levels in blood. Without treatment, this is fatal.
RENAL DIALYSIS (kidney dialysis machine):
Patient's blood is pumped from an artery through the machine
Blood flows past a partially permeable (dialysis) membrane
On the other side of the membrane: DIALYSIS FLUID of carefully controlled composition
Key principle — DIFFUSION across the membrane:
UREA: high in blood, absent in dialysis fluid → diffuses out of blood into dialysis fluid (removed)
GLUCOSE: dialysis fluid contains normal blood glucose concentration → no net movement (glucose is kept)
MINERAL SALTS: dialysis fluid adjusted to normal blood concentration → ions in excess move out; ions in deficit move in (balance maintained)
BLOOD CELLS and PROTEINS: too large to cross dialysis membrane → remain in blood
Advantages of dialysis: maintains life, available in hospitals
Disadvantages: requires treatment 3 times/week for ~4 hours; expensive; not as effective as normal kidneys; restricts lifestyle
KIDNEY TRANSPLANT: better long-term solution; requires compatible donor; patient must take immunosuppressant drugs for life to prevent organ rejection
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a labelled diagram of a nephron showing: Bowman's capsule with glomerulus, proximal and distal convoluted tubules, loop of Henlé, collecting duct, blood vessels (afferent/efferent arterioles)
2. Draw a flow diagram of osmoregulation: dehydration detected → hypothalamus → ADH released → collecting duct more permeable → water reabsorbed → concentrated urine → blood osmolarity normalises
curriculum.activity: Modelling the Nephron: Use dialysis tubing (visking tubing) filled with a mixture of glucose + starch solution (simulating blood filtrate). Place in pure water (simulating blood). After 30 minutes, test the water outside the tube for glucose (Benedict's) and starch (iodine). Discuss what the results tell you about selective reabsorption.
curriculum.homework:
1. Distinguish between excretion and egestion with one example of each.
2. Explain how urea is produced in the liver (deamination).
3. Describe the process of ultrafiltration in the kidney. What substances are filtered out and why are large proteins not filtered?
4. Explain the role of ADH in osmoregulation when blood solute concentration is too high.
5. Explain how a kidney dialysis machine works and why it must contain glucose solution in the dialysis fluid.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.topic: Coordination and Response — The Nervous System
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Nervous system overview | Neurones: sensory, motor, relay | The reflex arc | Synapses | The brain
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Coordination in the Body
Living organisms must respond to changes in their environment (stimuli) to survive. In humans, two systems coordinate responses:
1. NERVOUS SYSTEM: rapid, short-duration responses using electrical impulses
2. ENDOCRINE (HORMONAL) SYSTEM: slower, longer-lasting responses using chemical messengers (hormones)
STIMULUS: Any change in the environment (internal or external) that can be detected
RECEPTOR: A cell or structure that detects a stimulus (e.g. eye detects light, Pacinian corpuscle detects pressure)
EFFECTOR: A structure that carries out a response (e.g. muscle contracts, gland secretes)
COORDINATOR: The CNS (brain and spinal cord) that processes information and decides the response
Organisation of the Nervous System
The nervous system is divided into:
CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM (CNS):
BRAIN: processes all voluntary actions, memory, thought, emotions; coordinates body functions
SPINAL CORD: runs down the backbone inside the vertebral column; relays impulses between brain and body; coordinates reflex actions
PERIPHERAL NERVOUS SYSTEM (PNS):
All nerves outside the brain and spinal cord
SENSORY NERVES: carry impulses from receptors TO the CNS
MOTOR NERVES: carry impulses FROM the CNS to effectors
AUTONOMIC NERVOUS SYSTEM:
Controls automatic (involuntary) functions: heart rate, breathing, digestion, blood pressure
Two divisions: SYMPATHETIC (fight-or-flight — increases heart rate, etc.) and PARASYMPATHETIC (rest and digest — slows heart rate, etc.)
Neurones — Types and Structure
A NEURONE is a specialised nerve cell that transmits electrical impulses.
Three types:
1. SENSORY NEURONES:
Carry impulses FROM receptors (sense organs) TO the CNS (brain or spinal cord)
Have a long DENDRON (carries impulse toward cell body) and a shorter AXON (carries impulse away from cell body toward CNS)
Cell body is located OUTSIDE the spinal cord (in dorsal root ganglion)
2. MOTOR NEURONES:
Carry impulses FROM the CNS TO effectors (muscles, glands)
Have a short dendrite and a long AXON (can be over 1 metre long)
Cell body is located IN the spinal cord (ventral horn)
End at a NEUROMUSCULAR JUNCTION where the nerve meets muscle
3. RELAY NEURONES (INTERNEURONES):
Located entirely WITHIN the CNS (brain and spinal cord)
Connect sensory and motor neurones
Have many short dendrites and a short axon
Allow complex information processing
NEURONE STRUCTURE (all types have):
CELL BODY: contains nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes
AXON: long fibre that carries the electrical impulse
DENDRITES: short branched extensions that receive impulses from other neurones
MYELIN SHEATH: fatty insulating layer around many axons — speeds up impulse transmission; nodes of Ranvier are gaps in the myelin where the impulse "jumps" (saltatory conduction)
SYNAPTIC KNOBS (terminal boutons): swellings at the end of axons where synapses form
The Reflex Arc
A REFLEX is a RAPID, AUTOMATIC, INVOLUNTARY response to a stimulus. It DOES NOT require conscious thought — happens before the brain can process the stimulus.
Why reflexes are important:
Protection from harm (pulling hand from hot object)
Maintenance of body functions (breathing, blinking, pupil dilation)
Rapid enough to prevent or limit injury
SIMPLE REFLEX ARC — sequence of events:
1. STIMULUS: hot object touches skin (receptors in skin detect heat/pain)
2. SENSORY NEURONE: impulse travels from skin receptor → dorsal root → spinal cord
3. RELAY NEURONE: in the grey matter of spinal cord; connects sensory to motor neurone
4. MOTOR NEURONE: impulse travels from spinal cord → muscle in arm
5. EFFECTOR (MUSCLE): arm muscle CONTRACTS → hand is pulled away (response)
SIMULTANEOUSLY: a signal is also sent up to the BRAIN via relay neurones — so you FEEL the pain AFTER the reflex has already moved your hand.
Examples of reflex actions:
KNEE-JERK REFLEX: tapping the patellar tendon → quadriceps muscle contracts → leg kicks out
PUPIL REFLEX: bright light → pupils constrict (iris muscles respond); dim light → pupils dilate
BLINKING: object approaches eye → eyelid blinks shut
SALIVATION: smell of food → salivary glands secrete saliva
SNEEZING and COUGHING: clear airways of irritants
Synapses
A SYNAPSE is the junction between two neurones (or a neurone and an effector). Electrical impulses CANNOT jump across this gap directly — chemical transmission is used.
SYNAPTIC TRANSMISSION:
1. Electrical impulse arrives at PRESYNAPTIC TERMINAL (SYNAPTIC KNOB)
2. Impulse causes vesicles (sacs) containing NEUROTRANSMITTER to fuse with presynaptic membrane
3. NEUROTRANSMITTER (e.g. ACETYLCHOLINE) is released by EXOCYTOSIS into the SYNAPTIC CLEFT (gap)
4. Neurotransmitter diffuses across the cleft (very short distance ~20 nm)
5. Neurotransmitter binds to RECEPTOR PROTEINS on the POSTSYNAPTIC MEMBRANE
6. This stimulates the postsynaptic neurone to generate a new electrical impulse (if threshold is reached)
7. Neurotransmitter is BROKEN DOWN by enzymes (e.g. acetylcholinesterase) or reabsorbed by presynaptic cell → prevents continuous stimulation
Functions of synapses:
Allow signals to be sent in ONE DIRECTION only (vesicles only on presynaptic side)
Act as JUNCTIONS — one presynaptic neurone can connect to many postsynaptic neurones (divergence)
Several presynaptic neurones must fire together to reach threshold (SUMMATION) — filtering out weak/irrelevant signals
Site of drug/poison action: many drugs affect synapse function
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a complete labelled diagram of the reflex arc: receptor in skin → sensory neurone → relay neurone (in spinal cord grey matter) → motor neurone → effector (muscle)
2. Draw a synapse in detail: synaptic knob, vesicles, synaptic cleft, postsynaptic membrane with receptors, arrows showing neurotransmitter release
curriculum.activity: Reflex Testing: Test the knee-jerk reflex. Sit with legs dangling. Have a partner gently tap just below the kneecap with a ruler. Observe and record what happens. Try to predict and prevent the reflex response. What does this tell you about whether reflex arcs are voluntary?
curriculum.homework:
1. State the difference between the central nervous system and the peripheral nervous system.
2. Describe the function of each of the three types of neurone: sensory, relay, and motor.
3. Describe the sequence of events in a simple reflex arc when someone touches a very hot surface, from stimulus to response.
4. Explain why synaptic transmission ensures that nerve impulses travel in one direction only.
5. Explain what happens at a synapse when a nerve impulse arrives. Use these terms: neurotransmitter, synaptic vesicle, receptor, postsynaptic membrane.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.topic: The Eye, Skin, and Other Sense Organs
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Structure and function of the eye | Accommodation | Pupil reflex | Skin as a sense organ
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
The Human Eye — Structure
The eye is the receptor organ for LIGHT. It converts light energy into nerve impulses.
STRUCTURE OF THE EYE:
SCLERA: tough, white outer layer; maintains eye shape; protects internal structures
CORNEA: transparent front portion of sclera; dome-shaped; does most of the REFRACTION (bending) of light (about 70% of total)
IRIS: coloured ring of muscle around the pupil; controls PUPIL SIZE (how much light enters)
PUPIL: the hole in the centre of the iris; not a structure — it is just an opening
LENS: flexible, transparent, biconvex structure behind iris; fine-tunes focus (accommodation)
CILIARY BODY: ring of muscle surrounding the lens; connected to lens by SUSPENSORY LIGAMENTS
AQUEOUS HUMOUR: watery fluid in front of lens; helps maintain shape of front of eye; refracts light
VITREOUS HUMOUR: jelly-like fluid filling the main chamber; maintains eye shape
RETINA: light-sensitive inner layer covering the back of the eye
Contains PHOTORECEPTORS: RODS and CONES
RODS: sensitive to DIM LIGHT; detect shape/movement; NO colour discrimination; very numerous (~120 million)
CONES: sensitive to BRIGHT LIGHT; detect COLOUR; three types (red/green/blue); fewer (~6 million); concentrated at the FOVEA
FOVEA (yellow spot): point of maximum visual acuity; densely packed with cones; where light is focused in normal vision
BLIND SPOT (optic disc): where the OPTIC NERVE exits the eye; no photoreceptors → no vision here
OPTIC NERVE: bundle of nerve fibres carrying impulses from retina → brain (visual cortex in occipital lobe)
CHOROID: pigmented layer between retina and sclera; absorbs light (prevents reflection/scattering); contains blood vessels that supply retina
Accommodation
ACCOMMODATION: The process by which the lens changes shape to focus on objects at different distances.
The lens is elastic and can change from a ROUND (fat/thick) shape to a FLAT (thin) shape.
FOCUSING ON A NEAR OBJECT:
1. CILIARY MUSCLES CONTRACT
2. Ciliary body moves inward → reduces tension on suspensory ligaments
3. Suspensory ligaments go SLACK
4. Lens is ELASTIC → recoils to become MORE ROUND (more convex, fatter)
5. Fat lens refracts (bends) light MORE → focuses near object on retina
FOCUSING ON A DISTANT OBJECT:
1. CILIARY MUSCLES RELAX
2. Ciliary body ring widens
3. Suspensory ligaments become TAUT (tight)
4. Ligaments PULL lens → lens becomes THINNER and FLATTER
5. Flat lens refracts light LESS → focuses distant object on retina
MEMORY TIP: "Near = ciliary contracts = ligaments slack = fat lens"
The Pupil Reflex
The iris controls the SIZE of the pupil to regulate how much light enters the eye.
IN BRIGHT LIGHT:
Too much light could damage the retina
CIRCULAR (sphincter) MUSCLES of iris CONTRACT
RADIAL MUSCLES RELAX
Pupil becomes SMALLER (constricted)
Less light enters
IN DIM LIGHT:
Too little light reduces vision
RADIAL MUSCLES of iris CONTRACT
CIRCULAR MUSCLES RELAX
Pupil becomes LARGER (dilated)
More light enters
This is a REFLEX — controlled automatically by the autonomic nervous system. Both pupils change simultaneously even if only one eye is exposed to bright light.
Clinical use: Doctors shine a light in the eye to test if the pupil reflex is functioning — used to assess brain damage, anaesthesia depth, and drug effects.
Skin as a Sense Organ
The skin contains several types of sensory receptors that detect different stimuli:
PACINIAN CORPUSCLES: detect deep pressure and vibration
MEISSNER'S CORPUSCLES: detect light touch
FREE NERVE ENDINGS: detect pain, temperature (hot and cold), and very light touch
RUFFINI ENDINGS: detect sustained pressure and skin stretch
KRAUSE END BULBS: detect cold
Skin sensations:
Pain: protective signal; alerts you to tissue damage
Pressure: allows manipulation of objects
Temperature: helps maintain body temperature (thermoreception)
The fingertips, lips, and tongue have the HIGHEST DENSITY of receptors (most sensitive)
The back has the LOWEST DENSITY (least sensitive)
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a fully labelled diagram of the human eye showing all major structures with their functions listed
2. Draw two diagrams of the ciliary body and lens: (a) focusing on near object — thick round lens, contracted ciliary muscles, slack ligaments; (b) focusing on far object — thin flat lens, relaxed ciliary muscles, taut ligaments
curriculum.activity: Blind Spot Test: Draw a small dot on the left and a small cross 6 cm to the right on a piece of paper. Hold the paper at arm's length. Cover the left eye. Focus the right eye on the dot. Slowly move the paper closer. The cross disappears at a certain distance (when its image falls on the blind spot). Try with both eyes open — why does the cross not disappear?
curriculum.homework:
1. State the function of each of the following parts of the eye: (a) cornea, (b) iris, (c) retina, (d) fovea, (e) optic nerve.
2. Distinguish between rods and cones in terms of: light sensitivity, colour detection, and location in the retina.
3. Describe the changes that occur in the lens and ciliary muscles when you focus on a book held 20 cm from your eye.
4. Explain why we have a blind spot in our vision and where it is located in the eye.
5. Explain the pupil reflex when entering a dark room from bright sunlight.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Hormones vs nervous system | Major endocrine glands and hormones | Adrenaline | Insulin and glucagon — blood glucose regulation | Diabetes
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Hormones vs Nervous System
The body uses TWO communication systems:
FEATURE | NERVOUS SYSTEM | HORMONAL SYSTEM
Messenger | Electrical impulse | Chemical hormone
Transmission | Nerve fibres | Blood stream
Speed | Very fast (up to 120 m/s) | Slow (seconds to minutes)
Duration of response | Short-lived | Long-lasting
Target | Specific (one effector) | Widespread (all cells with receptors)
Example | Reflexes, voluntary movement | Growth, blood glucose control, puberty
HORMONE DEFINITION: A hormone is a chemical substance produced by an ENDOCRINE GLAND, transported in the BLOOD, and acts on TARGET ORGANS. Cells that respond to a hormone have specific RECEPTOR PROTEINS on their surface.
Major Endocrine Glands and Hormones
PITUITARY GLAND ("master gland") — base of brain:
Produces many hormones that control other glands
ADH: controls water reabsorption in kidneys
GROWTH HORMONE: stimulates growth
FSH and LH: control reproduction
THYROID GLAND — in neck:
THYROXINE: controls metabolic rate, growth, brain development; requires iodine
Underactive thyroid → slow metabolism, fatigue (hypothyroidism)
PANCREAS — below stomach:
INSULIN: lowers blood glucose (made by β-cells)
GLUCAGON: raises blood glucose (made by α-cells)
ADRENAL GLANDS — above each kidney:
ADRENALINE: "fight-or-flight" hormone; prepares for emergency
OVARIES (females):
OESTROGEN: controls puberty in females, menstrual cycle, prepares uterus
PROGESTERONE: maintains pregnancy
TESTES (males):
TESTOSTERONE: controls puberty in males, sperm production
Adrenaline — The Fight-or-Flight Hormone
ADRENALINE is released by the ADRENAL GLANDS (above kidneys) in response to STRESS, FEAR, EXCITEMENT, or EXERCISE.
EFFECTS OF ADRENALINE:
HEART RATE increases → more blood delivered to muscles
BREATHING RATE increases → more O₂ available
LIVER converts GLYCOGEN → GLUCOSE (gluconeogenesis) → raises blood glucose → more fuel for muscles
BLOOD VESSELS to muscles DILATE → more blood flow to muscles
BLOOD VESSELS to gut CONSTRICT → blood diverted away from digestion
PUPILS DILATE → better vision
HAIR STANDS ON END (piloerection)
MENTAL ALERTNESS increases
Purpose: Prepares the body for IMMEDIATE PHYSICAL ACTION ("fight or run away") — evolutionary survival response.
Adrenaline is unusual: it acts very QUICKLY (like a hormone but with nerve-speed onset) because it is released directly into blood from adrenal medulla, which is controlled by the sympathetic nervous system.
Insulin and Glucagon — Blood Glucose Regulation
Blood glucose must be maintained within narrow limits (~70–140 mg/100 ml). Too high (hyperglycaemia) or too low (hypoglycaemia) are both dangerous.
Blood glucose rises after a meal (carbohydrates → glucose absorbed from gut).
Blood glucose falls during exercise (glucose used in respiration).
KEY HORMONES from the PANCREAS:
INSULIN (from β-cells of islets of Langerhans):
Released when blood glucose is HIGH (after a meal)
Effects:
Stimulates liver and muscles to convert glucose → GLYCOGEN (glycogenesis — storage)
Increases uptake of glucose by cells for respiration
Blood glucose FALLS back to normal
GLUCAGON (from α-cells of islets of Langerhans):
Released when blood glucose is LOW (during exercise, between meals)
Effects:
Stimulates liver to convert GLYCOGEN → GLUCOSE (glycogenolysis)
Stimulates conversion of amino acids/fats → glucose (gluconeogenesis)
Blood glucose RISES back to normal
This is NEGATIVE FEEDBACK:
High glucose → insulin released → glucose falls → less insulin released
Low glucose → glucagon released → glucose rises → less glucagon released
GLYCOGEN is the storage form of glucose in liver and muscles
Diabetes Mellitus
DIABETES MELLITUS is a condition where blood glucose is not properly regulated.
TYPE 1 DIABETES (insulin-dependent):
β-cells in pancreas DESTROYED (auto-immune) → produce LITTLE or NO insulin
Blood glucose remains HIGH after meals → not controlled
Glucose excreted in urine (glycosuria)
Symptoms: extreme thirst, frequent urination, tiredness, weight loss
Treatment: insulin injections (multiple daily) OR insulin pump; monitoring blood glucose; managing diet and exercise
Typically develops in young people (childhood/adolescence)
TYPE 2 DIABETES (non-insulin-dependent):
Cells become RESISTANT to insulin (do not respond normally)
Pancreas may produce insulin but cells don't respond
Strongly linked to OBESITY, poor diet (high sugar/fat), sedentary lifestyle, genetic factors
Initially controlled by: diet modification, weight loss, exercise, oral drugs (e.g. metformin)
May eventually require insulin injections
Typically develops in adults (but increasingly in overweight adolescents)
Long-term complications of uncontrolled diabetes:
Damage to blood vessels → cardiovascular disease, kidney failure, stroke
Nerve damage (neuropathy)
Blindness (retinopathy)
Amputations (due to poor wound healing and nerve damage)
ICRANA TREATMENT — Insulin can now be produced by GENETICALLY ENGINEERED bacteria (see Chapter 21). Previously extracted from pig/cow pancreases.
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a flow diagram showing blood glucose regulation: after meal → blood glucose rises → β-cells detect → insulin released → cells take up glucose/glycogen stored → blood glucose falls → negative feedback loop
2. Draw a comparison table: Nervous system vs Hormonal system across: type of messenger, speed, duration, example
curriculum.activity: Modelling Negative Feedback: Use a large syringe of coloured water. Place a bucket with a target level marked. Have one student add water (eating — raising level), another student remove water with the syringe (insulin — lowering level). Demonstrate how the system self-corrects. Relate to blood glucose homeostasis.
curriculum.homework:
1. State TWO ways in which hormone communication differs from nervous communication.
2. Describe the role of adrenaline in preparing the body for emergency action. List FOUR specific effects.
3. Explain how blood glucose is returned to normal after a meal, including the role of insulin and negative feedback.
4. Distinguish between Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes in terms of cause and treatment.
5. A person with untreated Type 1 diabetes has glucose in their urine. Explain why this happens.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.topic: Homeostasis — Temperature and Blood Glucose Regulation
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Concept of homeostasis and negative feedback | Temperature regulation (thermoregulation) | Role of the skin | Fever
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Homeostasis?
HOMEOSTASIS: The maintenance of a CONSTANT INTERNAL ENVIRONMENT despite external changes.
The body's internal environment includes:
Body temperature (~37°C)
Blood glucose concentration (~90 mg/100 ml)
Blood water content/osmolarity
Blood pH (~7.4)
Blood CO₂ concentration
Blood O₂ concentration
Why is homeostasis essential?
Enzymes function only within narrow temperature and pH ranges
Cells and tissues can only function in precise chemical conditions
Disruption of these conditions → illness, organ failure, death
HOMEOSTATIC MECHANISM — NEGATIVE FEEDBACK:
1. Normal value (SET POINT) of a variable is established
2. RECEPTOR/DETECTOR detects when the variable moves away from set point
3. Signal sent to CONTROL CENTRE (usually the brain)
4. EFFECTOR produces a response that OPPOSES the change
5. Variable is returned toward set point
6. Response is REDUCED (negative feedback prevents over-correction)
Example: Body temperature rises → receptors detect → brain coordinates response → sweating/blood vessel dilation → heat lost → temperature falls back to normal → response stops.
Temperature Regulation (Thermoregulation)
The human body temperature is maintained at approximately 37°C (±0.5°C). This is the optimal temperature for body enzymes.
TEMPERATURE REGULATION CENTRE: HYPOTHALAMUS (in the brain)
Contains THERMORECEPTORS that detect blood temperature
Receives inputs from skin temperature receptors
Coordinates heating and cooling responses
RESPONSES TO HIGH BODY TEMPERATURE (too hot):
1. SWEATING:
Sweat glands (in dermis) produce SWEAT (mainly water + salts)
Sweat flows to skin surface → EVAPORATES
Evaporation requires LATENT HEAT → removes heat from skin → body cools
More effective in dry, windy conditions; ineffective in very humid conditions
2. VASODILATION of skin capillaries:
Arterioles near skin surface DILATE (widen)
MORE BLOOD flows through skin capillaries close to surface
Heat is RADIATED from the large surface area of skin to the environment
Skin appears FLUSHED/RED
3. HAIR LIES FLAT:
Erector pili muscles RELAX
Hairs lie flat against skin
Reduces the insulating trapped air layer
More heat can escape from skin surface
RESPONSES TO LOW BODY TEMPERATURE (too cold):
1. SHIVERING:
Skeletal muscles contract RAPIDLY and involuntarily
Muscle contraction releases HEAT
Raises body temperature
2. VASOCONSTRICTION of skin capillaries:
Arterioles near skin surface CONSTRICT (narrow)
LESS blood flows near skin surface
Less heat lost by radiation
Skin may appear PALE or BLUE (blue lips)
Blood diverted to vital organs
3. PILOERECTION (goose bumps):
Erector pili muscles CONTRACT
Hairs stand UPRIGHT
In animals with fur, this traps a layer of air → insulation
In humans (with little hair), largely ineffective — produces goose bumps
4. INCREASED METABOLIC RATE:
Thyroxine (long-term) stimulates more metabolic heat production
Adrenaline (short-term) increases cellular respiration
The Skin and Thermoregulation
The SKIN is a vital organ for temperature regulation. Its key structures:
EPIDERMIS: outer layer; waterproof; protective; contains keratin (dead cells on surface)
DERMIS: inner layer; contains sweat glands, capillary networks, hair follicles, sense receptors, sebaceous glands
SWEAT GLANDS: tubular glands in dermis; open via sweat pores on skin surface; produce sweat
ARTERIOLES (small blood vessels in dermis): dilate or constrict to control heat loss
HAIR FOLLICLES: produce hairs; erector pili muscles attached; hairs trap air when erected
SEBACEOUS GLANDS: produce SEBUM (oil) — waterproofs skin and has mild antibacterial properties
FAT LAYER (subcutaneous adipose tissue): beneath dermis; acts as thermal INSULATION; reduces heat loss; also energy store
Fever
FEVER: an elevated body temperature caused by infection, usually above 38°C.
How fever develops:
1. Pathogens produce toxins OR white blood cells release chemicals called PYROGENS
2. Pyrogens act on the hypothalamus → RESET POINT (thermostat) moves upward
3. Body responds as if it is too cold: vasoconstriction, shivering, reduced sweating
4. Body temperature rises to new (higher) set point → FEVER
BENEFITS of mild fever:
Many pathogens reproduce poorly at higher temperatures
Immune system (antibody production, phagocytosis) functions FASTER at higher temperatures
DANGERS of very high fever (>40°C):
Body enzymes begin to denature
Brain cells can be damaged
Convulsions (febrile seizures) in children
Treated with antipyretic drugs (aspirin, paracetamol/acetaminophen), cooling, fluids
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a diagram of the skin in cross-section labelling: epidermis, dermis, subcutaneous fat, sweat gland and duct, hair follicle and erector pili muscle, arteriole, sensory nerve ending
2. Draw a negative feedback diagram for temperature control: stimulus (high temp) → thermoreceptors in hypothalamus → effectors (sweat glands, vasodilation) → correction → feedback
curriculum.activity: Modelling Thermoregulation: Wrap one hand in a thin plastic bag (sealed). Wrap the other hand normally. Place both in warm water for 2 minutes. Which feels hotter? Why? Relate to sweating and evaporative cooling. Alternatively: measure room temperature, body temperature (forehead thermometer), and temperature after exercise. Discuss the regulatory mechanisms.
curriculum.homework:
1. Define homeostasis. State FOUR variables that are regulated by homeostasis in the human body.
2. Explain what is meant by negative feedback in homeostasis.
3. Describe THREE responses that occur when body temperature rises too high. For each, explain how it reduces body temperature.
4. Explain why a fever (mild) can actually help the body fight an infection.
5. A person with hypothermia (dangerously low body temperature) has pale skin and is shivering. Explain both observations in terms of thermoregulation mechanisms.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Phototropism | Gravitropism (geotropism) | Role of auxin (IAA) | Practical importance
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What are Tropic Responses?
TROPISM: A growth RESPONSE by a plant (or part of a plant) to a directional stimulus. The growth is either TOWARD or AWAY from the stimulus.
Types:
PHOTOTROPISM: response to LIGHT
GRAVITROPISM (GEOTROPISM): response to GRAVITY
HYDROTROPISM: response to WATER
Terminology:
POSITIVE tropism: growth TOWARD the stimulus
NEGATIVE tropism: growth AWAY FROM the stimulus
Tropisms are controlled by plant hormones — mainly AUXIN (IAA, indole-3-acetic acid).
Phototropism
PHOTOTROPISM: growth response of a plant shoot or root to the direction of LIGHT.
SHOOT: POSITIVELY phototropic — grows TOWARD light
Adaptive value: moves leaves toward sunlight for maximum photosynthesis
ROOT: NEGATIVELY phototropic — grows AWAY from light
Adaptive value: grows into soil where water and minerals are found
EXPLANATION using AUXIN (IAA):
1. Light hits the shoot from one side
2. Auxin produced at shoot TIP migrates AWAY FROM the lit side
3. More auxin accumulates on the SHADED SIDE of the shoot
4. Cells on the shaded side ELONGATE MORE (auxin promotes cell elongation)
5. The shaded side grows longer than the lit side
6. Shoot BENDS TOWARD the light
Evidence for the role of the tip:
Remove tip → no bending response
Cover tip with foil → no bending response (even if rest of shoot lit)
Place agar block soaked with auxin from tip on one side → shoot bends away from agar side (because auxin promotes elongation)
Frits Went (1928) first isolated the chemical from oat coleoptile tips and placed it on agar blocks to demonstrate asymmetric growth.
Gravitropism (Geotropism)
GRAVITROPISM: growth response to the direction of GRAVITY.
ROOT: POSITIVELY gravitropic — grows DOWNWARD (in direction of gravity)
Adaptive value: anchors plant; reaches water deep in soil
SHOOT: NEGATIVELY gravitropic — grows UPWARD (against gravity)
Adaptive value: reaches sunlight
EXPLANATION:
In ROOTS (where auxin has the OPPOSITE effect — high auxin INHIBITS growth in roots):
1. Gravity causes auxin to accumulate on the LOWER SIDE of a horizontal root
2. High auxin on lower side INHIBITS cell elongation in roots
3. Upper side (less auxin) elongates more
4. Root curves DOWNWARD (toward gravity)
In SHOOTS (where auxin promotes growth):
1. Gravity causes auxin to accumulate on the LOWER SIDE of a horizontal shoot
2. High auxin on lower side PROMOTES cell elongation in shoots
3. Lower side grows more → shoot curves UPWARD (away from gravity)
Key difference: Auxin PROMOTES elongation in SHOOTS but INHIBITS elongation in ROOTS (at high concentrations).
Auxin (IAA) — Properties and Practical Uses
AUXIN (IAA — Indole-3-Acetic Acid) is a PLANT GROWTH HORMONE.
Produced at: MERISTEMS (growing tips of shoots and roots)
Mechanism: moves to one side of a shoot/root → causes asymmetric growth
EFFECTS of auxin:
In SHOOTS: promotes cell elongation (at normal concentrations)
In ROOTS: inhibits cell elongation (roots are more sensitive to auxin — same concentration that promotes shoot growth inhibits root growth)
PRACTICAL APPLICATIONS of plant hormones:
1. ROOTING POWDER: synthetic auxin applied to stem cuttings → promotes formation of adventitious roots → allows propagation of plants without seeds
2. WEED KILLERS (HERBICIDES): some selective herbicides are synthetic auxins (e.g. 2,4-D); applied to broad-leaved weeds growing among narrow-leaved crop plants. Broad-leaved plants absorb more → overgrowth, stem distortion, death. Narrow-leaved grasses (crop plants) less affected.
3. PREVENTING FRUIT DROP: auxin sprayed on orchard trees to prevent premature fruit drop → extends harvest window
4. FRUIT RIPENING: ethylene (another plant hormone) promotes fruit ripening; auxin can delay it
5. SEEDLESS FRUITS: auxin can stimulate fruit formation without fertilisation → produces seedless fruits (parthenocarpy)
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw three diagrams of seedling shoots showing: (a) no light — grows straight; (b) light from one side — bends toward light; (c) tip removed — no response. Label the auxin distribution in each
2. Draw a diagram comparing auxin effects in shoots vs roots: bar chart showing low/high auxin concentration vs growth rate for shoot cells and root cells
curriculum.activity: Phototropism Investigation: Grow cress or bean seedlings in darkness for 3–4 days. Move one set to a box with a hole on one side. Observe after 3 days. Compare with seedlings grown in even light. Draw and photograph the results. Measure the bending angle.
curriculum.homework:
1. Define tropism. State the difference between a positive and a negative tropism.
