2. Explain why it is an advantage for plant shoots to be positively phototropic.
3. Explain how auxin causes a shoot to bend toward light. Include: tip, shaded side, cell elongation, bending.
4. Explain why the same concentration of auxin promotes shoot growth but inhibits root growth.
5. Describe ONE commercial application of auxin in horticulture and explain the biological basis.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Definition and types of drugs | Effects of alcohol | Heroin and opioids | Effects on synapses | Medicinal drugs: antibiotics, IVF
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is a Drug?
A DRUG is any substance taken into the body that changes or affects chemical reactions in the body — altering its physiology and/or psychology.
Drugs can be:
MEDICINAL: used to treat disease (antibiotics, painkillers, vaccines)
RECREATIONAL: taken for pleasure or altered consciousness (alcohol, cannabis, heroin)
PERFORMANCE-ENHANCING: taken to improve athletic performance (anabolic steroids)
ADDICTION: A state of compulsive need for a drug. When a person is addicted:
TOLERANCE develops: the body adapts → needs increasing doses to achieve the same effect
WITHDRAWAL SYMPTOMS: unpleasant physical or psychological symptoms when drug use stops
DEPENDENCE: physical and/or psychological reliance on the drug
TOLERANCE: receptor numbers or sensitivity in the brain decrease → more drug needed for same effect.
Alcohol (Ethanol)
Alcohol (ethanol) is a CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM DEPRESSANT — it SLOWS DOWN nerve transmission.
SHORT-TERM EFFECTS:
Relaxation and reduced inhibition (low doses)
Impaired coordination and judgment
Slowed reaction time — VERY dangerous for driving
Slurred speech
Blurred vision
Nausea and vomiting (high doses)
Loss of consciousness (very high doses)
COMA and DEATH (extreme doses — due to respiratory depression)
How alcohol affects the nervous system:
Enhances inhibitory neurotransmitters (GABA) → slows brain activity
Reduces activity of the prefrontal cortex (decision-making)
Affects the cerebellum (coordination)
LONG-TERM EFFECTS of heavy alcohol use:
LIVER DAMAGE: alcohol is metabolised in the liver; chronic use → fatty liver → CIRRHOSIS (irreversible scarring; liver cannot function)
BRAIN DAMAGE: alcohol kills brain cells; leads to memory loss, dementia
HEART DISEASE: increases blood pressure; weakens heart muscle
ADDICTION (alcohol dependence)
MALNUTRITION: alcohol provides empty calories; poor nutrient absorption
SOCIAL EFFECTS: family breakdown, accidents, job loss
ALCOHOL IN PREGNANCY: crosses placenta → FETAL ALCOHOL SYNDROME: brain damage, growth retardation, facial abnormalities in baby.
Heroin and Opioids
HEROIN is derived from MORPHINE (extracted from opium poppies). It is an OPIATE — a type of narcotic analgesic (painkiller) and CNS DEPRESSANT.
EFFECTS:
Intense euphoria (rush of pleasure)
Pain relief (analgesia)
Sedation and drowsiness
Slowed breathing
How heroin works at synapses:
Heroin binds to OPIOID RECEPTORS in the brain (normally for endorphins — natural painkillers)
Mimics and overstimulates natural neurotransmitters (endorphins)
Dramatically increases dopamine release → extreme pleasure
Over time: brain produces FEWER natural endorphins; receptors reduced → MORE heroin needed for same effect
DANGERS:
Highly ADDICTIVE (physical and psychological)
OVERDOSE: can stop breathing (respiratory depression) → death
INFECTION from sharing needles: HIV, hepatitis B and C
WITHDRAWAL: severe pain, vomiting, muscle cramps, psychological distress
Manufactured forms may contain dangerous contaminants
TREATMENT: Methadone (opioid substitute therapy) reduces withdrawal symptoms; counselling; rehabilitation.
Drugs and Synapses
Many drugs affect the nervous system by interfering with SYNAPTIC TRANSMISSION:
1. ECSTASY (MDMA): prevents REUPTAKE of serotonin (neurotransmitter) → serotonin accumulates in synapse → prolonged activation of receptors → feelings of euphoria and empathy. Long-term: depletes serotonin → depression.
2. COCAINE: blocks reuptake of dopamine → dopamine accumulates in synapse → intense euphoria; highly addictive.
3. HEROIN: mimics endorphins; binds to and activates opioid receptors.
4. NICOTINE (in tobacco): binds to acetylcholine receptors (nicotinic receptors); stimulates release of dopamine → reward and pleasure; highly addictive.
5. ALCOHOL: enhances GABA (inhibitory) neurotransmission; inhibits NMDA glutamate receptors (excitatory) → sedation.
6. ANTIDEPRESSANTS (SSRIs, e.g. Prozac): block reuptake of serotonin → increased serotonin in synapse → improved mood in depression.
General mechanism types:
Mimic neurotransmitters (agonists): e.g. heroin mimics endorphins
Block receptors (antagonists): e.g. some antipsychotic drugs block dopamine receptors
Prevent reuptake: e.g. cocaine, SSRIs
Prevent release of neurotransmitter: e.g. Botox prevents acetylcholine release
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a synapse diagram and show where different drugs act: (1) cocaine blocking reuptake transporter, (2) heroin binding to receptor, (3) GABA-enhancing drug effect of alcohol
2. Create a drug effects comparison table: Drug | Type | Effect on CNS | Short-term effects | Long-term effects | Addiction potential
curriculum.activity: Case Study Analysis: Provide students with 3 case studies of individuals affected by drug use (alcohol, heroin, nicotine). For each case, identify: symptoms described, biological mechanisms involved (using knowledge of synapses), short-term vs long-term effects, social consequences, and possible treatments.
curriculum.homework:
1. Define the term "drug". Give one example of a medicinal drug and one example of a recreational drug.
2. Explain what is meant by tolerance and why a drug addict may need increasingly large doses.
3. Explain the short-term effect of alcohol on reaction time and coordination.
4. Explain how heroin causes intense pleasure by acting at synapses.
5. Describe TWO long-term effects of heavy alcohol consumption and explain the biological mechanism for each.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Asexual reproduction: features and examples | Sexual reproduction: features | Advantages and disadvantages of each | Reproduction in flowering plants: pollination
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Asexual Reproduction
ASEXUAL REPRODUCTION: The production of offspring from a SINGLE PARENT organism, without the formation of gametes (sex cells), and without fertilisation.
KEY FEATURES:
Only ONE parent required
No gametes involved
No fertilisation
Offspring are GENETICALLY IDENTICAL to parent (and to each other) = CLONES
Very fast process
MECHANISM: Mitosis — cells divide to produce genetically identical copies of the parent
EXAMPLES:
1. BINARY FISSION (in bacteria): Single bacterial cell replicates its DNA → cell splits into TWO equal cells → two bacteria, each identical to parent → can occur every 20 minutes under ideal conditions
2. BUDDING (in yeast): Small outgrowth (bud) forms from parent yeast cell → grows → eventually separates as new organism → identical to parent
3. VEGETATIVE PROPAGATION (in plants): Parents produce new plants from vegetative (non-reproductive) parts:
RUNNERS (stolons): e.g. strawberry — horizontal stems that touch the ground and produce new plants
TUBERS: e.g. potato — underground storage organs; each "eye" (bud) can grow into a new plant
BULBS: e.g. onion, daffodil — large modified buds that split to form daughter bulbs
RHIZOMES: e.g. ginger, iris — horizontal underground stems that produce shoots
CUTTINGS: Humans can propagate plants from cuttings (stem, leaf) — a form of artificial asexual reproduction
4. SPORE PRODUCTION (in fungi and ferns): Haploid spores produced by mitosis; dispersed; germinate into new organisms
5. REGENERATION: Some animals (e.g. starfish, planaria worms) can regrow complete organisms from fragments
ADVANTAGES of asexual reproduction:
RAPID production of large numbers of offspring
Only ONE parent needed (e.g. isolated organism can reproduce)
Successful organisms can produce many identical copies of their successful genotype
No energy wasted in finding a mate
DISADVANTAGES:
No GENETIC VARIATION — all offspring identical to parent
If a disease attacks one individual, it attacks ALL (no resistance variation)
Cannot ADAPT to changing environments through natural selection
Sexual Reproduction
SEXUAL REPRODUCTION: The production of offspring involving the formation of GAMETES (sex cells — eggs and sperm in animals; ovules and pollen in plants) and FERTILISATION (fusion of gametes).
KEY FEATURES:
TWO PARENTS normally required (or at least two gametes)
Gametes formed by MEIOSIS (halves chromosome number)
Fertilisation restores full chromosome number
Offspring have a UNIQUE COMBINATION of genetic material from both parents
Offspring are GENETICALLY DIFFERENT from parents and from each other (except identical twins)
ADVANTAGES of sexual reproduction:
Produces GENETIC VARIATION in offspring
Variation provides the raw material for NATURAL SELECTION
Populations can ADAPT to changing environments
Some offspring may carry combinations of genes that make them more resistant to disease or better suited to new conditions
DISADVANTAGES:
REQUIRES TWO PARENTS — energy spent finding a mate
SLOWER than asexual reproduction
Only HALF of population produces offspring (females)
Complex reproductive processes required
Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants — Pollination
POLLINATION: The transfer of POLLEN from the ANTHER (male part) to the STIGMA (female part) of a flower. This must happen before fertilisation can occur.
FLOWER STRUCTURE (for sexual reproduction):
SEPALS: outer protective leaves of bud
PETALS: brightly coloured; attract pollinators; may have nectaries
STAMENS (male): consists of ANTHER (produces pollen) on a FILAMENT (stalk)
PISTIL/CARPEL (female): consists of STIGMA (sticky tip — receives pollen) + STYLE (stalk) + OVARY (contains one or more ovules with egg cells)
TYPES OF POLLINATION:
SELF-POLLINATION: Pollen transferred to the stigma of the SAME flower or another flower on the SAME plant
No genetic variation produced
Useful when pollinators are unavailable
CROSS-POLLINATION: Pollen transferred to the stigma of a DIFFERENT plant of the same species
Produces genetic variation
Preferred by most plants
AGENTS OF POLLINATION:
INSECT-POLLINATED FLOWERS (e.g. mango, sunflower, hibiscus):
Brightly coloured petals to attract insects
Strong scent (nectary nectar) as reward
Pollen grains are LARGE, STICKY, often spiky — attach to insect bodies
Stigma is STICKY — captures pollen
Usually few pollen produced (less wastage)
WIND-POLLINATED FLOWERS (e.g. maize, grass, oil palm, wheat):
Small, dull, no petals or very small petals (no need to attract insects)
No scent, no nectar
Pollen grains are SMALL, LIGHT, SMOOTH, produced in huge quantities
LONG STAMENS hang outside flower — easily catch wind
LARGE FEATHERY STIGMA hangs outside flower to catch airborne pollen
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw and label a complete flower showing: sepals, petals, stamens (anther + filament), carpel (stigma + style + ovary), receptacle
2. Draw comparison diagrams of insect-pollinated vs wind-pollinated flower features side by side
curriculum.activity: Flower Dissection: Obtain several flowers (different types if available). Dissect each flower and identify all the parts: sepals, petals, stamens, carpels. Count the number of anthers. Identify whether the flower is insect or wind pollinated based on its features. Press and mount the dissected parts with labels.
curriculum.homework:
1. State THREE differences between sexual and asexual reproduction.
2. Explain why asexual reproduction produces offspring that are genetically identical to the parent.
3. Give TWO advantages of asexual reproduction and TWO advantages of sexual reproduction.
4. Distinguish between self-pollination and cross-pollination.
5. Describe THREE features of an insect-pollinated flower and explain how each feature helps with pollination.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Male and female reproductive anatomy | Menstrual cycle | Fertilisation and implantation | Pregnancy and development
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Male Reproductive System
TESTES (singular: testis):
Two glands held in the SCROTUM (outside the body)
Why outside? Sperm production requires temperature LOWER than body temperature (about 2°C cooler)
Produce SPERM (male gametes) by MEIOSIS
Also produce TESTOSTERONE (male sex hormone — controls male secondary sexual characteristics: deep voice, facial/body hair, muscle development)
SEMINIFEROUS TUBULES: coiled tubes inside each testis where sperm are produced
EPIDIDYMIS: coiled tube behind testis where sperm mature and are stored
VAS DEFERENS (sperm duct): tube from epididymis to urethra; sperm travel along this during ejaculation
GLANDS:
SEMINAL VESICLES: produce fructose-rich fluid (provides energy for sperm swimming)
PROSTATE GLAND: produces alkaline fluid (neutralises acid in the vagina)
BULBOURETHRAL GLAND: produces lubricating mucus
TOGETHER: semen = sperm + secretions from these glands
URETHRA: tube through the penis; carries both urine and semen (not at the same time)
PENIS: organ for depositing semen into the vagina during sexual intercourse; becomes erect for penetration
Female Reproductive System
OVARIES (two): produce EGG CELLS (ova) by MEIOSIS; also produce OESTROGEN and PROGESTERONE
OVULATION: release of a mature EGG from the ovary; occurs approximately monthly
OVIDUCT (FALLOPIAN TUBE):
Tube connecting ovary to uterus
Lined with CILIA that waft the egg toward the uterus
Site of FERTILISATION
UTERUS (WOMB):
Muscular, pear-shaped organ
ENDOMETRIUM (uterine lining): thick, spongy layer with many blood vessels; where the embryo implants; shed during menstruation
Walls are thick muscle (MYOMETRIUM): contracts during labour
CERVIX: lower narrow part of uterus; dilates during childbirth
VAGINA: birth canal; receives penis during intercourse; opening to outside
The Menstrual Cycle
The menstrual cycle is a monthly hormonal cycle preparing the female body for possible pregnancy. Average length: ~28 days.
DAY 1–5: MENSTRUATION
Uterus lining (endometrium) BREAKS DOWN and is shed as menstrual blood
Occurs because progesterone levels dropped in previous cycle
DAY 1–14: FOLLICLE STAGE
FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) from pituitary → stimulates development of an egg follicle in ovary
Follicle produces OESTROGEN
Oestrogen → thickening of uterus lining (preparing for implantation)
Oestrogen → inhibits FSH (negative feedback)
Oestrogen → stimulates LH surge
DAY 14: OVULATION
LH (luteinising hormone) SURGE from pituitary → triggers release of egg (ovulation)
Egg released from ovary → enters oviduct
DAY 14–28: LUTEAL PHASE
Ruptured follicle → CORPUS LUTEUM (yellow body)
Corpus luteum produces PROGESTERONE
Progesterone maintains and develops the uterine lining (ready for embryo)
Progesterone inhibits FSH and LH
IF FERTILISATION DOES NOT OCCUR:
Corpus luteum degenerates
Progesterone levels FALL
Uterine lining breaks down → menstruation begins (Day 1 of next cycle)
IF FERTILISATION OCCURS:
Embryo produces HCG (human chorionic gonadotropin)
HCG maintains corpus luteum → corpus luteum continues producing progesterone
Uterine lining is maintained → no menstruation
Progesterone levels remain high throughout pregnancy
Fertilisation and Early Development
FERTILISATION: fusion of sperm and egg cell to form a ZYGOTE.
During sexual intercourse:
Semen deposited in vagina (~200–500 million sperm)
Sperm swim using flagella through cervix → uterus → oviduct
Many sperm are lost: die in vaginal acidity; fail to enter cervix; lost in wrong oviduct
ONE sperm reaches the egg in the oviduct
Sperm's ACROSOME releases enzymes to penetrate follicle cells and egg membrane
Sperm nucleus fuses with egg nucleus → ZYGOTE (diploid: 46 chromosomes)
Immediately: changes in egg membrane prevent further sperm from entering (polyspermy block)
EARLY DEVELOPMENT:
Zygote undergoes MITOSIS → 2 cells → 4 cells → 8 cells → MORULA → BLASTOCYST
Takes about 6–7 days for the developing embryo to travel down the oviduct to the uterus
IMPLANTATION: blastocyst embeds in uterine lining (endometrium)
PLACENTA develops: connects embryo to mother's blood supply
PLACENTA functions:
Allows glucose, O₂, water, amino acids, vitamins, minerals to pass from mother's blood → fetus
Allows CO₂ and urea to pass from fetus → mother's blood
Produces HCG → maintains corpus luteum and pregnancy
Barrier to most harmful substances (but NOT alcohol, nicotine, thalidomide, rubella virus, HIV)
AMNIOTIC SAC: membrane filled with AMNIOTIC FLUID surrounding the fetus
Protects fetus from shock/physical impact
Maintains constant temperature
Allows fetus to move
UMBILICAL CORD: connects fetus to placenta; contains umbilical vein (oxygenated blood to fetus) and umbilical arteries (deoxygenated blood from fetus)
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a fully labelled diagram of the female reproductive system: ovaries, oviducts, uterus, endometrium, cervix, vagina
2. Draw a graph of the menstrual cycle showing hormone levels (FSH, LH, oestrogen, progesterone) over 28 days with key events: menstruation, ovulation, corpus luteum
curriculum.activity: Menstrual Cycle Analysis: Provide students with a graph of hormone levels across a 28-day cycle. Ask them to identify: day of ovulation, days of menstruation, when the corpus luteum is active, and what would happen if fertilisation occurred on day 14 in terms of hormone changes.
curriculum.homework:
1. State the function of the following in the male reproductive system: (a) testes, (b) epididymis, (c) seminal vesicles, (d) urethra.
2. Describe what happens during the follicular phase of the menstrual cycle (days 1–14).
3. Explain what would happen to the uterine lining if progesterone levels did not rise after ovulation.
4. Describe the events that occur during fertilisation, from sperm reaching the egg to zygote formation.
5. State FOUR substances that cross the placenta from mother to fetus, and state the importance of each.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Secondary sexual characteristics | Hormonal control of puberty | Sexually transmitted infections | Contraception
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Secondary Sexual Characteristics and Puberty
PUBERTY: The period of physical and sexual maturation driven by sex hormones. Begins: females typically 10–13 years; males 12–14 years.
IN MALES (controlled by TESTOSTERONE):
Growth of testes and penis
Development of pubic, axillary (underarm) and facial hair
Voice deepens ("voice breaks")
Growth spurt
Increased muscle mass
Sperm production begins
Development of sex drive (libido)
IN FEMALES (controlled by OESTROGEN):
Growth of ovaries and uterus
Breast development
Development of pubic and axillary hair
Growth spurt
Widening of hips
Menstruation begins
Development of sex drive
OESTROGEN ROLES in the adult female:
Controls monthly menstrual cycle
Maintains female secondary sexual characteristics
During pregnancy: helps maintain uterus, prepares breasts for lactation
TESTOSTERONE ROLES in the adult male:
Stimulates and maintains sperm production
Maintains male secondary sexual characteristics
Contributes to muscle anabolism and bone density
Contraception
CONTRACEPTION: Methods to prevent pregnancy by preventing fertilisation or implantation.
1. BARRIER METHODS:
CONDOM (male/female): physical barrier prevents sperm reaching egg; ALSO protects against STIs
DIAPHRAGM (cap): dome-shaped device placed over cervix; must be used with spermicide
2. HORMONAL METHODS:
ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILL ("the pill"): contains synthetic oestrogen and progesterone; prevents ovulation; thickens cervical mucus to block sperm; thins uterine lining
INJECTION (Depo-Provera): progesterone injection every 3 months; prevents ovulation
IMPLANT: small rod implanted under skin; releases progesterone continuously
PATCH: worn on skin; releases hormones through skin
3. INTRAUTERINE DEVICES (IUD, coil):
Small T-shaped device inserted into uterus by doctor
Copper IUD: copper ions toxic to sperm; prevents fertilisation; lasts 5–10 years
Hormonal IUD (Mirena): releases progesterone; prevents implantation and thickens mucus
4. EMERGENCY CONTRACEPTION ("morning-after pill"):
Taken within 72 hours of unprotected sex
High-dose progestogen; delays or prevents ovulation; may prevent implantation
5. PERMANENT METHODS:
VASECTOMY (male): cutting/tying vas deferens → sperm cannot leave body
TUBAL LIGATION (female): cutting/tying oviducts → eggs cannot reach uterus
6. NATURAL METHODS:
ABSTINENCE: most effective
RHYTHM (calendar) METHOD: avoiding sex around ovulation; unreliable due to cycle variation
Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs)
STIs are infections transmitted primarily through sexual contact (exchange of bodily fluids).
1. HIV/AIDS:
Cause: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) — a retrovirus
Transmission: unprotected sex (especially anal); infected blood (needle sharing, blood transfusion); mother to child (birth, breastfeeding)
HIV attacks HELPER T LYMPHOCYTES (CD4+ cells) → destroys immune system
AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome): stage where immune system so damaged that opportunistic infections (e.g. TB, Pneumocystis pneumonia, Kaposi's sarcoma) occur
Treatment: ART (antiretroviral therapy) — suppresses virus but no cure; allows near-normal life
Prevention: condoms; clean needles; HIV testing; antiretrovirals in pregnancy
2. GONORRHOEA:
Cause: Neisseria gonorrhoeae (bacterium)
Transmission: unprotected vaginal, oral, or anal sex; eye contact with infected fluids
Symptoms: thick yellow/green discharge from penis or vagina; burning sensation when urinating; in some cases no symptoms
Complications if untreated: pelvic inflammatory disease (females) → infertility; epididymitis (males) → infertility; spread to bloodstream
Treatment: ANTIBIOTICS (though antibiotic-resistant strains are increasing — now a global concern)
3. SYPHILIS:
Cause: Treponema pallidum (bacterium)
Stages: primary (painless sore/chancre), secondary (rash, flu symptoms), latent, tertiary (heart disease, brain damage)
Treatment: penicillin
4. CHLAMYDIA:
Most common STI in many countries; often ASYMPTOMATIC
Can cause infertility if untreated
Treatment: antibiotics
PREVENTION OF STIs:
Consistent and correct use of CONDOMS
Reducing number of sexual partners
Regular STI testing
Treatment and notification of partners
Education and awareness
Draw the following diagrams in your notebook. Use a sharp pencil. Label clearly with ruled label lines.
1. Draw a comparison table of contraceptive methods: Method | How it works | Effectiveness | Protects against STIs?
2. Draw a flow diagram showing HIV infection → CD4 T cell destruction → AIDS → opportunistic infections
curriculum.activity: STI Awareness Campaign: In groups, create an educational poster or leaflet about one STI (HIV, gonorrhoea, or chlamydia). Include: the pathogen, transmission routes, symptoms, complications, treatment, and prevention. Display posters around the classroom.
curriculum.homework:
1. List FOUR secondary sexual characteristics that develop in males during puberty. State the hormone responsible.
2. Explain how the oral contraceptive pill prevents pregnancy. Name the hormones it contains.
3. Explain how HIV destroys the immune system and why untreated HIV eventually leads to AIDS.
4. State THREE ways in which HIV can be transmitted from one person to another.
5. Explain why a condom is the only contraceptive method that also protects against STIs.
EXPECTED LEARNING OUTCOMES — YEAR 1, TERM 3
Key Knowledge Students Should Gain
Explain the structure and function of the kidney, including the nephron
Describe ultrafiltration, selective reabsorption, and urine formation
Explain the role of ADH in osmoregulation using negative feedback
Describe how the nervous system coordinates responses
Explain reflex arcs and synaptic transmission
Describe structure of the eye and mechanism of accommodation
Explain hormonal coordination (insulin, glucagon, adrenaline, ADH)
Describe temperature regulation mechanisms
Explain tropic responses in plants and the role of auxin
Describe asexual and sexual reproduction
Explain the menstrual cycle and fertilisation
Describe transmission and effects of STIs
Skills Students Should Develop
Model kidney filtration using dialysis tubing experiments
Test knee-jerk reflex and pupil light reflex
Investigate phototropism in seedlings
Analyse hormone level graphs for the menstrual cycle
Dissect and label reproductive organs of flowers
Practical Competencies Students Should Demonstrate
Investigate osmosis using dialysis tubing (kidney model)
Investigate phototropism in cress seedlings
Dissect and identify parts of a flower
TEACHER NOTES — YEAR 1, TERM 3
Teaching Tips
Homeostasis should be introduced as the unifying principle linking kidneys, nervous system, and hormones
The reflex arc diagram is frequently assessed — ensure all students can draw and label it from memory
Use the analogy of a thermostat for negative feedback — very helpful for slower learners
Tropism practicals need at least a week of advance preparation — start growing seedlings early
The reproduction units contain sensitive content — review school guidelines on sex education before teaching
STI education should be balanced, non-judgmental, and factual — emphasise prevention and testing
Common Student Misconceptions
Excretion is NOT egestion — many students confuse these
ADH makes the kidney collect ducts MORE permeable, not less
Reflexes are involuntary — students often say "you decide to move your hand from heat" — correct this firmly
Hormones travel in blood to ALL cells but only those with the right receptors respond
Insulin does not directly lower blood glucose — it causes cells to take up glucose and liver to store it as glycogen
Auxin does not cause bending directly — it promotes cell elongation in the cells it reaches
Materials Required for Activities
Visking (dialysis) tubing, sucrose and glucose solutions for kidney modelling
Seedlings (cress or beans) for tropism experiment, cardboard boxes
Prepared slides of kidney tissue if available
Hormone level graph worksheets for menstrual cycle analysis
Fresh flowers for dissection (multiple types preferred)
Rubber mallet for knee-jerk reflex demonstration (or small ruler)

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Chromosome structure | Genes and alleles | Mitosis
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Chromosomes and Genes
CHROMOSOMES are thread-like structures found in the nucleus of every cell. They are made of DNA coiled around protein. Each chromosome carries hundreds or thousands of GENES.
Key facts:
• Human body cells contain 46 chromosomes arranged as 23 PAIRS of homologous chromosomes
• Each pair consists of one chromosome inherited from the mother and one from the father
• Sex cells (gametes) contain only 23 chromosomes — one from each pair
• Chromosomes are only visible under a microscope during cell division when they condense and coil up
GENE: a section of DNA that codes for the production of a specific protein. Proteins determine the characteristics of an organism.
LOCUS: the fixed position of a gene on a chromosome. Each gene occupies a specific locus.
ALLELE: one of two or more alternative forms of the same gene. For example, the gene for eye colour may have a 'brown' allele or a 'blue' allele.
For any gene, a diploid organism has TWO alleles — one on each chromosome of a homologous pair:
• If both alleles are the same → HOMOZYGOUS (e.g. BB or bb)
• If the two alleles are different → HETEROZYGOUS (e.g. Bb)
DOMINANT allele: expressed in the phenotype even when only one copy is present. Represented by a capital letter.
RECESSIVE allele: only expressed in the phenotype when two copies are present (i.e. homozygous). Represented by a lower-case letter.
GENOTYPE: the genetic make-up of an organism (e.g. BB, Bb, bb)
PHENOTYPE: the observable characteristics resulting from the genotype (e.g. brown eyes, tall plant)
DNA Structure and the Genetic Code
DNA (DEOXYRIBONUCLEIC ACID): the molecule that carries the genetic information of all living organisms.
Structure of DNA:
• Double helix — two strands twisted around each other like a twisted ladder
• Each strand is a chain of NUCLEOTIDES
• Each nucleotide consists of: a deoxyribose sugar + a phosphate group + a nitrogen base
• The four bases are: ADENINE (A), THYMINE (T), CYTOSINE (C), GUANINE (G)
• The two strands are held together by BASE PAIRING:
— Adenine always pairs with Thymine (A–T)
— Cytosine always pairs with Guanine (C–G)
• The base sequence along a gene codes for the sequence of amino acids in a protein
A GENE codes for the production of a specific protein. Different sequences of three bases (codons) code for different amino acids. The order of amino acids determines the shape and function of the protein.
MUTATION: a change in the base sequence of DNA. Mutations can be caused by:
• Radiation (ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma rays)
• Chemical mutagens (certain pesticides, tobacco smoke constituents)
• Spontaneous errors during DNA replication
Most mutations are HARMFUL (disrupt normal protein function) or NEUTRAL. Rarely, a mutation may be BENEFICIAL — giving the organism an advantage.
Chromosome mutations: entire chromosomes or segments can be duplicated, deleted, inverted, or translocated during cell division. Example: Down syndrome (trisomy 21 — three copies of chromosome 21 instead of two).
Mitosis — Cell Division for Growth and Repair
MITOSIS is the type of cell division that produces two genetically identical daughter cells. It occurs in all body (somatic) cells for:
• Growth — increasing cell number
• Repair — replacing damaged or dead cells
• Asexual reproduction
Before mitosis, DNA REPLICATION occurs: each chromosome is copied so the cell has double the normal DNA content.
Stages of mitosis (simplified):
1. PROPHASE: chromosomes condense and become visible; nuclear membrane breaks down
2. METAPHASE: chromosomes line up along the equator (middle) of the cell; spindle fibres attach to chromosomes
3. ANAPHASE: spindle fibres contract and pull identical chromatids to opposite poles of the cell
4. TELOPHASE: nuclear membranes reform around each set of chromosomes; chromosomes uncoil
5. CYTOKINESIS: cytoplasm divides, producing two daughter cells
Result of mitosis:
• Two genetically IDENTICAL daughter cells
• Both cells are DIPLOID (same chromosome number as parent cell)
• Human body cells: 46 chromosomes → mitosis → two cells each with 46 chromosomes
Cancer: uncontrolled mitosis caused by mutations in genes that regulate cell division. Cells divide rapidly without stopping, forming tumours. Cancer-causing agents are called CARCINOGENS (e.g. UV radiation, tobacco chemicals).
Stem cells: undifferentiated cells capable of repeated mitosis and differentiation into specialised cells. Important in tissue repair, development, and medicine.
curriculum.activity:
Using coloured clay or pipe cleaners, model a cell with 4 chromosomes (2 pairs). Demonstrate:
• DNA replication (duplicate each chromosome)
• Mitosis: line up pairs at equator, separate to poles
• Result: 2 identical cells, each with 4 chromosomes
Record diagrams of each mitosis stage.
curriculum.homework:
1. State the difference between a gene and an allele.
2. A cell has 12 chromosomes. After mitosis, how many chromosomes will each daughter cell have? Explain.
3. Define homozygous and heterozygous, giving an example of each.
4. Explain why mitosis produces genetically identical cells.
5. State two causes of gene mutation and explain why most mutations are harmful.
6. What is the base-pairing rule in DNA, and why is it important for replication?

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Meiosis vs mitosis | Gamete formation | Monohybrid inheritance
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Meiosis — Cell Division for Reproduction
MEIOSIS is the type of cell division that produces gametes (sex cells: sperm and eggs). Meiosis occurs in the reproductive organs (testes and ovaries in humans).
Key features of meiosis:
• Produces FOUR genetically different daughter cells
• Each daughter cell is HAPLOID (half the chromosome number of the parent cell)
• Human parent cell: 46 chromosomes → meiosis → four cells each with 23 chromosomes
Meiosis involves TWO divisions:
MEIOSIS I (Reduction Division):
• Homologous chromosome pairs line up together
• Homologous pairs are separated to opposite poles
• Result: two cells, each with 23 chromosomes (but each chromosome is still double-stranded)
• CROSSING OVER: homologous chromosomes exchange segments of DNA, creating new allele combinations → genetic variation
MEIOSIS II:
• Similar to mitosis
• The doubled chromosomes (chromatids) separate
• Result: four haploid cells
Genetic variation from meiosis:
• INDEPENDENT ASSORTMENT: during Meiosis I, homologous pairs line up randomly → each gamete gets a random mixture of maternal and paternal chromosomes
• CROSSING OVER: exchange of DNA between homologous chromosomes creates entirely new combinations of alleles
• Random FERTILISATION: any sperm can fertilise any egg
Summary comparison:
Mitosis: 2 identical diploid cells; for growth/repair
Meiosis: 4 genetically different haploid cells; for sexual reproduction
FERTILISATION: restores the diploid number. Haploid sperm (23 chromosomes) + haploid egg (23 chromosomes) → diploid zygote (46 chromosomes).
Monohybrid Inheritance
MONOHYBRID INHERITANCE: inheritance of a single characteristic controlled by a single gene with two alleles.
Genetic terminology:
• Dominant allele: expressed when one or two copies present (written as capital letter, e.g. B)
• Recessive allele: expressed only when two copies present (written as lower case, e.g. b)
• Homozygous dominant: BB (phenotype: brown eyes)
• Heterozygous: Bb (phenotype: brown eyes — B is dominant)
• Homozygous recessive: bb (phenotype: blue eyes)
PUNNETT SQUARE: diagram used to predict the outcomes of a genetic cross.
Example 1: Cross between homozygous brown-eyed (BB) and blue-eyed (bb):
Parents:       BB   ×   bb
Gametes:       B, B     b, b
Offspring:     Bb, Bb, Bb, Bb
All offspring: heterozygous (Bb) → brown eyes
Example 2: Cross between two heterozygous brown-eyed parents (Bb × Bb):
Punnett square:
B        b
B:      BB       Bb
b:      Bb       bb
Genotype ratio: 1 BB : 2 Bb : 1 bb
Phenotype ratio: 3 brown eyes : 1 blue eyes
Probability of blue-eyed child: 1/4 (25%)
Co-dominance: when two alleles are both expressed in the heterozygote. Example: ABO blood type has codominant A and B alleles.
Sex linkage: genes on sex chromosomes, most commonly the X chromosome. Males (XY) have only one copy of X-linked genes — they cannot be carriers.
Example: Red-green colour blindness is X-linked recessive. Symbol: X^B (normal) and X^b (colour blind)
• Affected male: X^b Y
• Carrier female: X^B X^b
• Affected female: X^b X^b
Because males have only one X chromosome, X-linked recessive conditions are much more common in males (e.g. haemophilia, colour blindness, Duchenne muscular dystrophy).
Sex Determination
Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes:
• 22 pairs of AUTOSOMES (non-sex chromosomes)
• 1 pair of SEX CHROMOSOMES: XX (female) or XY (male)
Sex determination cross:
Mother: XX (produces all X gametes)
Father: XY (produces X gametes and Y gametes)
Punnett square for sex determination:
X        Y
X:      XX       XY
X:      XX       XY
Genotype ratio: 2 XX (female) : 2 XY (male)
Probability of a boy or girl: 50% : 50%
It is the FATHER who determines the sex of the child — the Y chromosome comes from the sperm.
Note: Although the ratio is always theoretically 50:50, there is natural variation in actual birth ratios. No single outcome can be predicted with certainty.
SEX-LINKED GENES: genes located on the X chromosome. Because females have two X chromosomes, they can be carriers of recessive X-linked conditions without being affected. Males with one copy of the recessive allele are always affected.
Haemophilia A example:
• X^H = normal clotting; X^h = haemophilia
• Carrier mother: X^H X^h × Normal father: X^H Y
• Offspring:
— X^H X^H: normal female
— X^H X^h: carrier female
— X^H Y: normal male
— X^h Y: haemophiliac male
• Probability of haemophiliac son: 25% (of all children), or 50% of sons
Pedigree charts: family trees showing inheritance of a condition across generations. Circles = females; squares = males; filled shapes = affected individuals.
curriculum.activity:
Use two coins to simulate a Bb × Bb cross:
• Heads = B (dominant), Tails = b (recessive)
• Each student tosses both coins simultaneously 20 times
• Record genotype each time: HH=BB, HT or TH=Bb, TT=bb
• Calculate observed phenotype ratio (brown:blue)
• Compare class totals — do they approach 3:1?
Discuss: Why might individual results differ from expected?
curriculum.homework:
1. State two ways meiosis produces genetic variation.
2. In pea plants, tall (T) is dominant over short (t). Cross Tt × Tt. State the expected phenotype ratio of offspring.
3. A woman is a carrier for haemophilia (X^H X^h). Her husband is unaffected (X^H Y). What is the probability their son will have haemophilia? Show your working with a Punnett square.
4. Explain why males are more often affected by X-linked recessive conditions than females.
5. What is the difference between genotype and phenotype?
6. Define the term 'co-dominance' and give one example.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Continuous and discontinuous variation | Causes of variation | Environmental effects
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Types of Variation
VARIATION: the differences in characteristics between individuals of the same species.
All variation has two possible causes:
1. GENETIC VARIATION: differences in genotype (DNA)
2. ENVIRONMENTAL VARIATION: differences caused by the environment (diet, exercise, climate, disease, etc.)
Most characteristics are influenced by BOTH genes and environment.
DISCONTINUOUS VARIATION: characteristics that fall into distinct, separate categories with no intermediate forms.
Examples:
• ABO blood groups (A, B, AB, O — cannot be intermediate)
• Sex (male or female)
• Attached or detached earlobes
• PTC tasting ability (can taste / cannot taste)
• Sickle cell trait (normal, carrier, affected)
Features of discontinuous variation:
• Controlled by a single gene (usually)
• Little or no environmental influence
• Data shown as a bar chart
CONTINUOUS VARIATION: characteristics that show a range of values between two extremes, with many intermediate forms.
Examples:
• Height
• Body mass
• Foot length
• Finger length
• Skin colour
• Intelligence
Features of continuous variation:
• Controlled by multiple genes (polygenic inheritance)
• Significantly influenced by the environment
• Data shown as a bell-shaped (normal distribution) curve
• Shows a continuous range of values
Importance of variation: genetic variation within a population is essential for NATURAL SELECTION. Without variation, all individuals respond identically to environmental changes, making extinction more likely.
Causes of Genetic Variation
Genetic variation arises from:
1. MEIOSIS:
• Independent assortment — random orientation of homologous pairs at Meiosis I
• Crossing over — exchange of DNA between homologous chromosomes creates new allele combinations
• Both processes mean no two gametes are genetically identical (except in identical twins from same zygote)
2. RANDOM FERTILISATION:
• Any sperm can fertilise any egg → enormous number of possible genotype combinations
3. MUTATION:
• Random changes in DNA sequence
• The original source of ALL new alleles
• Can be beneficial, neutral, or harmful
• Mutation rate increased by mutagens: UV radiation, X-rays, gamma rays, chemical mutagens
4. SEXUAL REPRODUCTION:
• Combines genetic material from two different parents
• Each offspring gets a unique combination of alleles
Asexual reproduction produces offspring that are genetically identical to the parent (clones) — no genetic variation unless a mutation occurs.
ENVIRONMENTAL CAUSES OF VARIATION:
• Diet: undernutrition stunts growth; obesity increases body mass
• Exercise: physical activity increases muscle mass
• Disease: can affect height, weight, and development
• Climate and sunlight: affect skin colour (melanin production), plant leaf size
• Altitude: people living at high altitudes develop more red blood cells
• Education and experience: affect intelligence and skill
Example: Identical twins have the same genotype (DNA). Any differences between them (height, weight, personality) are caused by environmental factors. This demonstrates the interplay between genes and environment.
curriculum.activity:
Students measure: (a) hand span (cm), (b) height (cm), (c) determine blood group if known (or tongue-rolling ability)
For hand span and height: pool all measurements, group into 5 cm categories, draw a frequency histogram.
For tongue rolling: count rollers vs. non-rollers — draw a bar chart.
Discuss: Why do hand span and height show a bell curve? Why does tongue rolling show a bar chart?
curriculum.homework:
1. Distinguish between continuous and discontinuous variation with one example of each.
2. State three ways meiosis contributes to genetic variation.
3. A student measures the heights of 50 students. Describe the shape of the graph expected and explain why.
4. Explain how the environment can affect a characteristic that is also genetically controlled.
5. Why is genetic variation important for a species' survival?
6. ABO blood type shows discontinuous variation. Explain why it cannot be intermediate (e.g. 'between A and B').

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Natural selection | Adaptive features | Antibiotic resistance
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Natural Selection
NATURAL SELECTION: the process by which organisms best adapted to their environment are more likely to survive, reproduce, and pass on their alleles to the next generation.
Darwin's theory of natural selection (1859) — key points:
1. OVERPRODUCTION: organisms produce more offspring than the environment can support
2. VARIATION: individuals within a population show variation in their characteristics
3. STRUGGLE FOR EXISTENCE (COMPETITION): limited resources → competition for food, water, mates, territory
4. SURVIVAL OF THE FITTEST (DIFFERENTIAL SURVIVAL): individuals with advantageous characteristics (adaptations) are more likely to survive
5. INHERITANCE: survivors reproduce and pass their advantageous alleles to offspring
6. CHANGE OVER TIME: over many generations, the frequency of advantageous alleles increases in the population; the species gradually changes
This process is sometimes called SELECTIVE PRESSURE — the environmental factor that determines which individuals survive.
Evidence for natural selection:
• Fossil record — shows changes in species over time
• Antibiotic resistance in bacteria (see below)
• Pesticide resistance in insects
• Industrial melanism in peppered moths (Biston betularia)
• Darwin's finches — different beak shapes evolved on different Galápagos islands due to different food sources
Peppered moth example:
Before industrial revolution: pale-coloured moths camouflaged on light lichen-covered bark → dark moths more visible to predators → dark moths rare
After industrial revolution: soot darkened bark; lichen died → pale moths now visible to predators → dark moths had better camouflage → dark moths survived and reproduced more → dark moths became common in industrial areas
When pollution declined: balance shifted back toward pale moths
Adaptive Features
An ADAPTATION is a feature that makes an organism better suited to its environment, increasing its chances of survival and reproduction.
Types of adaptations:
1. STRUCTURAL adaptations — physical features
• Polar bear: thick fat (blubber) for insulation; white fur for camouflage in snow; large padded feet to spread weight on ice
• Cactus: thick stem stores water; leaf spines reduce transpiration and deter herbivores; deep/widespread roots
• Camel: hump stores fat (not water); long eyelashes keep out sand; can tolerate large body temperature fluctuations; concentrated urine reduces water loss
• Fish: streamlined body for movement through water; gills for gas exchange; lateral line detects water vibrations
• Xerophytes (desert plants): sunken stomata; thick waxy cuticle; reduced leaf area; CAM metabolism (close stomata during day)
• Hydrophytes (water plants): no waxy cuticle; stomata on upper surface; large air spaces (aerenchyma) for buoyancy
2. BEHAVIOURAL adaptations
• Migration — birds, fish, wildebeest move seasonally to exploit resources
• Hibernation — bears, hedgehogs reduce metabolic rate in winter
• Nocturnal activity — owls, bats avoid daytime predators and heat
3. PHYSIOLOGICAL adaptations
• High red blood cell count at altitude
• Efficient enzyme temperature optima matched to organism's habitat temperature
ARTIFICIAL SELECTION (Selective Breeding):
Humans deliberately select individuals with desirable traits for breeding.
Examples:
• Dairy cattle bred for high milk yield
• Wheat bred for disease resistance and high yield
• Dogs bred for size, temperament, or working ability
• Roses bred for flower size, colour, fragrance
Process: select desired parents → breed → select offspring with desired trait → breed again → repeat over many generations
Antibiotic Resistance — Natural Selection in Action
ANTIBIOTIC RESISTANCE is a real-time example of natural selection occurring in bacteria.
Antibiotic resistance — how it develops:
1. A population of bacteria contains genetic variation (some have a mutation giving resistance to an antibiotic)
2. Antibiotic is administered — acts as a SELECTIVE PRESSURE
3. Non-resistant bacteria are killed
4. Resistant bacteria SURVIVE and REPRODUCE rapidly (bacteria can divide every 20 minutes)
5. Resistant bacteria pass the resistance gene to all daughter cells by mitosis
6. Resistant bacteria can also transfer plasmids (carrying resistance genes) to other bacteria horizontally
7. Over time, the entire bacterial population becomes resistant
MRSA (Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus):
• A strain of bacteria resistant to many antibiotics
• Major problem in hospitals — kills thousands annually worldwide
• Can cause wound infections, pneumonia, bloodstream infections
How to reduce antibiotic resistance:
• Only prescribe antibiotics when truly necessary (not for viral infections)
• Complete the full course of antibiotics (never stop early — weakly resistant bacteria may survive)
• Do not use antibiotics in livestock farming unnecessarily
• Develop new antibiotics
• Improve hygiene in hospitals to prevent spread
This example demonstrates: variation in bacteria → antibiotic acts as selective pressure → resistant bacteria survive and reproduce → resistant population evolves. This is natural selection happening within months, not millions of years.
curriculum.activity:
Cut 30 pale paper moths and 30 dark paper moths. Place half of each colour on a pale surface (light-coloured cloth or paper), half on a dark surface.
Students act as 'predators' and pick up moths as fast as possible for 30 seconds.
Record how many of each colour were 'eaten'. Calculate % of each colour remaining.
Discuss: Why were more pale moths eaten on the dark surface? How would this change the population over generations?
curriculum.homework:
1. State the five key steps in Darwin's theory of natural selection.
2. Explain how antibiotic resistance in bacteria develops, using the principles of natural selection.
3. State three structural adaptations of a camel and explain how each helps the camel survive in a desert.
4. Distinguish between natural selection and artificial selection.
5. Explain why completing a full course of antibiotics helps prevent the development of resistant bacteria.
6. Why did the frequency of dark peppered moths increase in industrial areas of England?

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Ecosystems | Food chains and webs | Energy transfer
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Ecosystem Terminology
ECOLOGY: the study of the relationships between organisms and their environment.
Key terms:
• HABITAT: the place where an organism lives
• POPULATION: all the individuals of one species living in the same area at the same time
• COMMUNITY: all the populations of different species living in the same area at the same time
• ECOSYSTEM: the community of organisms plus their physical (abiotic) environment, interacting together
• ABIOTIC FACTORS: non-living environmental factors (temperature, light intensity, rainfall, pH, wind speed, salinity, oxygen concentration)
• BIOTIC FACTORS: living factors (competition, predation, disease, parasitism, mutualism)
Organisms can be classified by feeding role:
• PRODUCER: photosynthetic organism (plants, algae) that converts light energy into chemical energy in organic molecules
• CONSUMER: organism that gets energy by feeding on other organisms
• PRIMARY CONSUMER: herbivore — eats producers
• SECONDARY CONSUMER: carnivore — eats primary consumers
• TERTIARY CONSUMER: carnivore — eats secondary consumers
• DECOMPOSER: organism (bacteria, fungi) that breaks down dead organic matter, releasing mineral ions back into the environment
FOOD CHAIN: shows the transfer of energy from one organism to the next, starting with a producer.
Example: grass → grasshopper → frog → snake → hawk
FOOD WEB: a network of interconnected food chains, showing all feeding relationships in an ecosystem.
TROPHIC LEVEL: the position of an organism in a food chain.
• 1st trophic level: producers
• 2nd trophic level: primary consumers
• 3rd trophic level: secondary consumers
• 4th trophic level: tertiary consumers
Energy Transfer Through Ecosystems
Energy enters ecosystems from the SUN (solar energy). Only about 1% of the sunlight reaching the Earth's surface is captured by photosynthesis.
Energy transfer through food chains is INEFFICIENT:
At each trophic level, energy is LOST:
• Used in RESPIRATION (to power metabolic processes, movement, body heat)
• Lost as HEAT to the environment
• Passed out in FAECES (undigested material)
• Used in EXCRETION (urea, etc.)
• Not eaten (bones, roots, shells)
Only about 10% of energy at each trophic level is passed to the next level.
Why food chains rarely have more than 5 levels:
After 5 trophic levels, the amount of energy remaining is so small it cannot support a population of top predators.
Ecological pyramids:
PYRAMID OF NUMBERS: shows the number of organisms at each trophic level
• Can be irregular (e.g. one oak tree supports millions of caterpillars)
PYRAMID OF BIOMASS: shows the dry mass of organisms at each trophic level
• Always pyramid-shaped (biomass decreases up the chain)
• More useful than pyramid of numbers
PYRAMID OF ENERGY: shows the energy available at each trophic level per unit area per unit time
• Always pyramid-shaped
• Most informative — shows actual energy transfers
Implication for food production:
Feeding crop plants directly to humans is much more efficient than feeding them to livestock first.
• 100 kg of plant → 10 kg of beef (90 kg lost)
• 100 kg of plant → much more human food
Vegetarian diets are more energy-efficient, which is important for food security.
Global context: intensive agriculture with large-scale monocultures increases food production efficiency but reduces biodiversity and can have negative environmental effects.
curriculum.activity:
Provide students with organism cards for a local Nigerian woodland/savanna ecosystem:
Grass, cassava leaves, termites, grasshoppers, lizards, frogs, snakes, hawks, bacteria
Students draw arrows showing energy flow between organisms, creating a food web.
Then answer: (1) Identify two food chains of 4+ organisms. (2) Which organisms are producers? (3) Predict the effect on the hawk population if frogs were eliminated by disease.
curriculum.homework:
1. State the difference between a food chain and a food web.
2. Explain why only about 10% of energy is transferred between each trophic level.
3. A food chain has 4 trophic levels. If producers have 100,000 kJ of energy, estimate how much energy reaches the top carnivore.
4. Explain why pyramids of energy are always pyramid-shaped, unlike pyramids of numbers.
5. Suggest why vegetarian diets require less land to produce the same amount of food energy.
6. Define the terms 'producer', 'consumer', and 'decomposer'. Give one example of each.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Carbon cycle | Nitrogen cycle | Role of decomposers
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
The Carbon Cycle
The CARBON CYCLE describes the movement of carbon through living organisms and the physical environment.
Processes that REMOVE CO₂ from the atmosphere:
• PHOTOSYNTHESIS: plants, algae and cyanobacteria absorb CO₂ and convert it to glucose and other organic molecules
Processes that RETURN CO₂ to the atmosphere:
• RESPIRATION: all living organisms (plants, animals, fungi, bacteria) respire and release CO₂
• COMBUSTION (burning): burning fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) and wood releases CO₂
• DECOMPOSITION: decomposers (bacteria and fungi) break down dead organisms and organic waste, releasing CO₂ through their respiration
Carbon in the biosphere:
• Carbon is stored in: living organisms (as carbohydrates, proteins, fats, DNA), dead organic matter, soil humus, fossil fuels, carbonate rocks (limestone), oceans (dissolved CO₂ and carbonate ions)
FOSSIL FUELS:
• Formed over millions of years from compressed remains of ancient organisms
• When burned, they release CO₂ that was locked away, increasing atmospheric CO₂
• This contributes to the ENHANCED GREENHOUSE EFFECT
Greenhouse effect:
• CO₂ (and methane, water vapour) in the atmosphere absorb infrared radiation emitted by the Earth
• This warms the Earth — a natural process essential for life
• Burning fossil fuels and deforestation increases CO₂ levels beyond natural levels
• Enhanced greenhouse effect → global warming → climate change
Deforestation effects on the carbon cycle:
• Trees are major carbon stores (carbon in wood)
• Cutting down and burning trees releases stored carbon as CO₂
• Fewer trees means less CO₂ removed from atmosphere by photosynthesis
• Net effect: increases atmospheric CO₂
The Nitrogen Cycle
The NITROGEN CYCLE describes how nitrogen moves between the atmosphere, soil, and living organisms.
Nitrogen is essential for making amino acids, proteins, and nucleic acids (DNA).
Atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) makes up 78% of air but CANNOT be used directly by plants or animals.
Processes in the nitrogen cycle:
1. NITROGEN FIXATION: converting N₂ gas into usable compounds (ammonium/nitrate)
• Lightning: high temperature causes N₂ + O₂ → oxides of nitrogen → dissolve in rain → nitrates
• Nitrogen-fixing bacteria in soil (e.g. Azotobacter) — free-living
• Nitrogen-fixing bacteria in root nodules of leguminous plants (e.g. Rhizobium in pea, bean, clover roots)
2. NITRIFICATION: converting ammonium compounds to nitrates
• Carried out by NITRIFYING BACTERIA in the soil
• Step 1: Nitrite bacteria: ammonium → nitrites (NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻)
• Step 2: Nitrate bacteria: nitrites → nitrates (NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻)
• Plants absorb nitrates through roots to make amino acids and proteins
3. DECOMPOSITION: dead organisms → ammonium compounds
• DECOMPOSERS (bacteria and fungi) break down proteins in dead organisms and excretory products
• Proteins → amino acids → deamination → ammonium compounds (NH₄⁺)
4. DENITRIFICATION: nitrates → N₂ gas
• DENITRIFYING BACTERIA convert nitrates back to nitrogen gas
• These bacteria are anaerobic — found in waterlogged soils
• This returns nitrogen to the atmosphere, completing the cycle
Importance of bacteria in the nitrogen cycle:
• Nitrogen-fixing bacteria: make N₂ available
• Nitrifying bacteria: produce nitrates for plants
• Decomposers: return nitrogen from dead matter to soil
• Denitrifying bacteria: return nitrogen to atmosphere
Fertilisers:
• Farmers add nitrate or ammonium fertilisers to increase plant growth
• Leaching of nitrates into water bodies can cause EUTROPHICATION (see Week 38)
curriculum.activity:
Students draw a fully labelled carbon cycle from memory.
Must include: atmosphere (CO₂), photosynthesis (arrow from atmosphere to plants), respiration (arrows from all organisms to atmosphere), feeding (plant → animal), death and decomposition (organisms → decomposers → CO₂), combustion of fossil fuels.
Label each arrow with the process name. Add which organisms carry out each process.
curriculum.homework:
1. Name two processes that return carbon dioxide to the atmosphere.
2. Explain how burning fossil fuels affects the carbon cycle and the climate.
3. State the role of nitrogen-fixing bacteria in the nitrogen cycle.
4. Describe how nitrogen from a dead animal eventually becomes available to a plant.
5. Explain why waterlogged soils lead to loss of nitrogen from the ecosystem.
6. Why are leguminous plants (beans, peas, clover) valuable in crop rotation?

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Population growth | Limiting factors | Sigmoid growth curve
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Population Growth and the Sigmoid Curve
A POPULATION is a group of organisms of the same species living and interacting in the same area at the same time.
Factors affecting population growth rate:
• BIRTH RATE: number of organisms born per unit time
• DEATH RATE: number of organisms dying per unit time
• IMMIGRATION: organisms moving into the population area
• EMIGRATION: organisms leaving the population area
If birth rate > death rate: population INCREASES
If birth rate < death rate: population DECREASES
A SIGMOID (S-SHAPED) POPULATION GROWTH CURVE shows the typical pattern of population growth in a limited environment:
Phase 1 — LAG PHASE:
• Population grows slowly at first
• Organisms are adapting to the environment, may be few individuals
• Birth rate barely exceeds death rate
Phase 2 — EXPONENTIAL (LOG) PHASE:
• Population grows rapidly
• Resources are plentiful, little competition
• Birth rate much greater than death rate
• Population doubles in a fixed time period
Phase 3 — STATIONARY (STABLE) PHASE:
• Growth rate slows down as the population reaches the CARRYING CAPACITY of the environment
• Birth rate equals death rate — population size remains stable
• Limiting factors are now in full effect
Phase 4 — DECLINE/DEATH PHASE:
• If resources are severely depleted, population may fall
• Death rate exceeds birth rate
CARRYING CAPACITY: the maximum population size that an environment can sustainably support.
LIMITING FACTORS: factors that prevent further population growth. These include:
• FOOD SUPPLY: less food available as population grows → competition increases → some individuals starve
• COMPETITION: intraspecific competition for food, territory, mates intensifies
• PREDATION: as prey population increases, predator population increases too → more prey eaten
• DISEASE: denser populations spread infectious diseases more easily
• ACCUMULATION OF WASTE: toxic waste products build up
Predator-prey cycles:
• Prey population increases → predator population increases (more food)
• More predators eat more prey → prey population decreases
• Fewer prey → predator population decreases
• Fewer predators → prey population increases again
• This creates oscillating cycles (e.g. lynx and snowshoe hare)
curriculum.activity:
Students are given population data for bacteria in a culture over 24 hours:
Time (h): 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 20, 24
Population: 100, 120, 200, 800, 5000, 18000, 32000, 35000, 35500, 34000, 30000
Plot population (y-axis) vs time (x-axis). Draw a smooth sigmoid curve.
Identify and label: lag phase, exponential phase, stationary phase, decline phase.
Explain what happened at hours 12–14 and 20–24.
curriculum.homework:
1. Describe the four phases of a sigmoid population growth curve.
2. State four limiting factors that prevent a population from growing indefinitely.
3. Explain how predation acts as a limiting factor on a prey population.
4. A rabbit population is growing exponentially. Suggest two abiotic and two biotic factors that could eventually limit its growth.
5. What is meant by the 'carrying capacity' of an ecosystem?
6. Explain why disease spreads more rapidly in a dense population than in a sparse one.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Food production | Deforestation | Pollution and eutrophication
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Increasing Food Production
The human population has grown rapidly. To feed over 8 billion people, food production has been intensified using modern agricultural technology.
Methods used to increase food production:
1. AGRICULTURAL MACHINERY:
• Tractors, combine harvesters → larger areas of land can be cultivated more efficiently
2. CHEMICAL FERTILISERS:
• Provide nitrates, phosphates, potassium ions (NPK)
• Increase plant growth and crop yields
• Risk: can leach into water bodies causing eutrophication
3. PESTICIDES:
• INSECTICIDES: kill insects that damage crops
• HERBICIDES: kill weeds that compete with crops for light, water, minerals
• FUNGICIDES: kill pathogenic fungi
• Risks: pesticides can enter food chains; non-target species harmed; bioaccumulation (concentration increases up food chain)
4. SELECTIVE BREEDING (artificial selection):
• High-yielding varieties of wheat, rice, maize developed
• Disease-resistant crop varieties
• Cattle bred for high milk yield or meat production
5. MONOCULTURES:
• Growing one crop species over a large area
• Efficient and productive
• Risks: disease can spread easily; loss of biodiversity; soil nutrient depletion; heavy pesticide use
6. INTENSIVE FARMING:
• Battery farming of chickens; confined cattle feedlots
• Animals kept indoors at high density, fed controlled diet
• High food conversion efficiency (less energy wasted on movement and heat loss)
• Ethical and welfare concerns; disease risk in crowded conditions
7. IRRIGATION:
• Artificial watering of crops in dry regions
• Allows farming in semi-arid areas
• Risk: waterlogging and soil salinity if not managed
Habitat Destruction and Deforestation
HABITAT DESTRUCTION is the alteration or removal of natural ecosystems, usually for human purposes.
Causes of habitat destruction:
• Conversion of land for farming (agriculture is the leading cause globally)
• Urban development (housing, roads, factories)
• Mining and extraction of natural resources
• Reservoir construction
• Logging (timber extraction)
DEFORESTATION: large-scale removal of forest.
Why forests are cut down:
• Agriculture — land cleared for crops or cattle ranching
• Timber — wood for construction, furniture, fuel
• Charcoal production (fuelwood)
• Roads and infrastructure
Effects of deforestation:
• LOSS OF BIODIVERSITY: habitat destruction leads to species extinction
• SOIL EROSION: tree roots hold soil; without roots, rainwater washes soil away
• FLOODING: trees absorb rainfall; deforestation increases runoff and flood risk
• CARBON DIOXIDE BUILD-UP: fewer trees → less photosynthesis removing CO₂; burning trees releases stored CO₂; enhanced greenhouse effect → climate change
• LOSS OF CLOUD SEEDING: tropical forests generate rainfall through transpiration; deforestation can reduce regional rainfall
• LOSS OF MEDICINAL PLANTS: many undiscovered drugs may come from tropical forest plants
Conversion of tropical rainforest to farmland:
• Tropical forest soils are often nutrient-poor (nutrients are locked in the vegetation, not the soil)
• When forest is cleared and burned, nutrient release is brief
• Without constant recycling by the forest ecosystem, soils quickly become infertile
• Farmland often fails within a few years, requiring more forest to be cleared
Pollution and Eutrophication
POLLUTION: introduction of harmful substances into the environment.
WATER POLLUTION sources:
• Sewage (human waste): introduces bacteria, pathogens, and nutrients
• Agricultural run-off: nitrates and phosphates from fertilisers
• Industrial waste: heavy metals, chemicals, hot water
• Oil spills: coat feathers and fur, cause hypothermia and poisoning
EUTROPHICATION: process by which excess nutrients cause overgrowth of algae, depleting oxygen in water.
Steps of eutrophication:
1. Nitrates and phosphates from fertilisers or sewage leach into rivers/lakes
2. Excess nutrients stimulate rapid growth of algae (ALGAL BLOOM) on the water surface
3. Algae block sunlight reaching underwater plants → plants die
4. When algae and plants die, DECOMPOSERS (bacteria) multiply rapidly
5. Decomposers use large amounts of oxygen in aerobic respiration
6. DISSOLVED OXYGEN levels in water FALL dramatically
7. Fish and other aquatic organisms cannot get enough oxygen → they die (DEOXYGENATION)
8. The water becomes anaerobic and highly polluted
AIR POLLUTION:
• CARBON DIOXIDE from burning fossil fuels → enhanced greenhouse effect
• METHANE from cattle, rice paddies, landfill → potent greenhouse gas
• SULFUR DIOXIDE from burning coal → acid rain → damages forests and aquatic life
• CARBON MONOXIDE from incomplete combustion → toxic to humans
• NITROGEN OXIDES from car engines → acid rain; photochemical smog
Non-biodegradable plastics:
• Persist in ecosystems for hundreds of years
• Entangle and suffocate marine animals
• Microplastics enter food chains, accumulating in organisms
• Toxic chemicals leach into soil and water
curriculum.activity:
Students draw a labelled flowchart diagram showing all 8 steps of eutrophication.
Must include: fertiliser source, nitrate leaching, algal bloom, sunlight blockage, plant death, bacterial decomposition, oxygen depletion, fish death.
Then write a short paragraph (5–6 sentences) explaining how a farmer could reduce the risk of eutrophication on nearby water bodies.
curriculum.homework:
1. Describe the process of eutrophication in a lake, starting with fertiliser application.
2. State three effects of deforestation on the environment.
3. Explain why tropical rainforest soil is often infertile for farming after the forest is cleared.
4. Suggest two methods a farmer could use to improve crop yields without increasing pollution.
5. State two ways in which non-biodegradable plastics harm marine ecosystems.
6. Explain why growing animals for food is less efficient than growing crops for direct human consumption.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Biodiversity | Endangered species | Conservation strategies
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Biodiversity and Conservation
BIODIVERSITY: the variety of living organisms in an area, including the number of different species and the variation within each species.
Why biodiversity is important:
• ECOSYSTEM STABILITY: diverse ecosystems are more resilient to change
• FOOD SECURITY: biodiversity in crop plants provides genetic resources for developing new varieties
• MEDICINE: many drugs are derived from wild plants and animals (e.g. aspirin from willow bark, penicillin from mould, taxol from yew trees)
• ECOLOGICAL SERVICES: clean air, clean water, soil fertility, pollination, pest control
• CULTURAL and AESTHETIC value
Factors causing species to become ENDANGERED or EXTINCT:
• HABITAT DESTRUCTION (most important cause globally): deforestation, drainage of wetlands, urban development
• HUNTING and POACHING: for food, medicine, sport, or the pet trade
• CLIMATE CHANGE: shifts in temperature and rainfall disrupt habitats and migration
• POLLUTION: pesticides, heavy metals, plastics harm species
• INTRODUCTION OF FOREIGN (INVASIVE) SPECIES: these can outcompete or predate native species (e.g. Nile perch introduced to Lake Victoria wiped out hundreds of native fish species)
• DISEASE: novel pathogens can devastate populations with no immunity
A SUSTAINABLE RESOURCE is one that can be removed from the environment without depleting it — if managed carefully, it will continue to be available.
Examples: sustainably managed forests, fisheries with controlled catch limits.
Conservation strategies:
1. PROTECTED AREAS: national parks, nature reserves, marine protected areas — ban or restrict human activity
2. LEGISLATION: CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species) — bans trade in endangered species
3. CAPTIVE BREEDING PROGRAMMES: endangered animals bred in zoos, then reintroduced to the wild
4. SEED BANKS: store seeds from thousands of plant species for future use (e.g. Svalbard Global Seed Vault)
5. EDUCATION and ECOTOURISM: raise public awareness and provide economic incentives for conservation
6. QUOTAS and MONITORING: limit harvesting of fish and trees
7. AI and IVF: Artificial insemination and in vitro fertilisation used in captive breeding to maintain genetic diversity
Risks of small population size:
• INBREEDING: closely related individuals mate → offspring may inherit two copies of harmful recessive alleles → reduced health and fertility
• REDUCED GENETIC VARIATION: less ability to adapt to environmental changes
• DEMOGRAPHIC EFFECTS: small populations more vulnerable to random events (e.g. a single flood could wipe out a small population)
• The minimum viable population size varies by species but is generally estimated at 500–1000 individuals
curriculum.activity:
Research (or use provided fact sheets) on one of these Nigerian conservation efforts:
• Cross River Gorilla conservation (Cross River National Park)
• African elephant conservation in Yankari Game Reserve
• Leatherback sea turtle conservation (Nigerian coast)
Students write a 200-word summary covering:
• Why the species is endangered
• What conservation strategies are in use
• One challenge facing conservationists
curriculum.homework:
1. State three reasons why biodiversity is important to humans.
2. Explain how the introduction of an invasive species can reduce biodiversity.
3. Describe two strategies used to conserve endangered species.
4. What is a seed bank and why is it valuable?
5. Explain why inbreeding is a risk for very small populations.
6. Define 'sustainable resource' and give one example from forestry and one from fishing.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Fermentation | Enzymes in industry | Genetic modification
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Biotechnology and Fermentation
BIOTECHNOLOGY: the use of biological organisms, systems, or processes in manufacturing and service industries.
Fermentation is a key biotechnology process — anaerobic respiration by microorganisms.
Biofuels:
• ETHANOL (alcohol) can be produced by fermenting sugar or grain using YEAST
• Yeast: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ (anaerobic respiration)
• Ethanol can supplement or replace petrol as fuel
• BIODIESEL can be made from plant oils
• Advantages: renewable; lower net CO₂ emissions
• Disadvantages: land needed for crops reduces food production
Bread-making:
• Yeast ferments sugars in dough → CO₂ + ethanol
• CO₂ makes dough rise (trapped in gluten network)
• Ethanol evaporates during baking
• Amylases in flour break down starch to provide sugar for yeast
Yogurt and cheese:
• LACTOBACILLUS bacteria ferment lactose in milk → lactic acid
• Lactic acid lowers pH → causes milk proteins to denature and coagulate → yogurt forms
• Cheese: additional steps with rennet enzymes and ageing
Industrial enzymes:
• PECTINASE: breaks down pectin in fruit cell walls → increases juice yield from fruit
• PROTEASE in biological washing powders: digest protein stains (blood, egg) at low temperature
• LIPASE in washing powders: digest fat stains
• LACTASE: breaks down lactose in milk → produces lactose-free milk for lactose-intolerant people
• AMYLASE: used in food industry to make syrups from starch
Industrial fermenters (bioreactors):
Large-scale production of products like penicillin, insulin, enzymes, mycoprotein.
Conditions controlled in a fermenter:
• TEMPERATURE: ~26°C; cooling water jacket removes heat generated by metabolism
• pH: monitored and adjusted (usually 5–6, slightly acidic)
• OXYGEN: sterile air pumped in and stirred to aerate (for aerobic fermentation)
• NUTRIENTS: supply of sugar, nitrogen, minerals (feedstock)
• STERILITY: essential — contamination by other microorganisms ruins the product
Mycoprotein (Quorn):
• Made from the fungus Fusarium venenatum
• High in protein, low in cholesterol and saturated fat
• Suitable for vegetarian/vegan diets
• Fermented using glucose and salts in large industrial fermenters
Genetic Modification
GENETIC MODIFICATION (GM): changing the genetic material of an organism by removing, changing, or inserting individual genes.
Why bacteria are used in genetic modification:
• They can be grown in large quantities quickly (reproduce every ~20 minutes)
• They have plasmids — small circular pieces of DNA that can be used as VECTORS to carry foreign genes
• They share the same genetic code as all other organisms → can express human genes
• They do not raise significant ethical concerns (compared to using animals)
How genetic modification works (using insulin production as example):
1. RESTRICTION ENZYMES cut the human insulin gene from a human chromosome at specific recognition sites, creating STICKY ENDS
2. The same restriction enzyme cuts open a bacterial PLASMID
3. The human insulin gene is mixed with the cut plasmids
4. The sticky ends of the insulin gene bind to the complementary sticky ends of the plasmid
5. LIGASE ENZYME seals the joins → RECOMBINANT PLASMID is formed
6. Bacteria are stimulated to take up the recombinant plasmids
7. Bacteria containing the recombinant DNA are selected (using antibiotic resistance markers)
8. Selected bacteria are cultured in fermenters → they produce human insulin
9. Insulin is extracted, purified, and used to treat Type 1 diabetes
Advantages of GM insulin:
• Identical to human insulin → fewer allergic reactions than pig/cow insulin
• Can be produced in unlimited quantities
• Acceptable to people with religious restrictions on pig/cow products
GM crops — advantages:
• Pest resistance (e.g. Bt maize with insecticide gene from Bacillus thuringiensis → reduced pesticide use)
• Herbicide resistance → more effective weed control
• Drought resistance → grow in drier conditions
• Golden rice → vitamin A added → prevents blindness in developing countries
• Increased yields → food security
GM crops — disadvantages/concerns:
• Possible gene transfer to wild plants → superweeds
• Insects may develop resistance to Bt toxin
• Effect on non-target species (e.g. bees)
• Unknown long-term health effects on humans
• Large companies controlling crop seed supply → farmer dependency
• Ethical concerns about 'playing God' with nature
curriculum.activity:
Students role-play the steps of inserting the insulin gene into bacteria.
Materials: two types of paper strips (human DNA / plasmid DNA), scissors (restriction enzyme), tape (ligase enzyme)
1. Cut insulin gene from human DNA strip using scissors at marked recognition sites
2. Cut plasmid strip at same sites
3. Mix and pair up sticky ends
4. Tape joins together → recombinant plasmid
5. Fold into circle → represents the modified bacterium
Discuss: What would happen if the wrong restriction enzyme was used?
curriculum.homework:
1. Explain why bacteria are useful in genetic modification.
2. Describe the role of restriction enzymes and ligase in genetic modification.
3. State three advantages of GM crops.
4. Explain how pectinase increases the yield of fruit juice production.
5. Describe the conditions that must be controlled in an industrial fermenter and explain why each is important.
6. Explain why GM insulin is considered better than insulin extracted from pigs or cattle.
TERM 4 — LEARNING OUTCOMES
Describe the structure of DNA and chromosomes and explain the relationship between genes and proteins
Distinguish between mitosis and meiosis and explain their roles in growth, repair, and sexual reproduction
Construct and interpret Punnett squares for monohybrid inheritance including sex linkage
Describe continuous and discontinuous variation with examples, and explain their causes
Explain Darwin's theory of natural selection and apply it to modern examples (antibiotic resistance)
Describe the structure and function of ecosystems, food chains/webs, and energy transfer
Explain the carbon and nitrogen cycles, including the roles of microorganisms
Describe population growth curves and the factors that limit population size
Explain the causes and consequences of eutrophication and deforestation
Describe biodiversity, conservation strategies, and sustainable resource management
Explain biotechnology applications including fermentation, industrial enzymes, and genetic modification
TERM 4 — TEACHER GUIDANCE NOTES
Term Overview
Term 4 is the content-heavy genetics and ecology term. Students often struggle with Punnett squares at first — spend extra time on sex linkage (Week 32) which historically generates many errors. The ecology weeks (35–40) should use real Nigerian examples wherever possible to make content relevant: Lake Chad ecosystem, Cross River forest, Nigerian savanna. Biotechnology (Week 40) tends to generate good discussion.
Key Demonstrations & Activities
Chromosome modelling with clay or pipe cleaners (Week 31)
Coin-toss genetics simulation — very effective for grasping probability (Week 32)
Peppered moth predation simulation — excellent for natural selection (Week 34)
Food web construction using local Nigerian species cards (Week 35)
Nitrogen cycle diagram from memory — use as assessment (Week 36)
Sigmoid curve plotting from bacteria data (Week 37)
Genetic modification role play with paper DNA strips (Week 40)
Assessment Points
End-of-week class quiz on genetics terminology (Weeks 31–33)
Punnett square assessment including sex linkage (Week 33)
Natural selection application test (Week 34)
Food web and ecology short assessment (Week 36)
End-of-term topic test covering all of Term 4 content (Week 40)
Common Misconceptions to Address
Students confuse mitosis and meiosis — emphasise: mitosis = same number of cells (repair/growth); meiosis = half number (gametes)
Students write arrows in food chains in wrong direction — emphasise arrows show energy flow (from eaten to eater)
Students confuse eutrophication mechanism — use the 8-step mnemonic
Students confuse genotype and phenotype — use concrete examples every lesson
TERM 5 — LESSON NOTES

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Cells revisited | Biological molecules | Enzymes and transport
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Cell Biology — Key Concepts Review
CELL STRUCTURE REVISION:
Animal cell: cell membrane, nucleus (contains DNA in chromosomes), cytoplasm, mitochondria, ribosomes
Plant cell: all of the above PLUS cell wall (cellulose), chloroplasts, permanent vacuole, plasmodesmata
Bacterial cell: cell membrane, cell wall (peptidoglycan), circular DNA (no nucleus), ribosomes, sometimes plasmids, sometimes flagella
Cell size: most animal cells 10–30 μm; bacterial cells 1–10 μm; red blood cells ~8 μm (biconcave disc)
KEY CELL PROCESSES:
DIFFUSION: net movement of molecules from high to low concentration, down a concentration gradient. No energy required (passive). Affected by: surface area, concentration gradient, temperature, distance, molecular size.
Examples: CO₂ and O₂ in/out of alveoli; glucose into cells
