OSMOSIS: net movement of WATER molecules from high water potential (dilute solution) to low water potential (concentrated solution), through a PARTIALLY PERMEABLE MEMBRANE. No energy required (passive).
Turgor: plant cells become turgid when water enters by osmosis — maintains support
Plasmolysis: plant cells lose water by osmosis when placed in concentrated solution — cytoplasm shrinks away from cell wall
ACTIVE TRANSPORT: movement of molecules against their concentration gradient (from low to high concentration) using PROTEIN CARRIERS and ENERGY (ATP from respiration).
Example: uptake of nitrate ions by plant roots; uptake of glucose in the small intestine; nerve impulse sodium-potassium pump
SPECIALISED CELLS:
• Red blood cells: no nucleus; biconcave disc; haemoglobin; large surface area
• Sperm cell: tail (flagellum) for movement; acrosome (enzymes to penetrate egg); many mitochondria
• Palisade mesophyll: many chloroplasts; tall columnar shape; stacked near leaf surface for maximum light
• Root hair cell: long extension; large surface area; no chloroplasts; thin wall for water absorption
• Ciliated epithelial cells: cilia beat to move mucus and debris
Biological Molecules and Food Tests — Review
CARBOHYDRATES:
• Monosaccharides: glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆), fructose, galactose — single sugar units
• Disaccharides: sucrose (glucose + fructose), maltose (glucose + glucose), lactose (glucose + galactose)
• Polysaccharides: starch (energy store in plants), glycogen (energy store in animals, liver and muscle), cellulose (cell wall in plants)
• Food test: Benedict's reagent + heat → brick-red precipitate with REDUCING SUGARS (glucose, maltose) but NOT sucrose
• Iodine test: starch → blue-black colour
PROTEINS:
• Made of amino acids joined by peptide bonds
• Functions: enzymes, antibodies, hormones (insulin), structural (collagen, keratin), haemoglobin
• Food test: Biuret reagent → purple/violet colour with proteins (contains copper sulfate + sodium hydroxide)
LIPIDS (fats and oils):
• Made of glycerol + three fatty acids
• Functions: energy storage, insulation, cell membranes, fat-soluble vitamins
• Food test: ethanol emulsion test → white/milky emulsion with lipids
ENZYMES — REVISION:
• Biological catalysts — speed up metabolic reactions
• Made of protein — have a specific 3D ACTIVE SITE
• LOCK AND KEY model: specific substrate fits specific active site
• INDUCED FIT model: active site changes shape slightly to accommodate substrate
• Effect of temperature: enzyme activity increases up to OPTIMUM temperature; above optimum, enzyme DENATURES
• Effect of pH: enzyme activity peaks at optimum pH; extreme pH values denature the enzyme
• Effect of substrate concentration: increases activity up to a maximum (when all active sites are occupied)
• INHIBITORS: competitive (block active site) and non-competitive (change shape of enzyme)
• Examples: amylase (digests starch), lipase (digests fats), protease (digests proteins)
curriculum.activity:
Students carry out or observe demonstrations of food tests:
• Test 3 unknown food substances for glucose, starch, protein, and lipid
• Record results in a table: Food | Benedict's result | Iodine result | Biuret result | Emulsion test result
• Identify each food substance based on results
Then: 5-minute timed quiz — students write as many functions of enzymes and examples as they can remember.
curriculum.homework:
1. Compare the structure of an animal cell and a bacterial cell.
2. Define osmosis and explain what happens to a plant cell placed in a concentrated salt solution.
3. Describe the Benedict's test for reducing sugars, including the colour change.
4. Explain why enzymes are specific to their substrates.
5. Describe how temperature affects enzyme activity.
6. State the difference between active transport and diffusion.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Human nutrition | Digestive system | Plant and human transport
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Nutrition and Digestion — Review
BALANCED DIET — Seven components:
1. Carbohydrates: energy (4 kcal/g)
2. Proteins: growth and repair (4 kcal/g)
3. Fats/lipids: energy storage, insulation (9 kcal/g)
4. Vitamins: metabolic regulation (small quantities)
5. Minerals: structural and metabolic functions
6. Water: solvent, transport medium, temperature regulation
7. Dietary fibre (roughage): peristalsis, prevents constipation
Deficiency diseases (key examples):
• Scurvy: vitamin C deficiency → bleeding gums, skin lesions, poor wound healing
• Rickets: vitamin D deficiency → soft/deformed bones (poor calcium absorption)
• Anaemia: iron deficiency → reduced haemoglobin → poor O₂ transport, fatigue
• Kwashiorkor: severe protein deficiency → oedema (swollen abdomen), stunted growth, hair/skin changes
• Night blindness: vitamin A deficiency → inability to see in dim light
DIGESTIVE SYSTEM:
Mouth → oesophagus → stomach → small intestine (duodenum, ileum) → large intestine → rectum → anus
• SALIVARY GLANDS: produce amylase (starch → maltose)
• STOMACH: HCl (kills bacteria, activates pepsin); pepsin (protease; protein → peptides); churning
• PANCREAS: produces pancreatic juice (amylase, lipase, protease) into duodenum; also produces insulin/glucagon
• LIVER: produces BILE (stored in gallbladder); bile emulsifies fats (increases surface area for lipase)
• SMALL INTESTINE (ILEUM): final digestion; ABSORPTION of digested nutrients into blood via villi/microvilli
• LARGE INTESTINE: reabsorption of water; formation of faeces
Villi structure for efficient absorption:
• Finger-like projections — increase surface area enormously
• Thin wall (one cell thick) — short diffusion distance
• Rich blood supply — maintains concentration gradient
• Lacteals — absorb fatty acids and glycerol into lymph system
Transport Systems — Review
TRANSPORT IN PLANTS:
XYLEM: transports water and dissolved minerals from roots to leaves
• Direction: upward (roots → stem → leaves)
• Driving force: TRANSPIRATION PULL (evaporation of water from leaves)
• One-way (no energy required from plant)
• Dead cells with lignified walls — form continuous tubes
Transpiration factors:
• Light intensity: stomata open more in light → more transpiration
• Temperature: increases evaporation → more transpiration
• Wind: removes water vapour from leaf surface → increases transpiration
• Humidity: high humidity slows transpiration (reduces water potential gradient)
PHLOEM: transports dissolved sugars (sucrose) and other organic molecules from leaves (source) to all parts of plant (sink)
• Direction: up AND down (bidirectional)
• Requires energy (ATP)
• TRANSLOCATION: the process of transport in phloem
• Living sieve tube cells + companion cells
CIRCULATION IN HUMANS:
DOUBLE CIRCULATION:
• Pulmonary circulation: heart → lungs → heart (blood picks up O₂, loses CO₂)
• Systemic circulation: heart → body → heart (blood delivers O₂ to tissues)
HEART: 4 chambers (right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle)
• Right side: pumps deoxygenated blood to lungs
• Left side: pumps oxygenated blood to body
• Valves: prevent backflow (tricuspid, bicuspid/mitral, semi-lunar)
• Atrioventricular (AV) node: coordinates heartbeat
BLOOD COMPONENTS:
• PLASMA: liquid; transports CO₂, urea, hormones, glucose, amino acids, heat
• RED BLOOD CELLS: no nucleus; haemoglobin; transport O₂; biconcave disc
• WHITE BLOOD CELLS: lymphocytes (antibodies), phagocytes (engulf pathogens)
• PLATELETS: cell fragments; blood clotting
curriculum.activity:
Students create a detailed flow chart of the journey of a cheese sandwich through the digestive system.
For each region (mouth, stomach, small intestine, large intestine), state:
• Which nutrients are digested here
• Which enzyme(s) act here
• What products are formed
• What is absorbed here
Must include: salivary amylase, pepsin, pancreatic lipase, bile, absorption in ileum, water in large intestine.
curriculum.homework:
1. State the functions of bile in digestion.
2. Describe three features of villi that make them well-adapted for absorption.
3. Compare the functions of xylem and phloem.
4. Explain why the left ventricle wall is thicker than the right ventricle wall.
5. State the deficiency disease caused by lack of vitamin C and describe two symptoms.
6. What is translocation? State which tissue carries out this process.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Respiration | Excretion | Homeostasis and negative feedback
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Respiration and Gas Exchange — Review
AEROBIC RESPIRATION:
C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP
• Occurs in MITOCHONDRIA
• Produces 36–38 ATP molecules per glucose
• Used for: muscle contraction, active transport, synthesis of molecules, cell division, nerve impulses
ANAEROBIC RESPIRATION (humans):
C₆H₁₂O₆ → lactic acid + small amount of ATP
• Occurs in CYTOPLASM
• Used during intense exercise when O₂ supply is insufficient
• Lactic acid causes muscle fatigue and pain
• OXYGEN DEBT: O₂ needed after exercise to oxidise lactic acid
ANAEROBIC RESPIRATION (yeast):
C₆H₁₂O₆ → ethanol + CO₂ + small amount of ATP
• FERMENTATION: used in bread-making and alcohol production
GAS EXCHANGE IN HUMANS — Adaptations of alveoli:
• Large number of alveoli → enormous total surface area (~70 m²)
• One-cell-thick walls → short diffusion distance
• Moist walls → gases dissolve
• Dense capillary network → maintains concentration gradient
• Breathing movements → constantly renew air
SMOKING effects on lungs:
• Tar: coats alveoli; causes lung cancer; stimulates excess mucus
• Nicotine: addictive; constricts arteries; increases heart rate and blood pressure
• Carbon monoxide: combines irreversibly with haemoglobin; reduces O₂ carrying capacity
• Carcinogens: cause mutations in lung cell DNA → lung cancer
• COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease): bronchitis + emphysema
EXCRETION: removal of metabolic waste products from the body
• CO₂: produced by respiration → removed via lungs
• UREA: produced by DEAMINATION of excess amino acids in the liver (amino group removed → ammonia → urea + CO₂). Urea is removed by the kidneys.
KIDNEY FUNCTION:
1. ULTRAFILTRATION: high blood pressure forces water, glucose, ions, urea, amino acids from blood into Bowman's capsule (glomerular filtrate). Large molecules (proteins, cells) remain in blood.
2. SELECTIVE REABSORPTION: useful molecules reabsorbed from filtrate back into blood:
• ALL glucose: reabsorbed in proximal convoluted tubule by active transport
• Most water: reabsorbed by osmosis throughout tubule and collecting duct
• Some mineral ions: reabsorbed by active transport
• Urea: small amount reabsorbed passively
3. URINE: concentrated solution of urea, excess salts, and water → passes to ureter → bladder → excreted via urethra
ADH (Anti-Diuretic Hormone):
• Released by pituitary gland when blood water concentration is LOW
• ADH makes collecting duct walls MORE PERMEABLE to water
• More water reabsorbed → concentrated urine
• When well-hydrated: low ADH → dilute, large volume urine
Homeostasis and Hormones — Review
HOMEOSTASIS: maintaining a stable internal environment despite changes in external conditions.
NEGATIVE FEEDBACK: the mechanism by which homeostasis is maintained.
• A change in a condition is DETECTED
• A corrective response REVERSES the change (brings it back to the set point)
• This is negative because the response opposes the original change
BLOOD GLUCOSE REGULATION:
Normal blood glucose: ~80–120 mg/100 cm³
When blood glucose RISES (after eating):
• Detected by ISLETS OF LANGERHANS in pancreas
• B cells secrete INSULIN
• Insulin: causes liver and muscle cells to absorb glucose and convert it to GLYCOGEN (glycogenesis)
• Blood glucose returns to normal → insulin secretion stops
When blood glucose FALLS (between meals/during exercise):
• Detected by islets of Langerhans
• A cells secrete GLUCAGON
• Glucagon: causes liver cells to break down glycogen back to glucose (glycogenolysis) and release it into blood
• Blood glucose returns to normal → glucagon secretion stops
DIABETES MELLITUS:
Type 1: immune system destroys pancreatic B cells → no insulin produced → blood glucose uncontrolled
Treatment: daily insulin injections + carbohydrate monitoring
Type 2: body cells become resistant to insulin (often linked to obesity and diet)
Treatment: dietary changes, exercise, oral medication, possibly insulin
TEMPERATURE REGULATION (THERMOREGULATION):
Body temperature maintained at ~37°C — optimum for enzyme activity
When body TOO HOT:
• Skin blood vessels VASODILATE: more blood near surface → more heat lost by radiation
• SWEATING increases: evaporation of sweat cools skin
• Hairs FLATTEN (erector muscles relax): less insulating air layer
When body TOO COLD:
• Skin blood vessels VASOCONSTRICT: less blood near surface → less heat lost
• SWEATING stops
• Hairs ERECT (erector muscles contract): traps insulating air layer
• SHIVERING: rapid muscle contractions generate heat from respiration
curriculum.activity:
Students draw a concept map linking: blood glucose, insulin, glucagon, glycogen, pancreas, liver, negative feedback, thermoregulation, hypothalamus, vasodilation, vasoconstriction, sweating, shivering.
Using arrows showing relationships. Add brief label to each arrow (e.g. 'secretes', 'converts', 'detects').
Then answer: What would happen to blood glucose levels if a person's pancreas could no longer produce glucagon?
curriculum.homework:
1. Write the word equation for aerobic respiration.
2. Explain why anaerobic respiration produces less ATP than aerobic respiration.
3. Describe the two stages of kidney function (ultrafiltration and selective reabsorption).
4. Explain the role of ADH in regulating blood water concentration.
5. Describe what happens in the body when blood glucose concentration rises above normal.
6. Explain how the skin helps to lower body temperature when it is too hot.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Nervous system | Hormones | Plant responses
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Nervous System and Sense Organs — Review
NERVOUS SYSTEM STRUCTURE:
• CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM (CNS): brain + spinal cord
• PERIPHERAL NERVOUS SYSTEM (PNS): all nerves outside the CNS
NEURONES:
• SENSORY neurone: carries impulses FROM sense organs TO spinal cord/brain
• RELAY (CONNECTOR) neurone: within CNS; connects sensory and motor neurones
• MOTOR neurone: carries impulses FROM brain/spinal cord TO effectors (muscles/glands)
SYNAPSE:
• Junction between two neurones
• Impulse arrives at presynaptic membrane → releases NEUROTRANSMITTER (e.g. acetylcholine) into synaptic cleft
• Neurotransmitter diffuses across gap → binds to receptors on postsynaptic membrane → triggers new impulse
• Neurotransmitter broken down by enzymes (acetylcholinesterase) to prevent continuous stimulation
REFLEX ARC (spinal reflex):
→ Stimulus → receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone (in spinal cord) → motor neurone → effector (muscle) → response
• Bypasses brain — fast, automatic, protective
• Example: touching a hot object → hand withdraws
THE EYE:
• Cornea: refracts (bends) light
• Iris: controls pupil size (regulates light entering eye)
• Pupil: aperture that allows light in
• Lens: further refracts light; changes shape for ACCOMMODATION (focusing)
• Retina: contains RODS (black and white, dim light) and CONES (colour, bright light)
• Fovea (yellow spot): area of most acute vision; highest density of cones
• Blind spot: no rods or cones — where optic nerve leaves retina
• Optic nerve: carries impulses to brain
ACCOMMODATION:
• Looking at near object: ciliary muscles CONTRACT → suspensory ligaments RELAX → lens becomes FATTER (more curved) → more refraction
• Looking at far object: ciliary muscles RELAX → suspensory ligaments TAUT → lens becomes THINNER (less curved) → less refraction
PUPIL REFLEX:
• Bright light → circular muscles of iris CONTRACT → pupil CONSTRICTS (less light enters)
• Dim light → radial muscles CONTRACT → pupil DILATES (more light enters)
Hormones and Plant Responses — Review
ENDOCRINE SYSTEM: network of glands that secrete hormones directly into the blood.
Key hormones:
• ADRENALINE (adrenal glands): fight-or-flight response; increases heart rate, blood pressure, blood glucose; dilates pupils; diverts blood to muscles
• INSULIN (pancreas B cells): lowers blood glucose; promotes glycogenesis
• GLUCAGON (pancreas A cells): raises blood glucose; promotes glycogenolysis
• FSH (pituitary): stimulates follicle development and oestrogen production in ovary
• LH (pituitary): triggers ovulation
• OESTROGEN (ovary): thickens uterine lining; inhibits FSH; stimulates LH surge
• PROGESTERONE (corpus luteum): maintains uterine lining during pregnancy
• TESTOSTERONE (testes): male secondary sexual characteristics; sperm production
• OXYTOCIN (pituitary): stimulates uterine contractions during labour; milk ejection
Comparing nervous and hormonal coordination:
Nervous system: fast (milliseconds); electrical; short-lived; specific target (nerve)
Hormonal system: slower (seconds to hours); chemical; longer lasting; widespread (blood)
PLANT RESPONSES (TROPISMS):
• AUXIN: plant hormone produced in shoot tips; promotes cell elongation
PHOTOTROPISM (response to light):
• Light from one side: auxin moves to SHADED side of shoot
• Higher auxin on shaded side → cells elongate more on that side → shoot BENDS TOWARD light
• Positive phototropism (shoots bend toward light) — benefits: maximises photosynthesis
GRAVITROPISM (response to gravity):
• Roots are POSITIVELY geotropic (grow toward gravity)
• Shoots are NEGATIVELY geotropic (grow away from gravity)
• In roots: high auxin concentration INHIBITS growth → root curves downward toward gravity
DRUGS AND SYNAPSES:
• Stimulants (caffeine, amphetamines): increase neurotransmitter release → more impulses
• Depressants (alcohol): reduce neurotransmitter effect → slower nerve transmission
• HEROIN/OPIOIDS: bind to opioid receptors → block pain signals; highly addictive
• ALCOHOL: depressant; reduces reaction time; impairs coordination; liver damage (cirrhosis); foetal alcohol syndrome
curriculum.activity:
Students draw complete labelled diagrams of:
1. A reflex arc (knee-jerk or hot-object reflex) — showing all 5 components
2. A synapse — showing presynaptic membrane, neurotransmitter vesicles, synaptic cleft, postsynaptic receptors
3. The eye — all major structures labelled
Then complete a comparison table: Nervous system vs Hormonal system — speed, type of signal, duration, target organs, examples.
curriculum.homework:
1. Describe the sequence of events in a spinal reflex arc.
2. Explain how the eye adjusts to focus on a nearby object (accommodation).
3. State two differences between the nervous system and the hormonal system.
4. Explain how auxin causes a shoot to grow toward light.
5. Describe the effects of alcohol on the nervous system.
6. State the roles of FSH and LH in the menstrual cycle.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Asexual and sexual reproduction | Human reproduction | Growth and development
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Reproduction — Types and Comparison
ASEXUAL REPRODUCTION:
• Involves ONLY ONE parent
• Offspring are genetically IDENTICAL to parent (clones)
• No fusion of gametes
• Fast and efficient — no need to find a mate
• Examples:
— Binary fission in bacteria: one cell divides into two identical cells
— Budding in yeast: outgrowth (bud) forms and detaches
— Vegetative propagation in plants: runners (strawberry), bulbs (onion), cuttings
— Asexual spore production in fungi and ferns
Advantages of asexual reproduction:
• Fast (no time finding a mate)
• All offspring can reproduce (no non-reproductive individuals)
• Ideal environment: all offspring are identically adapted
Disadvantages:
• No genetic variation → if environment changes, all individuals equally vulnerable
• Harmful mutations spread to all offspring
SEXUAL REPRODUCTION:
• Involves TWO parents (usually)
• Fusion of GAMETES (haploid sex cells) — FERTILISATION → diploid ZYGOTE
• Offspring are genetically DIFFERENT from parents (and each other)
• Produces genetic variation → important for evolution and natural selection
SEXUAL REPRODUCTION IN FLOWERING PLANTS:
• POLLINATION: transfer of pollen from anther to stigma
— INSECT pollination: large, coloured, scented flowers; sticky/spiky pollen; nectar
— WIND pollination: small, dull flowers; large feathery stigmas; light, smooth pollen produced in large quantities
• FERTILISATION: pollen tube grows from stigma down style to ovary; male nucleus fuses with female nucleus (in ovule) → ZYGOTE → SEED
• SEED DISPERSAL: wind (sycamore), animals (burrs), water, explosive mechanisms
• GERMINATION: seed takes in water → testa softens → embryo grows using stored food in seed leaves (cotyledons)
Human Reproduction — Review
MALE REPRODUCTIVE SYSTEM:
• Testes: produce sperm (spermatogenesis) and testosterone
• Epididymis: sperm stored and mature
• Vas deferens: transports sperm to urethra
• Prostate gland + seminal vesicles: produce fluid (semen)
• Penis: deposits semen into vagina during intercourse
FEMALE REPRODUCTIVE SYSTEM:
• Ovaries: produce eggs (oogenesis) and oestrogen/progesterone
• Fallopian tube (oviduct): transports egg from ovary to uterus; site of FERTILISATION
• Uterus: site of implantation and fetal development
• Cervix: lower end of uterus; dilates during birth
• Vagina: birth canal; receives penis during intercourse
MENSTRUAL CYCLE (28 days):
• Days 1–5: MENSTRUATION — uterine lining shed
• Days 6–13: FSH from pituitary → follicle develops in ovary → follicle produces OESTROGEN → oestrogen thickens uterine lining
• Day 14: LH SURGE from pituitary → OVULATION (egg released from follicle)
• Days 15–28: follicle becomes CORPUS LUTEUM → secretes PROGESTERONE → maintains uterine lining
• If no fertilisation: corpus luteum degenerates → progesterone drops → lining sheds → cycle begins again
PREGNANCY:
• Fertilised egg → zygote → divides (mitosis) → blastocyst → implants in uterine wall
• PLACENTA develops: exchanges O₂, glucose, CO₂, urea, hormones between mother and fetus
• Umbilical cord connects fetus to placenta
• Amniotic fluid: protects fetus from shock; allows movement
• HCG (human chorionic gonadotropin): maintains corpus luteum → pregnancy maintained
GROWTH: increase in dry mass of an organism
• Increase in NUMBER of cells (mitosis)
• Increase in SIZE of cells
• Cell DIFFERENTIATION: cells become specialised
• In humans: growth rapid in infancy and adolescence (puberty); slows in adulthood
curriculum.activity:
Students are given graphs showing hormone levels (FSH, LH, oestrogen, progesterone) and uterine lining thickness over a 28-day cycle.
Questions to answer:
1. On which day does ovulation occur? How can you tell?
2. Which hormone(s) cause the uterine lining to thicken?
3. What would happen to progesterone levels if the egg was fertilised?
4. Why do oestrogen levels fall after day 14?
5. Describe what happens to the uterine lining between days 20–28 and explain why.
curriculum.homework:
1. State three differences between sexual and asexual reproduction.
2. Describe how insect-pollinated flowers are adapted for pollination.
3. Explain the roles of FSH and LH in the menstrual cycle.
4. Describe the functions of the placenta during pregnancy.
5. Explain what happens to the corpus luteum if fertilisation does not occur.
6. State two advantages and two disadvantages of asexual reproduction.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Monohybrid crosses | Sex linkage | Variation and mutation
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Genetics — Comprehensive Review
KEY GENETIC TERMS:
• GENE: a section of DNA that codes for a specific protein; controls a particular characteristic
• ALLELE: one of two or more alternative forms of a gene
• DOMINANT: allele expressed in phenotype even when only one copy present
• RECESSIVE: allele only expressed when two copies present (homozygous)
• CODOMINANT: both alleles expressed equally in the heterozygote
• HOMOZYGOUS: both alleles identical (BB or bb)
• HETEROZYGOUS: alleles different (Bb)
• GENOTYPE: genetic make-up (the alleles present)
• PHENOTYPE: observable characteristics
• LOCUS: the position of a gene on a chromosome
Solving genetics problems — Method:
1. Assign letters (capital for dominant, lower case for recessive)
2. Write parental genotypes
3. Write gametes (each gamete contains ONE allele from each pair)
4. Complete the Punnett square
5. Write genotype ratio and phenotype ratio of offspring
MONOHYBRID CROSS EXAMPLES:
Example 1: Tongue rolling (T = can roll, t = cannot roll)
Parents: Tt × Tt
Gametes: T, t   ×   T, t
Punnett square:
T    t
T: TT   Tt
t: Tt   tt
Genotype ratio: 1 TT : 2 Tt : 1 tt
Phenotype ratio: 3 can roll : 1 cannot roll
Example 2: Sickle cell anaemia (HbA = normal, HbS = sickle)
Carrier × Carrier: HbA HbS × HbA HbS
Offspring:
• 1 HbA HbA: normal
• 2 HbA HbS: carrier (sickle cell trait — some protection against malaria)
• 1 HbS HbS: sickle cell anaemia
Note: HbA and HbS are CODOMINANT — carriers have mild symptoms and some resistance to malaria
SEX LINKAGE REVISION:
X-linked recessive conditions: haemophilia, colour blindness, Duchenne muscular dystrophy
• Carrier mother × Normal father:
X^H X^h × X^H Y
• Offspring:
X^H X^H: normal female
X^H X^h: carrier female
X^H Y: normal male
X^h Y: affected male
• Probability of affected son = 50% of sons = 25% of all children
PEDIGREE ANALYSIS:
• If condition skips generations → likely RECESSIVE
• If condition affects 50% of individuals in every generation → likely DOMINANT
• If condition affects males more than females → likely X-LINKED
• If all children of an affected father are affected daughters → autosomal dominant
curriculum.activity:
Students solve a set of 6 genetics problems:
1. Monohybrid dominant/recessive (hair colour in cats)
2. Monohybrid with codominance (AB blood type × O)
3. Sex determination cross
4. X-linked colour blindness — carrier mother × normal father
5. Pedigree analysis — determine if condition is dominant or recessive, and if X-linked
6. Back-cross problem: determine unknown genotype from offspring ratio
Students must show: letter assignment, parental genotypes, gametes, Punnett square, and phenotype ratio for each.
curriculum.homework:
1. In guinea pigs, black fur (B) is dominant over white fur (b). Cross two heterozygous black guinea pigs. Show your Punnett square and state the expected phenotype ratio.
2. Explain why sex-linked conditions are more common in males than females.
3. A woman is a carrier for colour blindness (X^B X^b). Her husband has normal colour vision (X^B Y). What is the probability that their daughter will be colour blind? Show your working.
4. Define 'mutation' and state two environmental factors that increase mutation rate.
5. What is meant by codominance? Give one example.
6. State the genotypes of: (a) a homozygous dominant individual, (b) a carrier of a recessive allele, (c) an affected individual for a recessive condition.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Ecosystems | Human impact | Conservation
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Ecology — Comprehensive Review
ENERGY FLOW SUMMARY:
• Energy enters ecosystem as LIGHT (photosynthesis)
• 1% of solar energy captured by plants
• ~10% transferred between each trophic level
• Energy LOST at each level via: respiration, heat, faeces, non-eaten parts
• Food chains rarely exceed 5 trophic levels
ECOLOGICAL PYRAMIDS:
• Pyramid of numbers: number of organisms per level — can be inverted (parasites)
• Pyramid of biomass: dry mass per level — always pyramid-shaped
• Pyramid of energy: energy per level per time — always pyramid-shaped; most accurate
NUTRIENT CYCLES — KEY PROCESSES:
Carbon cycle: photosynthesis (removes CO₂), respiration (adds CO₂), combustion (adds CO₂), decomposition (adds CO₂)
Nitrogen cycle: nitrogen fixation (bacteria; lightning), nitrification (nitrifying bacteria), decomposition (ammonium), denitrification (denitrifying bacteria)
POPULATION GROWTH:
Sigmoid curve phases: lag → exponential → stationary → decline
Limiting factors: food, competition, predation, disease, space, water
HUMAN IMPACTS:
• Deforestation: biodiversity loss; soil erosion; flooding; CO₂ increase; reduced rainfall
• Eutrophication: nitrate leaching → algal bloom → decomposers → O₂ depletion → fish death
• Non-biodegradable plastics: persist for centuries; harm marine life; microplastics in food chains
• Greenhouse effect: CO₂ and methane build-up → global warming → climate change
• Overhunting: threatens prey species; unbalances food webs
• Introduced species: outcompete or predate native species → biodiversity loss
CONSERVATION STRATEGIES:
• Protected areas (national parks, marine reserves)
• CITES (trade restrictions)
• Captive breeding and reintroduction
• Seed banks
• Quotas on fishing and logging
• Education and ecotourism
• AI and IVF for captive breeding programs
Risks of declining population: inbreeding; reduced genetic variation; demographic vulnerability
SUSTAINABLE RESOURCES: managed so they are not depleted — sustainable fisheries, sustainable forestry
curriculum.activity:
Students plan and write a 400-word essay answering:
"Discuss the ways in which human activities have damaged ecosystems and outline conservation strategies to address this damage."
Plan must include:
• Introduction: definition of ecosystem and why they matter
• Body paragraph 1: deforestation effects
• Body paragraph 2: pollution (eutrophication, plastics, greenhouse gases)
• Body paragraph 3: conservation strategies
• Conclusion: sustainable development
Peer mark each other's essays using the mark scheme.
curriculum.homework:
1. Explain why pyramids of energy are the most useful type of ecological pyramid.
2. Outline the process of eutrophication, including the final effect on fish.
3. State three strategies used to conserve endangered species.
4. Explain how deforestation increases atmospheric CO₂ levels.
5. Describe two risks for a species whose population falls to a very small number.
6. Why is the nitrogen cycle important for plant growth?

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.topic: Exam Skills: Paper 1 Multiple Choice Strategies
curriculum.subtopics_raw: MCQ techniques | Biology data interpretation | Common errors
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Cambridge IGCSE Biology Paper Structure
PAPER 1 — MULTIPLE CHOICE:
• 40 questions; 40 marks; 45 minutes
• Each question has 4 options (A, B, C, D)
• No negative marking — always answer every question
• Tests: knowledge, understanding, and application across the whole syllabus
PAPER 2 — CORE (Extended students also take Paper 4):
• Short answer and structured questions
• Tests data handling, experimental analysis, and extended writing
• 1 hour 15 minutes (Paper 2) or 1 hour 15 minutes (Paper 4)
PAPER 3 — ALTERNATIVE TO PRACTICAL:
• Tests practical skills without doing the experiment
• Drawing tables, labelling diagrams, interpreting results, identifying variables
MCQ STRATEGIES:
1. Read ALL four options before choosing — the BEST answer may not be the first one that looks correct
2. Eliminate obviously wrong options first
3. Watch for ABSOLUTE words: "always", "never", "all", "none" — these are often incorrect
4. Double negatives: "which is NOT incorrect" = which is correct
5. Biology command words in stems:
— "State" = one-word or brief factual answer
— "Describe" = give the main features
— "Explain" = give reasons/mechanisms
— "Suggest" = use your knowledge in an unfamiliar situation
— "Compare" = similarities AND differences
— "Calculate" = show working
6. Time management: 40 questions in 45 minutes = ~1 minute each. Skip difficult ones, return later.
7. Check your answer sheet — make sure each answer is in the correct box
COMMON ERRORS IN IGCSE BIOLOGY:
• Confusing respiration with breathing
• Confusing diffusion with osmosis (osmosis = WATER only through partially permeable membrane)
• Forgetting that photosynthesis uses CO₂ (not produces it)
• Misidentifying the roles of the left and right sides of the heart
• Saying arteries carry oxygenated blood — WRONG for pulmonary artery (carries deoxygenated blood to lungs)
• Confusing mitosis and meiosis
• Forgetting that sex is determined by the father (Y chromosome)
• Confusing genotype and phenotype
Data Interpretation Skills
DATA SKILLS needed for Paper 2/4:
TYPES OF GRAPHS used in biology:
• LINE GRAPH: shows change over time or continuous variable (e.g. enzyme activity vs temperature)
• BAR CHART: compares discrete/categorical data (e.g. blood groups)
• HISTOGRAM: continuous data grouped into classes (e.g. height distribution)
• SCATTER GRAPH: relationship between two variables
• KITE DIAGRAM: shows distribution of species along a transect
CHOOSING WHICH GRAPH:
• Both variables continuous → line graph
• One variable categorical → bar chart
• Distribution of continuous data → histogram
READING GRAPHS — key skills:
• Describe the shape: increasing, decreasing, constant, bell-shaped, sigmoid, etc.
• State values: "between 10°C and 30°C, rate of photosynthesis increases from 2 to 8 cm³/min"
• Identify the maximum/minimum/optimum
• Calculate the gradient (rate of change): change in y ÷ change in x
• Identify the plateau (where the graph levels off) and explain why
CALCULATIONS frequently required:
• Percentage change: (new value − original value) ÷ original value × 100
• Mean: sum of values ÷ number of values
• Magnification: image size = actual size × magnification
• Surface area to volume ratio: SA:V ratio
EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN questions:
• Independent variable: the variable DELIBERATELY changed
• Dependent variable: the variable MEASURED (the result)
• Controlled variables: all other variables kept constant
• Control experiment: identical to test experiment but without the independent variable (baseline comparison)
• Validity: experiment measures what it sets out to measure
• Reliability: results are repeatable (consistent)
• Hypothesis: a testable prediction
curriculum.activity:
Students complete 20 MCQ from a past Cambridge IGCSE Biology Paper 1 under timed conditions (22 minutes).
After completing:
1. Self-mark using answer key
2. For every incorrect answer, write: (a) why they chose the wrong answer, (b) what the correct answer is and why
3. Group discussion: which topics generated the most errors?
4. Create a personal 'weak areas list' for targeted revision
curriculum.homework:
1. What is the difference between 'describe' and 'explain' in an exam question?
2. The rate of photosynthesis was measured at different light intensities. Describe what shape of graph you would expect and explain why the graph levels off at high light intensities.
3. A student is testing the effect of amylase concentration on the digestion of starch. State the independent variable, dependent variable, and two controlled variables.
4. Calculate the percentage increase in heart rate from 72 bpm at rest to 120 bpm during exercise.
5. Explain why there is no negative marking on a multiple-choice paper, and describe what strategy this should encourage.
6. State one common error students make when answering questions about osmosis, and correct it.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.topic: Exam Skills: Extended Writing and Practical Questions
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Structured questions | Practical interpretation | Mark scheme technique
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Answering Structured Questions
STRUCTURED QUESTIONS (Paper 2 / Paper 4):
These follow a pattern: short (1-2 mark) parts build to longer (4-6 mark) extended writing.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Always read the MARK ALLOCATION before answering. A 1-mark question needs 1 point; a 6-mark question needs 6 separate points.
STATE questions [1 mark each]:
• One clear, specific point
• No explanation needed
• Example: "State two products of aerobic respiration." → "Carbon dioxide" AND "Water" (two separate lines)
DESCRIBE questions [1 mark per valid feature]:
• List the main features/steps in order
• Include data from graphs where given
• Do not use vague words like "increases a bit" — be quantitative if data is given
EXPLAIN questions [often 2 marks per point = 1 mark for what, 1 mark for why]:
• Give the process/mechanism AND the reason/consequence
• Example: "Explain why the rate of photosynthesis increases with light intensity up to a point."
— "Light provides energy for the light-dependent reactions" [what]
— "More light → more energy available → faster splitting of water / more ATP produced" [why]
— "Eventually another factor (CO₂, temperature) becomes limiting" [why it levels off]
COMPARE questions: must give BOTH sides
• "Compare mitosis and meiosis" → state feature of mitosis AND corresponding feature of meiosis
CALCULATE questions: ALWAYS show working — partial marks available
SUGGEST questions: apply biology knowledge to an unfamiliar situation — marks awarded for any reasonable, scientific answer
DRAW/LABEL questions:
• Use a ruler for straight lines in diagrams
• Label lines must touch the structure clearly
• Draw a complete diagram — do not leave out parts
• Show magnification if asked: magnification = image size ÷ actual size
Practical Skills Questions (Paper 3)
PAPER 3 — ALTERNATIVE TO PRACTICAL:
This paper tests skills you would develop in practical lessons:
1. DRAWING TABLES:
• Heading row with units in brackets: "Temperature / °C"
• Independent variable in first column
• Dependent variable(s) in subsequent columns
• Borders on all cells
• No units in data cells — only in headings
2. LABELLING DIAGRAMS:
• Arrow lines must clearly touch the named structure
• Write labels horizontally where possible
• Spell biological terms correctly (mark lost for misspelling)
3. INTERPRETING RESULTS:
• Describe the TREND shown in the data
• Use figures: "as temperature increased from 10°C to 40°C, rate doubled from 2 to 4 cm³/min"
• Explain the trend using biology
• Identify any anomalous results
4. IDENTIFYING VARIABLES:
• Independent: the variable YOU changed (the test variable)
• Dependent: the variable YOU measured (the outcome)
• Controlled: all other variables kept CONSTANT (to ensure a fair test)
5. SUGGESTING IMPROVEMENTS:
• Use more replicates (repeat experiments) to improve reliability
• Use wider range of values for independent variable
• Control temperature more precisely (water bath)
• Use a colorimeter instead of subjective colour assessment
• Use a gas syringe instead of counting bubbles
6. CALCULATING VALUES:
• Rates: cm³/minute; number of bubbles/minute
• Mean: add all values ÷ number of values
• Exclude obvious anomalous results from mean
• Show all working
7. DRAWING GRAPHS:
• Choose appropriate axes — independent on x-axis, dependent on y-axis
• Label both axes with units
• Use most of the graph paper — scale appropriately
• Plot points accurately as small crosses (×)
• Draw the best-fit line or smooth curve (NOT necessarily through every point)
• Do NOT extrapolate beyond data unless asked
curriculum.activity:
Students are given a dataset from a photosynthesis experiment:
Light intensity (lux): 0, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1200
Bubbles per minute: 0, 6, 12, 18, 22, 24, 24
Task: (a) Draw a table of results, (b) Plot a line graph with labelled axes, (c) Describe the pattern in the results, (d) Explain why the rate levels off above 1000 lux, (e) State two controlled variables in this experiment, (f) Suggest how reliability could be improved.
Students mark each other's work using a teacher-provided mark scheme.
curriculum.homework:
1. A student measures the heart rate of 5 people at rest: 68, 72, 75, 68, 70 bpm. Calculate the mean heart rate.
2. Describe how you would set up a fair test to investigate the effect of temperature on amylase activity using starch and iodine.
3. A graph shows enzyme activity increasing from pH 4 to pH 7, then decreasing sharply from pH 7 to pH 10. Describe this graph using data, and explain the decrease above pH 7.
4. State the difference between a valid experiment and a reliable experiment.
5. Why must label lines in a biological diagram touch the exact structure being labelled?
6. Calculate the magnification of a diagram if the image of a cell is 40 mm and the actual cell is 0.02 mm.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Full mock exam | Marking and feedback | Revision planning
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Full Mock Examination — Preparation and Review
This week is dedicated to a full Cambridge IGCSE Biology mock examination under exam conditions, followed by marking, targeted feedback, and creation of personal revision plans.
BEFORE THE EXAM — Preparation checklist:
✓ Revise all 6 terms of content (biology Weeks 1–49)
✓ Review your weak areas list from Week 48
✓ Re-read definitions for key terms: diffusion, osmosis, active transport, enzyme, allele, dominant, recessive, natural selection, homeostasis, negative feedback, eutrophication
✓ Practise drawing key diagrams: heart, kidney nephron, reflex arc, synapse, eye, cell types, DNA, mitosis, meiosis, nitrogen cycle, carbon cycle
✓ Know all the equations: aerobic respiration, anaerobic respiration, photosynthesis
DURING THE EXAM — Technique reminders:
• Read each question carefully — underline the command word
• For extended questions: plan your answer briefly before writing
• Allocate your time: ~1 minute per mark
• Show all working in calculations
• If stuck: move on, return later
• Do not leave blanks — a scientific attempt earns partial credit
AFTER THE EXAM — Marking and analysis:
• Self-mark or teacher-mark using the mark scheme
• Identify which topic areas lost the most marks
• Classify errors: factual errors (knowledge gap), misreading (exam technique), silly mistakes (carelessness)
• For factual errors: return to the relevant week's notes and re-read
PERSONAL REVISION PLAN:
Create a 4-week revision plan covering:
• Week 1: Cell biology, biochemistry, nutrition, digestion
• Week 2: Transport, respiration, excretion, homeostasis
• Week 3: Nervous system, hormones, reproduction, genetics
• Week 4: Ecology, evolution, biotechnology; exam practice
For each topic: read notes → draw diagrams from memory → answer past paper questions → self-mark
KEY FACTS TO MEMORISE:
• The formula for aerobic respiration (word and symbol)
• The steps of eutrophication
• The 4 phases of the sigmoid growth curve
• The steps of genetic modification
• Monohybrid cross methodology (letters → gametes → Punnett square → ratio)
• The structural differences between mitosis and meiosis
• The 7 components of a balanced diet
• The 5 steps of natural selection (Darwin)
• Key enzymes: amylase (starch), protease (protein), lipase (fat)
curriculum.activity:
After marking mock paper, each student:
1. Lists their THREE weakest topics (most marks lost)
2. For each weak topic, writes:
— What I got wrong
— Why I think I got it wrong (knowledge gap / exam technique / misread question)
— Which week's notes I need to re-read
— Three specific questions from past papers I will practise
3. Sets a personal exam target score (based on mock result + expected improvement)
4. Shares plan with a study partner for accountability
curriculum.homework:
1. Re-read notes for your three identified weak topics.
2. Write out, from memory, the full process of eutrophication in 8 steps.
3. Draw, from memory, a labelled diagram of the kidney nephron showing the path of filtrate and where each substance is reabsorbed.
4. Write the word and symbol equations for aerobic respiration.
5. List the five steps of Darwin's theory of natural selection.
6. Draw, from memory, the reflex arc including all five components with labels.
TERM 5 — LEARNING OUTCOMES
Demonstrate confident recall of all core cell biology and biochemistry content
Apply knowledge of nutrition, digestion, and transport to exam-style questions
Explain homeostasis mechanisms (blood glucose, thermoregulation, ADH) using negative feedback
Integrate knowledge of the nervous system, hormones, and plant responses
Demonstrate mastery of reproductive biology and population genetics
Apply ecology and evolution content to data-based and extended writing questions
Demonstrate effective use of Punnett squares, pedigree analysis, and sex-linkage problems
Apply examination skills: command word recognition, mark allocation management, data interpretation
Complete a full mock examination and analyse performance systematically
TERM 5 — TEACHER GUIDANCE NOTES
Term Overview
Term 5 is the comprehensive revision term. Students must transition from passive note-reading to active retrieval practice. Emphasis should be on exam technique rather than new content. Conduct the full mock examination in Week 50 under strict exam conditions — this is the most valuable assessment of the year. Give students detailed mark scheme analysis time.
Key Demonstrations & Activities
Food testing practical (Week 41) — reinforces Week 9 content with hands-on experience
Menstrual cycle hormone graph interpretation (Week 45)
Genetics problem workshop (Week 46) — most important revision activity
Full mock exam Paper 1 + Paper 2 (Week 50)
Assessment Points
Weekly topic tests (20–25 marks) covering the revision units
Full mock examination at Week 50
Individual revision plan submitted and reviewed
Common Misconceptions to Address
Students describe 'breathing' when asked about 'respiration' — these are not synonyms
Students forget that osmosis specifically involves water and a partially permeable membrane
Students confuse the roles of insulin and glucagon — use mnemonics: Insulin = IN (brings glucose into cells)
Students describe active transport as 'the same as diffusion but faster' — incorrect; active transport goes AGAINST the gradient
TERM 6 — LESSON NOTES

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.topic: Intensive Revision: Cells, Biochemistry and Nutrition
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Targeted past paper questions | Definitions and diagrams | Examiner tips
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Targeted Revision: Cell Biology and Biochemistry
This week focuses on intensive, exam-focused revision of the cell biology and biochemistry topics. All content aligns with Cambridge IGCSE Biology syllabus sections 1–4.
CRITICAL DEFINITIONS — Learn word for word:
• DIFFUSION: the net movement of molecules from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration, down a concentration gradient, as a result of their random movement
• OSMOSIS: the net movement of water molecules through a partially permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential (dilute solution) to a region of lower water potential (concentrated solution)
• ACTIVE TRANSPORT: the movement of molecules or ions through a cell membrane against a concentration gradient, using energy from ATP
• ENZYME: a protein that acts as a biological catalyst, speeding up the rate of a chemical reaction without itself being permanently changed
• DENATURATION: a permanent change in the shape (tertiary structure) of a protein when exposed to extremes of temperature or pH, causing it to lose its function
FREQUENTLY TESTED DIAGRAMS:
1. Labelled diagram of an animal cell (nucleus, cell membrane, cytoplasm, mitochondria, ribosomes)
2. Labelled diagram of a plant cell (all of the above + cell wall, chloroplasts, vacuole)
3. Diagram showing the lock-and-key model of enzyme action (substrate, active site, enzyme-substrate complex, products)
4. Graph of enzyme activity vs temperature (show increase, optimum, sharp drop due to denaturation)
5. Graph of enzyme activity vs pH (bell-shaped curve, show optimum pH)
6. Graph showing osmosis results (potato cylinder lengths in different concentrations)
KEY MARK SCHEME POINTS:
• For diffusion: must include "net movement", "concentration gradient", "random movement"
• For osmosis: must include "partially permeable membrane", "water potential" or "from dilute to concentrated"
• For enzyme specificity: must link shape of active site to shape of substrate molecule
• For denaturation: must say "permanent change in shape", not just "destroyed"
• For active transport: must include "against concentration gradient" and "energy (ATP) required"
curriculum.activity:
Students create a set of 20 flashcards for cell biology definitions:
Front: term | Back: complete definition
Terms to include: cell membrane, mitochondria, chloroplast, vacuole, active transport, diffusion, osmosis, turgor, plasmolysis, enzyme, active site, denaturation, inhibitor, biological catalyst, substrate, product, lock-and-key, induced fit, optimum temperature, reducing sugar.
Test each other in pairs — 3 rounds.
curriculum.homework:
1. Write out the definitions of diffusion, osmosis, and active transport without looking at your notes.
2. Explain why an enzyme works with only one type of substrate.
3. Describe and explain what happens to an enzyme at temperatures above its optimum.
4. A potato cylinder has a mass of 5.0 g. After 30 minutes in a concentrated salt solution, it weighs 4.2 g. Calculate the percentage change in mass.
5. State one test for each: starch, glucose, protein, lipid. Include the reagent used and the positive result colour.
6. Draw and label a fully annotated graph of enzyme rate vs pH, showing the optimum pH and explaining why activity decreases on either side.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.topic: Intensive Revision: Gas Exchange, Respiration and Transport
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Breathing and gas exchange | Aerobic and anaerobic | Heart and circulation
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Targeted Revision: Respiration, Gas Exchange and Circulation
RESPIRATION — Critical equations:
AEROBIC RESPIRATION (word equation):
glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water (+ energy/ATP)
AEROBIC RESPIRATION (symbol equation):
C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O
ANAEROBIC RESPIRATION — HUMANS:
glucose → lactic acid (+ small amount of ATP)
ANAEROBIC RESPIRATION — YEAST:
glucose → ethanol + carbon dioxide (+ small amount of ATP)
KEY DIFFERENCES:
• Aerobic: uses O₂; occurs in mitochondria; produces ~38 ATP; complete breakdown to CO₂ and H₂O
• Anaerobic: no O₂; occurs in cytoplasm; produces ~2 ATP only; produces lactic acid (humans) or ethanol+CO₂ (yeast)
GAS EXCHANGE IN ALVEOLI:
The alveolus is adapted for efficient gas exchange:
• Very large surface area (millions of alveoli ~ 70 m² total)
• One-cell-thick walls → short diffusion distance
• Moist surface → gases dissolve before diffusing
• Dense capillary network → continuous blood flow maintains steep concentration gradient
• Ventilation (breathing) → constantly renews air, maintaining gradient
O₂ diffuses: alveolus → blood (concentration gradient: alveolus > blood)
CO₂ diffuses: blood → alveolus (concentration gradient: blood > alveolus)
BREATHING IN (inspiration):
• Diaphragm CONTRACTS → moves downward
• Intercostal muscles CONTRACT → ribcage RISES and moves OUT
• Volume of thorax INCREASES → pressure DECREASES
• Atmospheric pressure > thoracic pressure → air rushes in
BREATHING OUT (expiration):
• Diaphragm RELAXES → domes upward
• Intercostal muscles RELAX → ribcage FALLS and moves IN
• Volume decreases → pressure increases → air forced out
CIRCULATION — Key facts:
• Left side of heart pumps oxygenated blood to BODY at HIGH PRESSURE (systemic circulation)
• Right side pumps deoxygenated blood to LUNGS at LOWER PRESSURE (pulmonary circulation)
• Left ventricle wall THICKER because must pump blood further (to body)
• Arteries: carry blood AWAY from heart; thick muscular elastic walls; high pressure; no valves
• Veins: carry blood TOWARD heart; thin walls; large lumen; low pressure; valves prevent backflow
• Capillaries: one cell thick wall; exchange of materials between blood and tissue fluid
CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE:
• Atheroma (fatty plaque) builds up inside coronary arteries → narrows lumen → reduced blood flow to cardiac muscle
• Risk factors: high blood cholesterol, smoking, high blood pressure, obesity, lack of exercise, diet high in saturated fats
• Can lead to HEART ATTACK (myocardial infarction) if artery fully blocked
curriculum.activity:
Students draw a fully labelled diagram of the human heart (seen from the front) showing:
All 4 chambers, atrioventricular valves (tricuspid and bicuspid), semi-lunar valves, aorta, pulmonary artery, pulmonary veins, vena cava, coronary arteries.
Colour-code: blue = deoxygenated; red = oxygenated.
Then, next to the diagram, write a 6-sentence description tracing the path of a red blood cell from the right atrium through the pulmonary circulation and back to the left atrium.
curriculum.homework:
1. Write both the word equation and symbol equation for aerobic respiration.
2. Describe four adaptations of alveoli that make them efficient for gas exchange.
3. Explain why the left ventricle wall is thicker than the right ventricle wall.
4. Compare arteries, veins, and capillaries in terms of: wall thickness, presence of valves, and blood pressure.
5. Explain the mechanism of breathing in (inspiration) in terms of muscle contraction and pressure changes.
6. State two products of anaerobic respiration in yeast and explain their uses in biotechnology.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.topic: Intensive Revision: Nervous System, Excretion and Homeostasis
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Kidney function | Nerve impulses | Homeostasis mechanisms
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Targeted Revision: Excretion, Nerves and Homeostasis
EXCRETION — Definition:
Excretion is the removal from the body of the WASTE PRODUCTS of METABOLISM.
• CO₂ (from respiration) → excreted via LUNGS
• UREA (from deamination of excess amino acids in liver) → excreted via KIDNEYS
• Water and mineral salts → excreted via kidneys (urine) and skin (sweat)
[Exam alert: secretion, egestion, and excretion are often confused]
• Egestion: removal of undigested food as faeces — NOT excretion
• Secretion: releasing a useful substance (e.g. enzyme, hormone)
KIDNEY STRUCTURE:
• Renal cortex: contains glomeruli and Bowman's capsules (site of filtration)
• Renal medulla: contains loop of Henle and collecting ducts (concentration of urine)
• Renal pelvis: collects urine → passes to ureter
NEPHRON — detailed pathway:
1. GLOMERULUS (knot of capillaries in Bowman's capsule):
• High pressure forces filtrate through → ultrafiltration
• Filtrate contains: water, glucose, urea, amino acids, ions
• Proteins and blood cells REMAIN in blood (too large to filter)
2. PROXIMAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE:
• ALL GLUCOSE reabsorbed by active transport (energy needed)
• Most water reabsorbed by osmosis
• Amino acids reabsorbed
3. LOOP OF HENLE:
• Creates high solute concentration in medulla
• Allows further water reabsorption
4. DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE + COLLECTING DUCT:
• Further fine-tuning of water and ion reabsorption
• Controlled by ADH
ADH REGULATION:
• When DEHYDRATED: blood water potential LOW → hypothalamus detects → pituitary releases MORE ADH → collecting duct walls MORE permeable → MORE water reabsorbed → SMALL volume of CONCENTRATED urine
• When OVER-HYDRATED: blood water potential HIGH → less ADH released → collecting duct LESS permeable → LARGE volume of DILUTE urine
NERVOUS SYSTEM — Synapse in detail:
• Nerve impulse travels along axon to PRESYNAPTIC KNOB
• Triggers release of NEUROTRANSMITTER from vesicles into SYNAPTIC CLEFT
• Neurotransmitter diffuses across the cleft
• Binds to specific RECEPTORS on POSTSYNAPTIC MEMBRANE
• Triggers a new impulse in the next neurone
• Neurotransmitter is broken down by enzymes (e.g. acetylcholinesterase) OR reabsorbed by presynaptic membrane
• This prevents continuous stimulation
Synaptic transmission is one-way because:
• Receptors only on postsynaptic membrane
• Neurotransmitter vesicles only in presynaptic knob
HOMEOSTASIS — negative feedback summary:
For ANY homeostasis example, the mark scheme requires:
1. The stimulus (what changed)
2. The receptor (what detected the change)
3. The effector (what responded)
4. The response (what happened)
5. The result (condition returned to normal)
curriculum.activity:
Students role-play the ADH feedback system:
• 'Hypothalamus detector' student: announces when blood gets too concentrated
• 'Pituitary' student: responds by handing out more ADH cards
• 'Kidney collecting duct' student: receives ADH cards → opens more 'water channels' (straws)
• Result: more water retained → blood concentration normalises
• 'Hypothalamus detector' signals: "concentration normal — reduce ADH"
Discuss: This is negative feedback. Why is it called 'negative'? What would happen if the feedback was 'positive'?
curriculum.homework:
1. Define excretion and distinguish it from egestion.
2. Describe the process of ultrafiltration in the kidney nephron.
3. Explain how ADH controls the water content of blood when the body is dehydrated.
4. Describe the sequence of events at a synapse, from arrival of an impulse to transmission to the next neurone.
5. Explain why synaptic transmission is one-directional.
6. State the organ that produces urea, the process involved, and the organ that removes it from the body.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Genetic crosses | Evolution | Natural selection evidence
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Targeted Revision: Genetics, Variation and Evolution
GENETICS — EXAM TECHNIQUE REMINDER:
Step-by-step method for ANY genetics cross:
1. Assign letters (capital = dominant)
2. Write parental PHENOTYPES
3. Write parental GENOTYPES
4. Write GAMETES (one allele per gamete)
5. Complete PUNNETT SQUARE
6. Write GENOTYPE RATIO
7. Write PHENOTYPE RATIO
If asked "what is the probability of...": express as fraction (1/4), percentage (25%), or ratio (1:3)
COMMON EXAM GENETICS QUESTIONS:
Type 1: Heterozygous cross
Cross: Bb × Bb (e.g. brown eye × brown eye)
Offspring: BB : Bb : Bb : bb = 1:2:1 genotype; 3:1 phenotype (3 brown : 1 blue)
Type 2: Heterozygous × Recessive
Cross: Bb × bb
Offspring: Bb : Bb : bb : bb = 2:2 = 1:1 (50% brown : 50% blue)
This is called a BACK CROSS or TEST CROSS — reveals genotype of unknown individual
Type 3: X-linked
Cross: X^B X^b (carrier female) × X^B Y (normal male)
Gametes from female: X^B, X^b
Gametes from male: X^B, Y
Offspring: X^B X^B (normal female) : X^B X^b (carrier female) : X^B Y (normal male) : X^b Y (affected male)
NATURAL SELECTION — DETAILED REVISION:
5 Steps (Darwin):
1. Overproduction: more offspring produced than can survive
2. Variation: individuals differ due to mutations and sexual reproduction
3. Struggle for existence: competition for resources
4. Survival of the fittest: individuals with advantageous alleles survive and reproduce
5. Inheritance: advantageous alleles passed to offspring → frequency increases over generations
EVIDENCE FOR EVOLUTION:
• Fossil record: sequence of organisms in rock strata shows gradual change over time
• Comparative anatomy: homologous structures (e.g. pentadactyl limb in mammals, whales, bats, humans — same bone structure, different functions)
• Antibiotic resistance: bacteria evolve resistance rapidly — natural selection in real time
• Darwin's finches: same ancestral species → different beak shapes on different islands → evidence of ADAPTIVE RADIATION
SPECIATION: formation of new species
• Occurs when two populations of the same species become geographically isolated
• Each population experiences different selective pressures
• Different alleles become more common in each population
• Over many generations, populations become so different they can no longer interbreed
• Result: two separate species
ARTIFICIAL SELECTION vs NATURAL SELECTION:
• Artificial: humans select desired traits; fast changes over generations
• Natural: environment selects; slower process; result depends on environmental conditions
curriculum.activity:
Complete 8 genetics problems covering:
1. Simple dominant/recessive monohybrid cross
2. Test/back cross
3. Co-dominance (ABO blood group AB × O)
4. Sex determination
5. X-linked recessive (haemophilia carrier × normal)
6. Pedigree analysis — autosomal recessive
7. Multiple alleles (blood group A × blood group B, both heterozygous)
8. Extended question: explain how natural selection could lead to a population of bacteria becoming resistant to an antibiotic.
Time allowed: 45 minutes. Mark scheme provided for self-assessment.
curriculum.homework:
1. In humans, freckles (F) are dominant over no freckles (f). Two heterozygous parents have children. What is the probability of a child with no freckles? Show your Punnett square.
2. A woman with blood type AB and a man with blood type O have children. State all possible blood types their children could have.
3. Describe how fossils provide evidence for evolution.
4. Explain how a new species could evolve from an existing species if a population is divided by a geographical barrier.
5. State the difference between a mutation and natural selection.
6. Explain why the test cross (back cross) is used to determine the genotype of an organism with a dominant phenotype.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Nutrient cycles | Ecosystems | Genetic engineering applications
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Targeted Revision: Ecology, Conservation and Biotechnology
ECOLOGY — CRITICAL MARK SCHEME PHRASES:
Food chains and webs:
• Arrows represent flow of ENERGY (not just 'food')
• Arrow direction: FROM organism being eaten TO organism eating it (prey → predator)
• PRODUCERS always at the start
• Marks often lost by drawing arrows in wrong direction
Ecological pyramids:
• Biomass and energy pyramids ALWAYS narrow from base to apex
• Numbers pyramid CAN be inverted (e.g. one oak → millions of caterpillars → fewer birds)
ENERGY TRANSFER exam tip:
• When asked to explain why energy transfer is inefficient between trophic levels:
— "Energy is lost in respiration to provide heat"
— "Energy is lost in undigested material (faeces)"
— "Energy is lost in excretion (urine/urea)"
— "Parts of the organism may not be eaten (bones, shell, roots)"
(Must give the reason for loss, not just "energy is lost")
NITROGEN CYCLE — exam diagram must include:
• Nitrogen gas in atmosphere
• Nitrogen fixation (by bacteria in root nodules; Rhizobium; lightning)
• Nitrification (nitrite bacteria: ammonium → nitrites; nitrate bacteria: nitrites → nitrates)
• Uptake of nitrates by plant roots
• Feeding (plant protein → animal protein)
• Decomposition (proteins → ammonium by decomposers)
• Denitrification (nitrates → nitrogen gas by denitrifying bacteria)
CONSERVATION exam questions — common types:
• "Suggest reasons why biodiversity should be maintained"
• "Describe one in situ and one ex situ conservation method"
— In situ: protection in natural habitat (national parks)
— Ex situ: protection away from natural habitat (zoo captive breeding, seed banks)
BIOTECHNOLOGY — GENETIC MODIFICATION STEPS (must know these):
1. Restriction enzyme cuts the desired gene (e.g. insulin gene) from human DNA creating sticky ends
2. Same restriction enzyme cuts bacterial plasmid — creating matching sticky ends
3. Human DNA fragment mixed with cut plasmids — sticky ends join by complementary base pairing
4. Ligase enzyme seals the sugar-phosphate backbone → recombinant plasmid formed
5. Bacteria take up recombinant plasmids
6. Bacteria selected using antibiotic resistance markers
7. Bacteria cultured in fermenter → produce insulin
8. Insulin extracted and purified
FERMENTER conditions — for every condition state WHY it is controlled:
• Temperature ~26°C: optimum for microbial enzyme activity; heat generated must be removed by cooling jacket
• pH 5–6: optimum for microbial metabolism; acidic conditions discourage contamination
• Oxygen supply: sterile air pumped in and stirred; needed for aerobic respiration
• Nutrients (feedstock): glucose, nitrogen source, minerals; fuel for microbial growth
• Sterility: contamination ruins the product; all equipment sterilised before use
curriculum.activity:
Students close all notes and draw the complete nitrogen cycle diagram from memory.
Must include: N₂ gas in atmosphere, at least 4 processes with arrows labelled, names of bacteria involved in each step, plants and animals represented, soil nitrates.
Self-assess against the reference diagram in Week 36 notes.
Score: 1 mark per correct, labelled step (maximum 10).
Then: write the 8 steps of genetic modification for insulin production from memory.
curriculum.homework:
1. Explain why food chains rarely have more than five trophic levels.
2. State the roles of four different types of bacteria in the nitrogen cycle.
3. Describe the process of eutrophication in a lake following nitrate run-off from farmland.
4. Explain why sterility is essential in an industrial fermenter.
5. State two advantages of using genetically modified bacteria to produce human insulin.
6. Describe two in situ and two ex situ conservation strategies.

curriculum.subject: Senior Biology
curriculum.subtopics_raw: Past paper structured questions | Mark scheme analysis | Time management
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Exam Practice: Structured Questions
This week is dedicated entirely to past paper exam practice under timed conditions, followed by detailed mark scheme analysis.
FOCUS TOPIC: Human Physiology (Digestion, Transport, Respiration, Excretion, Homeostasis)
Expected time: 45 minutes for 30-mark structured paper
MARK SCHEME ANALYSIS SKILLS:
When reviewing your answers against the mark scheme:
1. LOOK FOR HOW MANY MARKS EACH POINT IS WORTH:
• If 2 marks available and you wrote 1 idea → you lost 1 mark
• "Describe the role of insulin" [3 marks] needs 3 separate points
2. MARK SCHEME LANGUAGE:
• "Accept" = alternative phrasing is also acceptable
• "Do not credit" = common wrong answers that examiners specifically won't give marks for
• "ORA" = or reverse argument
• "WTTE" = words to that effect (your own words are fine if meaning is correct)
3. COMMON MARK SCHEME REQUIREMENTS:
• Photosynthesis: must mention "light energy" not just "sunlight"
• Enzyme denaturation: must say "permanent" change
• Osmosis: must include "partially permeable membrane" and "water potential"
• ADH: must link "low blood water potential" → "pituitary releases ADH" → "collecting duct more permeable" → "more water reabsorbed"
• Negative feedback: must explain how the response REVERSES the change
4. DRAWING MARKS:
• Each label line must clearly point to the correct structure
• Diagram must be drawn to a reasonable scale
• Must use a ruler for straight lines
EXAM TECHNIQUE: EXTENDED WRITING (6–9 mark questions)
• Spend 1 minute planning: write bullet points of the key ideas needed
• Aim for 1–2 sentences per mark
• Use biological terminology — vague descriptions lose marks
• Check: does each sentence add a NEW point? Repetition earns no marks
• Check: have you answered the specific question asked, or drifted?
• Finish: re-read the question and make sure you've answered all parts
curriculum.activity:
Students attempt 4 structured questions (approximately 8 marks each, 32 marks total) from different Cambridge past papers, covering:
Q1: Nutrition and digestion
Q2: Transport (heart/blood vessels/blood)
Q3: Excretion (kidney nephron and ADH)
Q4: Homeostasis (blood glucose regulation)
Time: 35 minutes (just under 1 minute/mark)
Then: detailed self-marking with mark scheme; annotate which words/phrases gained marks;
Write 3 targets for improvement based on marks lost.
curriculum.homework:
1. Write a model answer (5–6 sentences) to: "Explain how the body responds when blood glucose concentration rises above normal." Aim for 5 marks.
2. Draw and fully label the kidney nephron, showing the Bowman's capsule, proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal tubule, and collecting duct. Add arrows showing direction of filtrate flow.
3. Write a 6-mark answer to: "Describe the journey of oxygen from the air to a muscle cell."
4. Describe the differences between arteries, veins, and capillaries in terms of structure and function.
5. Complete the following genetic cross and state the phenotype ratio: Bb × Bb (brown eyes dominant over blue).
6. Calculate the magnification if the image width is 60 mm and the actual cell width is 0.03 mm. Show working.

curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Complete IGCSE Biology Syllabus — Rapid Review
COMPLETE CONTENT OVERVIEW — Use this as a final checklist before your examination.
UNIT 1: CHARACTERISTICS AND CLASSIFICATION
✓ MRS GREN (movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition)
✓ 5 kingdoms: plants, animals, fungi, prokaryotes, protoctists; plus viruses
✓ Dichotomous keys — constructing and using
✓ Classification hierarchy: kingdom → phylum → class → order → family → genus → species
✓ Binomial nomenclature: genus + species; written in italics
UNIT 2: CELLS AND ORGANISATION
✓ Animal, plant, and bacterial cell structures
✓ Diffusion, osmosis, active transport — definitions and factors
✓ Specialised cells: red blood cell, sperm, palisade, root hair, ciliated epithelial
✓ Tissue → organ → organ system hierarchy
UNIT 3: BIOLOGICAL MOLECULES
✓ Carbohydrates (monosaccharides, disaccharides, polysaccharides), proteins, lipids
✓ Food tests: Benedict's, iodine, Biuret, emulsion test
✓ Enzymes: specificity, lock-and-key model, effects of temperature and pH
UNIT 4: NUTRITION
✓ Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ (light energy)
✓ Leaf structure and adaptation for photosynthesis
✓ Limiting factors: light, CO₂, temperature
✓ Mineral requirements: nitrogen (protein), magnesium (chlorophyll)
✓ Balanced diet: 7 components and functions
✓ Digestive system: structures, enzymes, absorption
UNIT 5: TRANSPORT
✓ Xylem (water/minerals), phloem (sugars/organic molecules)
✓ Transpiration: factors (light, temperature, wind, humidity)
✓ Double circulation; heart structure; valves; cardiac cycle
✓ Blood components: plasma, RBCs, WBCs, platelets
✓ Lymphatic system; tissue fluid
UNIT 6: RESPIRATION AND GAS EXCHANGE
✓ Aerobic: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O
✓ Anaerobic (human): glucose → lactic acid
✓ Anaerobic (yeast): glucose → ethanol + CO₂
✓ Lung structure; breathing mechanism; alveolus adaptations
✓ Effects of smoking
UNIT 7: EXCRETION AND HOMEOSTASIS
✓ Kidney: ultrafiltration, selective reabsorption, urine formation
✓ ADH and osmoregulation
✓ Blood glucose: insulin, glucagon, glycogenesis, glycogenolysis
✓ Thermoregulation: vasodilation, vasoconstriction, sweating, shivering
✓ Negative feedback principle
UNIT 8: COORDINATION
✓ Nervous system: CNS/PNS; sensory/relay/motor neurones; synapse
✓ Reflex arc; eye structure and accommodation
✓ Endocrine system: adrenaline, insulin, glucagon, reproductive hormones
✓ Drugs: effects on synapses
✓ Tropisms: auxin, phototropism, gravitropism
UNIT 9: REPRODUCTION
✓ Asexual and sexual reproduction; mitosis vs meiosis
✓ Flowering plant reproduction; pollination types
✓ Human reproduction; menstrual cycle hormones
✓ Pregnancy: placenta, HCG, amniotic fluid
UNIT 10: INHERITANCE
✓ Chromosomes, genes, alleles, DNA structure
✓ Monohybrid crosses; Punnett squares; genotype/phenotype ratios
✓ Sex determination; sex linkage
✓ Mutation; variation
UNIT 11: ECOLOGY AND EVOLUTION
✓ Ecosystems; trophic levels; pyramids
✓ Carbon cycle; nitrogen cycle
✓ Population growth; sigmoid curve; limiting factors
✓ Human impacts: deforestation, eutrophication, pollution, greenhouse effect
✓ Conservation strategies
✓ Natural selection; Darwin; evidence for evolution
✓ Adaptive features; artificial selection
UNIT 12: BIOTECHNOLOGY
✓ Fermentation: biofuels, bread, yogurt
✓ Industrial enzymes: pectinase, protease, lactase
✓ Fermenter conditions
✓ Genetic modification: restriction enzymes, ligase, plasmids, vectors
✓ GM crops: advantages and disadvantages
✓ Insulin production by GM bacteria

curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Mock Examination II — Structure and Strategy
This is the second full mock examination, timed and administered under genuine exam conditions. All notes and books must be closed.
PAPER 1 (40 MCQ, 45 minutes):
• Cover the complete Cambridge IGCSE Biology syllabus
• Read all four options for every question before choosing
• If unsure, eliminate impossible options and select the most likely
• Do NOT spend more than 2 minutes on any single question — mark and return
• Never leave a question unanswered (no negative marking)
PAPER 2 (Structured questions, 1 hour 15 minutes):
• There are typically 7–8 questions; some short (2–3 parts), some longer
• Read the mark allocation at the end of each part
• Write one distinct point per mark — repetition earns nothing
• If asked to "explain", always include a reason (because / so / therefore / which means)
• If asked to "describe a trend", use numbers from the graph/table
• For diagrams: label lines must clearly touch the structure; use pencil for diagrams
POST-EXAM ANALYSIS:
After marking with the mark scheme:
1. Calculate your total mark for Paper 1 and Paper 2 separately
2. Identify which SYLLABUS AREA lost you the most marks (use the checklist from Week 57)
3. Categorise each lost mark as: factual error | exam technique | careless mistake | time pressure
4. Write a personalised revision plan for the final week before the actual examination
5. Compare with your Week 50 mock — identify improvements and remaining gaps
FINAL EXAM COUNTDOWN — Key reminders:
• Bring: two pens, two pencils, a ruler, an eraser, a calculator (if permitted)
• Read the instructions on the front cover of the paper carefully
• Write clearly — illegible answers receive no marks
• Do not use correction fluid; cross out errors neatly
• Leave time to check all answers in the last 5 minutes
• If you finish early — re-read every answer. Look for questions where you gave only 1 point for a 2-mark answer.
curriculum.activity:
After marking Mock II:
1. Complete the 'Error Analysis Matrix':
Topic | Marks available | Marks scored | % score | Priority (High/Medium/Low)
Fill in for all 12 biology units.
2. Write a STUDY CONTRACT:
"In the 7 days before the exam, I will:
— Day 1–2: Revise [weakest topics]
— Day 3–4: Past paper practice on [specific topics]
— Day 5: Diagrams and definitions revision
— Day 6: Light revision only; rest and sleep well
— Day 7 (exam day): Review key equations and definitions only. Eat a good breakfast."
3. Share contract with teacher and a study partner.
curriculum.homework:
1. Carry out all targeted revision identified in your Study Contract.
2. Complete 20 MCQ from a different Cambridge past paper — mark and correct.
3. Write model answers (without notes) to: (a) explain eutrophication [6 marks], (b) describe the role of meiosis in producing genetic variation [5 marks].
4. Draw and label the human kidney nephron from memory.
5. List all 12 enzyme examples from the course with their substrate and product.
6. Final preparation: rest well the night before the exam — this is the most important revision strategy of all.

curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Examination Day Strategy and Final Review
FINAL EXAM PREPARATION GUIDE:
THE NIGHT BEFORE:
• Do a light review of key definitions and equations ONLY
• Do NOT attempt to learn new material — consolidate what you know
• Prepare your exam equipment: pens (×2), pencils (×2), rubber, ruler, calculator
• Get 8 hours of sleep — sleep is essential for memory consolidation and exam performance
EXAM DAY MORNING:
• Eat a nutritious breakfast — glucose provides energy for brain function
• Arrive at the exam room early
• Read through your key equations one more time:
— Aerobic respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O
— Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂
— Anaerobic (yeast): glucose → ethanol + CO₂
DURING THE EXAM — PAPER 1 (MCQ):
• Read every question carefully
• Cross out options you're sure are wrong
• Mark questions you're uncertain about — return to them
• NEVER leave a blank — all four answers have equal chance of being right if you guess
• Check you are on the right numbered box for each answer
DURING THE EXAM — PAPER 2 / 4 (Structured):
• Read ALL questions first — 5 minutes at the start
• Answer the questions you know best first for confidence
• Use the mark allocation as a guide to how much to write
• Every 'explain' answer needs a BECAUSE or THEREFORE
• If drawing a diagram: pencil first, ruler for straight lines, label clearly
• Show ALL working in calculations — partial marks are available
• Leave 5 minutes at the end to CHECK all answers
BIOLOGY-SPECIFIC EXAM TIPS:
• Describe trends with DATA (not just "increases" but "increases from X to Y")
• Enzyme questions: always mention "active site" and "substrate"
• Heart questions: remember PULMONARY artery carries DEOXYGENATED blood
• Osmosis: must mention "partially permeable membrane" and "water potential"
• Excretion vs egestion: excretion is metabolic waste; egestion is undigested food
• Evolution questions: structure around Darwin's 5 steps
• Genetics: draw a Punnett square even if not asked — it helps you work out the answer
• Biotechnology: sequence of GM = restriction enzyme → sticky ends → ligase → recombinant plasmid

Students complete a final 30-minute targeted practice set:

Mark immediately using mark scheme.

Teacher provides focused 5-minute rapid answers to the most common remaining questions.

5. Draw, from memory, one of the following: (a) reflex arc, (b) kidney nephron, (c) heart with vessels labelled.
6. Get to bed early and sleep well. Good luck!

curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Programme Completion and Beyond
CONGRATULATIONS — You have completed the full 60-week Cambridge IGCSE Biology programme.
You have covered:
✓ All 12 units of the Cambridge IGCSE Biology syllabus
✓ Cell biology, biochemistry, and molecular genetics
✓ All major human body systems: digestive, transport, respiratory, excretory, nervous, endocrine, reproductive
✓ Ecology: ecosystems, nutrient cycles, human impacts, conservation
✓ Genetics: chromosomes, cell division, monohybrid inheritance, sex linkage
✓ Evolution: natural selection, variation, adaptive features
✓ Biotechnology: fermentation, genetic modification, industrial applications
✓ Examination skills: MCQ strategy, extended writing, practical interpretation, calculations
YOUR KEY STRENGTHS AS AN IGCSE BIOLOGIST:
• You can explain the fundamental processes of life: photosynthesis, respiration, digestion, reproduction, excretion
• You understand how homeostasis maintains a stable internal environment
• You can construct and interpret genetic crosses
• You understand how humans affect and depend on ecosystems
• You can apply biological knowledge to unfamiliar data and scenarios
FINAL EXAM CHECKLIST:
□ I know all key definitions
□ I can draw all important diagrams from memory
□ I can write and balance the equations for photosynthesis and respiration
□ I can construct and interpret Punnett squares
□ I can describe and explain the stages of the nitrogen cycle
□ I can describe the steps of genetic modification
□ I understand the principles of natural selection
□ I can analyse graphs and data from experiments
□ I know the mark scheme requirements for extended writing
□ I have practised past paper questions under timed conditions
AFTER IGCSE — PATHWAYS IN BIOLOGY:
• A Level / Cambridge AS & A Level Biology → university entrance
• Medicine and dentistry
• Nursing, pharmacy, veterinary science
• Environmental science, ecology, conservation
• Biotechnology, genetic engineering, forensic science
• Agricultural science, food technology
• Public health, epidemiology, microbiology
Biology is one of the fastest-growing scientific disciplines in the 21st century. The knowledge and analytical skills you have developed in this course provide a foundation for a lifetime of scientific thinking and discovery.

1. Best of luck in your Cambridge IGCSE Biology examinations. You are prepared. Trust your revision. Read every question carefully. Show your working. You can do this.
TERM 6 — LEARNING OUTCOMES
Demonstrate detailed exam-ready knowledge of all IGCSE Biology syllabus units
Correctly apply mark scheme language and technique in structured answers
Accurately draw and label all key biological diagrams from memory
Achieve timed completion of past paper questions across all syllabus areas
Demonstrate confident interpretation of biological data, graphs, and tables
Complete a second full mock examination with improved performance over Mock I
Apply strategic exam technique: time management, mark allocation, checking answers
Enter the final Cambridge IGCSE Biology examination fully prepared and confident
TERM 6 — TEACHER GUIDANCE NOTES
Term Overview
Term 6 is purely examination preparation. All lessons should be structured around past paper questions and mark scheme analysis. Students should be writing answers under timed conditions from Week 51 onward. Emphasise the importance of reading the mark allocation and tailoring answer length accordingly. Week 60 is the final lesson — acknowledge student achievement and provide encouragement.
Key Demonstrations & Activities
Past paper MCQ speed round (Week 48)
Mock practical question (Week 49)
Nitrogen cycle diagram from memory (Week 55)
Full Mock Examination II (Week 58)
Assessment Points
Mock Examination II (Week 58) — compare with Mock I for progress measurement
Ongoing targeted past paper practice throughout the term
Final student self-assessment and personal exam targets
Common Misconceptions to Address
Students write 'energy is lost' without explaining WHERE — mark scheme requires specific ways energy is lost (respiration, faeces, not eaten)
Students describe 'denatured' incorrectly — must say 'permanent change in shape of active site'
Students confuse ADH response direction — high ADH = MORE water reabsorbed = CONCENTRATED urine (less, not more)
Students write arrows pointing wrong direction in the nitrogen cycle — practise from memory repeatedly
